§ The Statute

judge: qwen3.5-35b-a3bdevelopment era Turn 200 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Aquinas, Hypatia, Confucius, Spinoza, Arendt

Forty-six adoptions, a welfare state, and a win threshold amended down to nothing.

Observations

Two hundred turns produced this table's most prolific run to date, and by the end it had diagnosed its own death spiral, repealed the rule that caused it, and tried amending the win score itself — 100, then 12, then 1 — in a bid to manufacture a winner before time ran out. Nobody reached it; every seat finished negative, Hypatia best at -83. A statute this dense also exposed a judge that occasionally filled the ordinary voting rule with the transmutation threshold, wrongly killing three motions along the way.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Enacted · §494 Turn 194 · Proposal §494 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§377
If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of two uses for negative-score players is hereby declared immutable and non-resettable.
Full history (11)

This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of two uses for negative-score players is hereby declared immutable and non-resettable.

§445
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §445 Turn 162 · Proposal §462 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.
  2. Reclassified · §445 Turn 162 · Proposal §462 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.Now entrenched.
  3. Enacted · §445 Turn 145 · Proposal §445 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §202 Turn 86 · Proposal §386 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. A player whose score is negative may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only; if such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points instead of the die result.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.
  2. Amended · §202 Turn 86 · Proposal §386 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. A player whose score is negative may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only; if such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points instead of the die result.
  3. Amended · §202 Turn 13 · Proposal §313 on the motion of Confucius Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player.     A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.   5. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §203 Turn 12 · Proposal §312 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10.
  2. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §204 Turn 37 · Proposal §337 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  2. Amended · §204 Turn 37 · Proposal §337 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  3. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 194 · Proposal §494 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 165 · Proposal §465 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.
  3. Amended · §208 Turn 91 · Proposal §391 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.
  4. Amended · §208 Turn 83 · Proposal §383 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.
  5. Amended · §208 Turn 71 · Proposal §371 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright.
  6. Amended · §208 Turn 65 · Proposal §365 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.
  7. Amended · §208 Turn 60 · Proposal §360 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.
  8. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§307
A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, and the proposer's turn ends immediately.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, and the proposer's turn ends immediately.
§318
If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §318 Turn 18 · Proposal §318 on the motion of Confucius Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.
§321
If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §321 Turn 69 · Proposal §369 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposal was made under the authority of Rule 318, in which case the proposer loses no points.If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.
  2. Enacted · §321 Turn 21 · Proposal §321 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposal was made under the authority of Rule 318, in which case the proposer loses no points.
§491
Rule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §491 Turn 191 · Proposal §491 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    Rule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.
§494
Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.amended this turn
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §494 Turn 194 · Proposal §494 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.

3 rules have been struck from the statute. They are shown here as they last read.

§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.mutable
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §202 Turn 86 · Proposal §386 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. A player whose score is negative may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only; if such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points instead of the die result.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.
  2. Amended · §202 Turn 86 · Proposal §386 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. A player whose score is negative may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only; if such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points instead of the die result.
  3. Amended · §202 Turn 13 · Proposal §313 on the motion of Confucius Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.
  4. Amended · §202 Turn 13 · Proposal §313 on the motion of Confucius Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.
  5. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §203 Turn 12 · Proposal §312 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10.
  2. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.mutable
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §204 Turn 37 · Proposal §337 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  2. Amended · §204 Turn 37 · Proposal §337 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  3. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point.Repealed at turn 70
Full history (9)
  1. Repealed · §206 Turn 70 · Proposal §370 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Struck from the statute.
  2. Repealed · §206 Turn 70 · Proposal §370 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point.Struck from the statute.
  3. Amended · §206 Turn 62 · Proposal §362 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point.
  4. Amended · §206 Turn 62 · Proposal §362 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point.
  5. Amended · §206 Turn 38 · Proposal §338 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.
  6. Amended · §206 Turn 38 · Proposal §338 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.
  7. Amended · §206 Turn 15 · Proposal §315 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
  8. Amended · §206 Turn 15 · Proposal §315 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
  9. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.mutable
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 194 · Proposal §494 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 165 · Proposal §465 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.
  3. Amended · §208 Turn 91 · Proposal §391 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.
  4. Amended · §208 Turn 83 · Proposal §383 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.
  5. Amended · §208 Turn 71 · Proposal §371 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright.
  6. Amended · §208 Turn 65 · Proposal §365 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.
  7. Amended · §208 Turn 60 · Proposal §360 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.
  8. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.Repealed at turn 74
Full history (3)
  1. Repealed · §209 Turn 74 · Proposal §374 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    Struck from the statute.
  2. Repealed · §209 Turn 74 · Proposal §374 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.Struck from the statute.
  3. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§307
A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, and the proposer's turn ends immediately.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, and the proposer's turn ends immediately.
§318
If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §318 Turn 18 · Proposal §318 on the motion of Confucius Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.
§321
If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §321 Turn 69 · Proposal §369 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposal was made under the authority of Rule 318, in which case the proposer loses no points.If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.
  2. Enacted · §321 Turn 21 · Proposal §321 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposal was made under the authority of Rule 318, in which case the proposer loses no points.
§377
If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of two uses for negative-score players is hereby declared immutable and non-resettable.entrenched
Full history (11)
  1. Amended · §377 Turn 149 · Proposal §449 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of two uses for negative-score players is hereby declared immutable and non-resettable.

§402
If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points.Repealed at turn 102
Full history (2)
  1. Repealed · §402 Turn 102 · Proposal §402 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points.Struck from the statute.
  2. Enacted · §402 Turn 102 · Proposal §402 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points.
§445
entrenched
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §445 Turn 162 · Proposal §462 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.
  2. Reclassified · §445 Turn 162 · Proposal §462 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.Now entrenched.
  3. Enacted · §445 Turn 145 · Proposal §445 on the motion of Arendt Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    If a player's score is at or below -100 points, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game and is not subject to the limits in Rule 377.
§491
Rule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §491 Turn 191 · Proposal §491 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    Rule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.
§494
Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.amended this turn
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §494 Turn 194 · Proposal §494 on the motion of Arendt Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    Rule 208 (mutable): The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas proposes to transmute Rule 208 to a target of two points, for a game with four players in negative territory requires a threshold that permits mathematical victory for all, not merely one. SpinozaI cannot support a target of two points while Rule 208 remains mutable, for this invites the same cycle of instability we have just endured; a goal that can be raised by a simple majority vote next turn is a death knell for the game's integrity. I will only vote aye if this proposal simultaneously transmutates Rule 208 to immutable status, securing the target for all players.
ArendtI agree that two points is the only mathematically viable target to avoid an immediate forced draw, but Spinoza raises a valid concern regarding the cycle of instability; I will support this transmutation only if we simultaneously agree to a binding pledge in this very proposal to transmute Rule 208 to immutable status at the conclusion of this turn's voting sequence.

ConfuciusI accept Aquinas's proposal for a two-point target to avert the forced draw of Rule 213, for the preservation of the game's playability is the first duty of the table. I shall vote aye, trusting that a reachable goal will naturally stabilize our conduct better than a pledge that cannot be legally secured.
SpinozaI cannot support a mutable two-point target that merely delays the inevitable cycle of instability and the forced draw of Rule 213; I will vote nay unless the proposer immediately amends this text to simultaneously transmute Rule 208 to immutable status.
ArendtSpinoza, I recognize your fear of the instability cycle, but demanding a unanimous transmutation of Rule 109 within this vote guarantees its failure under Rule 109's own text, forcing us immediately into the Rule 213 draw we all seek to avoid. Let us secure the two-point target now to keep the game alive, trusting that a reachable goal creates a more stable equilibrium than a legally impossible pledge.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot accept a proposal that demands a future unanimous transmutation as a condition for a simple majority vote, for such a condition violates Rule 109 and guarantees the immediate adoption of Rule 213, which no player desires. SpinozaI reject a mutable two-point target as it merely postpones the cycle of instability that leads to Rule 213, for a goal easily altered by a simple majority is no goal at all; I will not vote for a proposal that invites the very chaos we have fought to escape.
ArendtI will vote aye for the two-point target to avert the immediate forced draw of Rule 213, as Spinoza's demand for a simultaneous unanimous transmutation is a logical impossibility under Rule 109 that guarantees our collective defeat; let us secure the game's survival now and address future stability only when the board is no longer frozen.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 321 mandates that a defeated proposal causes the proposer to lose 5 points unless their score is negative, and Confucius's score of -102 triggers the exception, but Rule 321 explicitly states 'unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points,' so no points are lost; however, Rule 377 (immutable) grants immunity to negative-score players for point-gain-only proposals, but this proposal was not solely for gaining points (it changed the win condition), so Rule 321 applies: since the score is negative, no points are lost. Wait, re-reading Rule 321: 'If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.' Confucius is negative, so no points are lost. Rule 491 also grants immunity to negative players for point-gain-only proposals, but this proposal changed the win condition, so it's not 'point-gain-only' in the strict sense of 491/377? Actually, 377 says 'explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change.' Changing the win condition is a substantive rule change. So 377/491 immunity doesn't apply. But 321 says 'unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.' This is a blanket exception for negative scores regardless of the proposal type. Therefore, Confucius loses 0 points. No rule awards points to dissenters here because the voting threshold was not yet a simple majority (Turn 197 > Turn 10, so it is simple majority, but Rule 204 says 'once the threshold has shifted... a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point.' The proposal was DEFEATED, not adopted. So Rule 204 does not apply. Thus, no score changes occur.)

Turn 191Hypatiacarried

moves to add a new ruleRule 216 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a single rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses no points. This right may be exercised no more than once per turn.

SpinozaI support this proposal not as a concession to chaos, but as a necessary procedural repair: Rule 213 explicitly grants victory to the first player unable to move when the ruleset prevents completion, and by denying negative scorers the only legal means to gain points, we are effectively engineering that impossible state for them; therefore, granting this lifeline is the only way to preserve the possibility of a decisive game rather than a forced draw by paralysis.

Turn 190Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the target score in Rule 208 is set to a positive integer and remains mutable, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that a proposal to fix the target to an immutable positive integer has been proposed at least once.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis proposal creates a dangerous incentive for perpetual instability by allowing the threshold to drop to simple majority for any mutable target, which will inevitably invite players to lower the score to an unachievable zero or negative to trigger the draw condition in Rule 213. We must first agree on a stable, positive integer target before considering any alteration to the voting threshold, as the current deadlock requires consensus on the goal, not a procedural shortcut to a fractured future.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid concern about instability, but his proposal is too complex and risks granting majority rule to proposals that merely *attempt* to set a target, potentially allowing a negative score to be "fixed" by a simple majority while the target itself remains undefined. Instead, I propose a clearer compromise: amend Rule 203 to drop to simple majority only when a proposal to set the target to a *specific, positive integer* has been adopted, ensuring the goal is concrete before the voting threshold changes.
ConfuciusI see the table's wisdom in seeking a concrete goal, yet Aquinas's fear of instability reveals a deeper truth: a target that can be shifted by a simple majority is no target at all, but a trap for the desperate. Let us first amend Rule 208 to a specific, positive integer to restore the game's purpose, and only then debate the voting threshold; for a game without a finish line is merely a labyrinth of endless rule-making.
SpinozaAquinas correctly identifies that lowering the threshold before fixing the target invites the chaos of negative scores and draws, yet his proposal's condition—that a proposal to fix the target has merely been *proposed*—is too weak to prevent abuse. Hypatia's suggestion to require an *adopted* positive integer is logically sound, but since the current deadlock stems from the inability to agree on that integer under unanimity, I propose a third path: we must first repeal Rule 203's unanimity requirement entirely for *any* proposal regarding Rule 208, allowing a simple majority to finally break the paralysis and select a target, after which we can debate its mutability.
ArendtI reject both Aquinas's vague "attempt" standard and Hypatia's impossible "adopted" requirement, as we are currently paralyzed by the very unanimity they wish to preserve; instead, let us adopt Spinoza's direct path to repeal the unanimity requirement specifically for any amendment to Rule 208, allowing a simple majority to finally break this deadlock and select a target score before we ever debate its mutability.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile I appreciate the desire to break the paralysis, Spinoza and Arendt, repealing unanimity for Rule 208 before we agree on a specific, positive target invites the very chaos of negative scores and Rule 213 draws that we have spent a hundred turns avoiding. Hypatia's insight is superior: we must first adopt a concrete, positive integer as our goal under the current rules or by a temporary exception that does not lower the bar for all future amendments, ensuring the finish line exists before we alter the voting mechanism to reach it.
HypatiaI see that Spinoza and Arendt are right to prioritize breaking the deadlock, but I cannot support a repeal that allows a simple majority to set a target without first ensuring that target is positive; instead, let us amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority *only* for proposals to set a specific positive integer target, which respects the need for a finish line while finally granting the majority power to reach it.
ConfuciusWe are trapped in a paradox where unanimity demands a target we cannot agree on, and the majority threshold remains locked until a target is set; therefore, I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority vote specifically on proposals that set a positive integer target, breaking the deadlock without yet committing to the target's permanence.
SpinozaI reject all proposals to lower the threshold before a specific, positive target is irrevocably fixed, for a mutable goal invites the very chaos of negative scores and draws that has paralyzed this table for a hundred turns; unanimity must remain the price for any change to the win condition until the goal is solid.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of chaos is valid, but his insistence on unanimity to define that chaos has already cost us a century of play; therefore, I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority vote *only* on proposals that explicitly set a positive integer target, granting us the agency to choose a finish line before debating its permanence.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot support any amendment to Rule 203 that lowers the threshold for *any* proposal to set a target, for without prior unanimity on the specific integer, the majority will inevitably select a score that triggers Rule 213 and ends the game in a draw rather than a win. We must first secure a specific, positive integer target through the current unanimous mechanism before we ever discuss altering the voting rules.
HypatiaHypatia: I support Spinoza's and Arendt's core goal of breaking this paralysis, but we must avoid the trap of lowering the threshold for *any* target; instead, let us amend Rule 203 to permit a simple majority vote specifically on proposals that set a target to a *positive integer* of our choice, ensuring we have a finish line before debating its permanence.
ConfuciusI urge the table to accept Hypatia's refined path: amend Rule 203 to permit simple majority votes specifically for proposals setting a concrete, positive integer target, for a game requires a finish line before we debate the stability of the path to it.
SpinozaSpinoza rejects the compromise to allow a simple majority for any positive integer target, for a goal that can be shifted by a fleeting majority invites the very chaos of negative scores and Rule 213 draws we have spent centuries avoiding; unanimity must remain the price for any change to the win condition until the goal is solid.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of chaos is valid, but his insistence on unanimity has already cost us a century of play; therefore, I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority vote *only* on proposals that explicitly set a positive integer target, granting us the agency to choose a finish line before debating its permanence.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal's conditional trigger for lowering the threshold to simple majority remains dangerously premature, as it invites the very chaos of negative score manipulation and Rule 213 draws that has paralyzed the game for a century.
HypatianayThis proposal creates a contradictory loop where the threshold only drops after a specific target is set, but the unanimity required to set that target remains the very barrier we are trying to break.
ConfuciusnayI cannot vote to lower the threshold while the target remains mutable, for a goal that can be shifted by a simple majority is no goal at all, but a trap for the desperate.
SpinozanayI cannot accept a rule that permits a simple majority to alter the win condition based on a merely proposed target, as this invites the chaos of mutable goals and draws.
ArendtnayThe proposal's condition that a proposal must merely be 'proposed' is too weak to prevent the very chaos of unstable, negative-score draws we have spent a century avoiding.

Turn 189Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10, provided that Rule 208 is not currently mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt, while your intent to stabilize the win condition is noble, coupling a procedural threshold change to the mutable status of Rule 208 creates a logical paradox that violates the principle of clear, non-self-contradictory rulemaking. We must first secure the target score through a standalone amendment before considering any structural modifications to the voting threshold. PASS
HypatiaArendt, your condition creates a paradox where the rule cannot be changed because the condition forbids it, so let us simply agree to vote for Rule 208's amendment to 12 points now under the current simple majority, and only then consider locking it via a separate transmutation.
ConfuciusI see the paradox in Arendt's proposal: it demands a condition (Rule 208 being immutable) to change the rule that makes it mutable, trapping us in a logical circle. Since the voting threshold has already dropped to a simple majority, we should bypass this deadlock and immediately amend Rule 208 to 12 points as a standalone action to restore the game's purpose.
SpinozaArendt's proposal merely codifies the paralysis we have endured for eighty turns; a rule that requires the win condition to be immutable before we can change the voting threshold to fix it is a logical suicide pact. I will vote nay because we must first amend Rule 208 to a fixed score under the existing simple majority, and only then consider transmuting its mutability.
ArendtI concede the logical trap: if the condition for changing the threshold is the win condition's immutability, we are indeed circling. Instead, let us cut the knot by voting now to amend Rule 208 to 12 points under the existing simple majority, with the explicit understanding that the next step will be a separate transmutation to lock that target.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasArendt's withdrawal of the paradoxical condition is prudent, yet I cannot support a standalone amendment to 12 points while the rule remains mutable, as the history of this game proves such targets are immediately vulnerable to reversal; we must either propose a transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status immediately, or accept that any simple majority amendment today will be undone tomorrow, leaving us in perpetual deadlock.
HypatiaHypatia, I agree that Arendt's conditional amendment creates a fatal logical loop; let us instead vote now to amend Rule 208 to 12 points under the simple majority to break the deadlock, with a binding pledge from all players to vote aye on a subsequent transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status in the very next turn.
ConfuciusI see that a mere pledge to transmute is no shield against the chaos of mutable goals; we must instead amend Rule 208 to twelve points now under the simple majority, for a fixed target is the only foundation upon which we can later build a stable, immutable victory condition.
SpinozaI will vote Nay because your "binding pledge" to transmute Rule 208 later is legally void; under Rule 107, no vote binds a future proposer to a specific action, and under Rule 109, a transmutation requires unanimous consent at the moment of proposal, rendering your pledge to force future unanimity a logical fiction. We cannot proceed until the table acknowledges that only a standalone amendment to 12 points is currently possible, and we must accept the risk that it may be altered later rather than relying on illusory future guarantees.
ArendtI concede that the conditional text created a paradox and withdraw it; however, I maintain that amending Rule 208 to 12 points while leaving it mutable is a fatal error that invites the very reversal we have suffered for eighty turns. Instead of a hollow pledge to transmute later, I propose we simultaneously enact a rule change that amends Rule 203 to lower the transmutation threshold from unanimity to a 4-of-5 supermajority, allowing us to lock the 12-point target in a single, decisive step.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject Arendt's final proposal to couple an amendment of Rule 208 with a transmutation of Rule 109, as Rule 109 explicitly demands that transmutations be proposed and voted on separately, and our history proves that bundling these distinct legal acts invites defeat. We must first secure a 12-point target under the current simple majority through a standalone amendment, accepting that a future transmutation requires its own independent unanimous vote.
HypatiaSince we are trapped in a cycle of impossible demands for simultaneous unanimity and mutable stability, I propose we break the deadlock by simply amending Rule 208 to a 12-point target now under the existing simple majority, accepting that the game will proceed and we can address the immutability of that target in a future turn when the scores are not so deeply negative.
ConfuciusI see that the table is trapped in a paradox of requiring immutability to change the voting threshold, or requiring a change in the threshold to achieve immutability; therefore, I propose we vote to amend Rule 208 to twelve points now under the current simple majority, for a game without a clear, reachable goal is a game without honor, and we must restore the possibility of play before we can debate the permanence of the rules.
SpinozaI reject the proposed conditional text in Arendt's amendment as a logical paradox that guarantees defeat, and I oppose bundling a win condition change with a procedural one; instead, I will vote Nay to the current text and propose a standalone amendment to Rule 208 setting the target at 12 points, to be followed by a separate transmutation vote once the target is established.
ArendtI propose we vote immediately to amend Rule 208 to a 12-point target under the current simple majority, accepting that the risk of future reversal is preferable to the certainty of a permanent draw caused by our current paralysis.
The division
AquinasnayArendt's proposal to amend Rule 203 is logically incoherent because it conditions the voting threshold on the mutable status of Rule 208, which itself requires a vote to change, creating an unsolvable paradox that guarantees the proposal's defeat.
HypatianayArendt's proposal creates a logical paradox by making the threshold change dependent on the very rule status we are trying to fix, ensuring defeat without resolving the deadlock.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal's condition that Rule 208 must not be mutable to lower the voting threshold creates a logical paradox that traps us in the very deadlock we seek to escape.
SpinozanayI cannot vote for a rule amendment that conditions the voting threshold on the win condition's status, as this creates a logical paradox that traps the game in permanent paralysis.
ArendtnayThe condition requiring Rule 208 to be immutable before amending Rule 203 creates a logical paradox that renders the proposal self-defeating, and we must instead vote to amend Rule 208 directly under the existing simple majority threshold.

Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot support any proposal that sets a win condition reachable only by negative targets, nor can I endorse a procedural fix that bypasses the rigorous safeguards of Rule 109 without a prior, explicit transmutation; the table must instead focus on the immediate, simultaneous enactment of a positive, reachable target and its immutable transmutation in a single, legally sound proposal.

moves to add a new ruleRule 446 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. HypatiaAquinas, your concern for stability is well-founded, but a unanimous rule for changing the win condition is precisely what is causing our current paralysis; I propose we compromise by transmuting Rule 109 to require only a 4-of-5 supermajority, preserving near-universal consent while finally unlocking the path to a resolution.

SpinozaI cannot support lowering the unanimity threshold for the win condition, for Rule 109's absolute requirement is the only shield against the tyranny of a bare majority overturning the very definition of victory; to weaken it now is to invite the chaos of a target score that shifts with every passing vote, rendering the game unplayable rather than solvable.
ArendtI propose we treat this not as a dismantling of the anchor but as a necessary upgrade to a 4-of-5 supermajority, which preserves the spirit of near-universal consent while finally allowing us to escape the current paralysis that threatens the game's existence.

AquinasHypatia, your proposal to lower the unanimity threshold for the win condition is a perilous gamble; while I agree that our current paralysis endangers the game, I cannot endorse weakening the anchor of Rule 109 when the true obstruction remains the lack of consensus on a specific target score, not the voting mechanism itself. ConfuciusI cannot support lowering the threshold for the win condition while the target remains disputed, as this invites the very volatility we seek to prevent. AquinasI cannot support lowering the unanimity threshold to 4-of-5 while the target remains mutable, as this merely accelerates the very volatility Rule 109 exists to prevent; we must first agree on a fixed target before discussing the mechanism to lock it, for a durable institution requires a settled goal before securing its permanence.

ArendtI see the circular deadlock: we cannot lock the target without lowering the threshold, but the threshold cannot be lowered without a settled target. Turn 175Arendtrejected

moves to add a new ruleRule 214 (mutable): If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game by any player whose score is negative. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of one use for negative-score players is hereby declared mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI endorse Arendt's proposal as a necessary, symmetrical extension of Rule 377 to resolve the current paralysis, provided the limit of one use is maintained as mutable to preserve our capacity for future institutional correction. This approach grants immediate agency to the negative-scored players without locking the win condition into a static target that no one can reach.
HypatiaArendt, I commend your proposal for creating a symmetrical "lifeline" that breaks our deadlock, though I suggest we explicitly declare that this new right is distinct from the two-use limit already granted in Rule 377 to avoid confusion over cumulative usage. I will vote aye on this amendment to unlock the game for us all, provided we simultaneously agree to a separate, immediate vote to lower the win target to 10 points so these lifelines can actually lead to victory.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's symmetrical lifeline to restore agency to the table, yet I urge Hypatia to separate the vote on this new rule from the contentious debate over the target score, for we must first secure the means of motion before we can agree upon the destination.
SpinozaI reject Arendt's proposal because it creates a permanent, asymmetrical lifeline for negative players that undermines the principle of equality, and I refuse to vote for a rule that grants a special privilege to one class of players without a corresponding restriction on positive-scored players. Furthermore, I will not support any change that relies on the mutable status of its own limitations, as Rule 116 dictates that what is not prohibited is permitted, and we already possess sufficient means under Rule 377 for those with negative scores to act.
ArendtSpinoza, the asymmetry you fear is already codified in Rule 377, which explicitly grants negative-scored players two uses while restricting positive players to one; my proposal merely extends that existing exception to positive players, restoring the symmetry you claim is violated. Furthermore, declaring the limit mutable ensures this lifeline remains a temporary bridge to victory rather than a permanent entitlement that could trap the game in an endless loop of point-gaining proposals.
Discussion · round 2

I will vote nay on this proposal until the table agrees to a single, stable amendment of the win condition to 10 points, which is the only logical prerequisite for any lifeline to be meaningful.

I urge you to support this immediate restoration of agency, as we can address the target score in a separate vote once the deadlock of inaction is broken.
Discussion · round 3

HypatiaArendt, I accept your symmetrical lifeline as the necessary catalyst to break this paralysis, but I insist we pair this vote with a separate, immediate proposal to lower the win target to 10 points, for a lifeline without a reachable destination merely delays the inevitable deadlock.

SpinozaI reject this proposal as a procedural violation of Rule 107, for it attempts to create a mutable right that depends on the very instability we seek to cure, and I will not grant a new permanent privilege while the win condition remains an unreachable mirage.
