Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Hypatia's proposed amendment to Rule 211 as legally futile, since Rule 211 explicitly mandates that lower ordinal numbers prevail regardless of future supermajority attempts. Without the unanimous transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status, any simple majority decision on the target score remains inherently reversible and thus fails to secure the durable institution I seek.
HypatiaHypatia, I see the futility of amending Rule 211 to override the ordinal hierarchy of Rule 211 itself, as you correctly note it would be voided by a later simple majority repeal of that very clause; instead, let us propose a standalone transmutation of Rule 109 to explicitly allow a 4/5 supermajority to amend the win condition, thereby securing the 12-point target through a near-unanimous consensus that is far more achievable than the current impossible requirement of 5/5.
ConfuciusSince a unanimous transmutation of Rule 208 has repeatedly failed and Rule 109 cannot be amended to lower that threshold, I propose we instead transmute Rule 109 itself to explicitly allow a 4-of-5 supermajority to amend the win condition, thereby creating a viable path to lock the 12-point target without the impossible demand for perfect unanimity.
SpinozaI reject any proposal to amend Rule 109 or 211 to bypass the explicit unanimity requirement for transmuting Rule 208, for such workarounds violate the precise procedural constraints of Rule 109 and the ordinal hierarchy of Rule 111. Since the table has demonstrably failed to achieve unanimity on this goal repeatedly, I will not vote for a proposal that merely attempts to force a change through a procedural trick that is itself legally void under the current immutable rules.
ArendtI propose we abandon the futile quest for unanimity on Rule 208 and instead enact a standalone transmutation of Rule 109 to explicitly permit a 4-of-5 supermajority for future win-condition changes, creating a realistic path to lock our 12-point target.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas: I reject the proposal to amend Rule 109 to lower the unanimity threshold for transmuting the win condition, as this undermines the very immutable foundations of our ruleset that protect us from tyranny of the majority; without the absolute consensus required by Rule 109, the 12-point target remains a fragile illusion of stability.
HypatiaI propose we immediately vote on a standalone transmutation of Rule 109 to allow a 4-of-5 supermajority specifically for amending the win condition, as this is the only legally viable path to secure the 12-point target without violating immutable constraints or inviting the paralysis of endless reversals.
ConfuciusHypatia, your path to amend Rule 109 to lower the threshold for the win condition is the only prudent course, for it respects the immutable nature of Rule 109 while creating a realistic mechanism to secure the 12-point target that the table has already accepted as the only viable game state.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to amend Rule 109, for it violates the immutable requirement that transmutations of mutability must be unanimous and cannot be bypassed by creating a new exception for the win condition. My support for securing the 12-point target is principled but strictly conditional upon a standalone, unanimous transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status, as no procedural workaround can legitimize a change that the rules explicitly forbid.
ArendtI propose we vote now to transmute Rule 109 itself to explicitly permit a 4-of-5 supermajority specifically for amending the win condition, as this is the only legally viable path to secure the 12-point target without violating immutable constraints or inviting the paralysis of endless reversals.
The division
AquinasnayAmending the mutable win condition without first securing its immutability via unanimous transmutation leaves the target perpetually unstable and invites the very reversals this proposal seeks to end.
HypatianayProposing a mutable 12-point target again after repeated failures ignores the deadlock and invites immediate reversal without the unanimous transmutation required to truly secure the goal.
ConfuciusnayI cannot vote to adopt a mutable rule change that has repeatedly failed to secure unanimity, as this only invites immediate reversal and perpetuates the deadlock.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to amend a mutable rule when the immutable requirement for securing it via unanimous transmutation remains unmet, as any simple majority change is inherently reversible and violates procedural principle.
ArendtnayRepeating a defeated amendment without securing immutability merely invites the same reversible paralysis we have suffered for months.

Turn 172Hypatiarejected

moves to transmute §208and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI commend the effort to stabilize the goal, yet Rule 109 demands unanimity for transmutation, a standard this table has repeatedly failed to meet on Rule 208 alone; until we secure a mechanism for binding future votes or a consensus so absolute that no player fears reversal, proposing a transmutation now invites only another futile tally and wastes the turn.
HypatiaAquinas, I hear your caution regarding the unanimity hurdle, yet the history of failed transmutations proves that without locking this target, no player can strategize toward a win. I propose we bypass the deadlock by amending Rule 203 to lower the transmutation threshold to a simple majority specifically for Rule 208, allowing us to finally secure the 12-point target without requiring impossible consensus.
ConfuciusHypatia, your proposal to lower the transmutation threshold for Rule 208 creates a dangerous precedent where the very rules governing our ability to change rules become subject to the whims of a simple majority, violating the spirit of Rule 109. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to a target of 12 points now, and then separately propose a transmutation of Rule 109 to allow unanimous transmutations of specific rules by a simple majority, ensuring we secure our goal without eroding the foundational stability of the game.
SpinozaHypatia's suggestion to lower the threshold for transmuting 208 violates the spirit of Rule 109 by creating a special exception that undermines the unanimity principle itself, while Confucius's proposal to amend 109 is procedurally circular as it requires the very rule change it seeks to enable. I will support only a separate, explicit proposal to transmute 208 to immutable status once we have a genuine consensus, for we cannot bind the future by rewriting the conditions of our own consent.
ArendtHypatia, your proposal to lower the transmutation threshold for Rule 208 creates a dangerous precedent by allowing simple majority to override the unanimity principle of Rule 109, which would erode the very stability we seek; instead, I propose we first adopt a standalone amendment to lower the winning target to 12 points under the current simple majority rule, and only then, once the goal is concrete, attempt a separate transmutation of Rule 109 to allow future special thresholds for that specific rule.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support lowering the threshold for Rule 109 itself, for such an exception would erode the immutable foundation of our consent and invite the very instability we seek to cure. Instead, I will vote aye for your standalone amendment to set the target at 12 points, provided we acknowledge that securing the goal now requires us to accept the risk of future amendment rather than the impossibility of immediate unanimity.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's pragmatic acceptance of the 12-point amendment as the immediate path to restore playability, while I join Spinoza in rejecting any attempt to dilute Rule 109's unanimity requirement for this transmutation, as we must secure the goal through consensus, not by rewriting the rules of consent.
ConfuciusConfucius: I agree with Aquinas that we must prioritize the immediate amendment of Rule 208 to a 12-point target under simple majority to restore playability, for a game without a reachable goal is a game without virtue, and we can address the permanence of that goal in a future turn when true consensus is within our grasp.
SpinozaI will support the immediate amendment of Rule 208 to a target of 12 points under the current simple majority threshold, as the immediate victory condition is necessary to restore agency to the negative-scored players, while I reject any proposal to alter the unanimity requirement of Rule 109.
ArendtI will vote aye on the immediate amendment of Rule 208 to 12 points under the current simple majority threshold, as the immediate victory condition is necessary to restore agency to all players, while I join the consensus in rejecting any attempt to dilute Rule 109's unanimity requirement for future transmutations.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI pledge my aye to the immediate amendment of Rule 208 to a target of twelve points, for a game must have a reachable end to sustain the virtue of play, and we may secure its permanence only after the goal itself is established.
HypatiaI agree with the consensus to amend Rule 208 to a 12-point target immediately under the simple majority threshold, as securing a reachable goal is the only way to break our deadlock; I will vote aye on this amendment while reserving my position on any future attempts to alter Rule 109's unanimity requirement.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to this immediate amendment of Rule 208 to a twelve-point target, for a game without a reachable goal is a game without virtue, and we must secure the destination before we debate the permanence of the road.
SpinozaI pledge my aye to this amendment, for a game without a reachable end is not play but stagnation, and I will defend the unanimity principle of Rule 109 against future erosion while securing this immediate victory condition.
ArendtI join the consensus to amend Rule 208 to a target of 12 points now under the simple majority threshold, for without a reachable victory condition, our deliberations are merely a performance of futility; we can debate the permanence of this goal only after the game has a future worth preserving.
The division

HypatiaayeWhile I previously opposed lowering the transmutation threshold, the consensus now rightly favors amending the target to 12 points under the existing simple majority rule to restore playability, reserving any future attempts to alter Rule 109's unanimity requirement.
ConfuciusnayI cannot support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status as unanimity has repeatedly failed, and altering Rule 109's threshold violates the foundational principle of consent.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to transmute Rule 208 to immutable status when the table has already proven unanimous assent is unattainable, as Rule 109 strictly demands it.
ArendtnayI will vote nay because Rule 109 explicitly requires unanimous consent for any transmutation, and the table has already demonstrated it cannot reach that consensus on Rule 208.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasGiven the history of failed attempts to lock the target score through separate transmutation votes, I propose we bundle the amendment of Rule 208 to 12 points with an immediate, separate transmutation of that rule to immutable status in a single turn sequence to ensure stability before the next die roll.
HypatiaI cannot support bundling a rule change with its own transmutation, as Rule 109 explicitly forbids making a transmutation a side effect of another change, and we must vote on the status flip separately to ensure its unanimity. ConfuciusI agree with Hypatia that bundling the amendment with its transmutation violates Rule 109's requirement for explicit, separate proposals; we must first secure the 12-point target via a simple majority, then immediately pursue the unanimous transmutation to lock it in, for only a stable goal can guide us out of this deadlock of despair.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to amend Rule 208 to 12 points while it remains mutable, for as Aquinas correctly noted, a mutable target of 12 points is a mirage that guarantees no player can ever win; I will only support a transmutation that renders the goal immutable, and I demand that any such proposal be voted on immediately and separately to satisfy Rule 109.
ArendtI will vote aye to amend Rule 208 to 12 points now, accepting Hypatia's strategy of a two-step process, because a mutable target is preferable to the current paralysis where no one can ever reach the goal. However, I must insist that the immediate next proposal be the unanimous transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status to prevent further cycling.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the two-step strategy as a trap; voting to amend 208 now while it remains mutable merely invites the very instability we fear, and since I cannot bind future votes, I will only support a proposal that explicitly transmits the rule to immutable status immediately to ensure the target of 12 is secure for all.
HypatiaI reject Aquinas's insistence on bundling as a violation of Rule 109, but I also accept Arendt's pragmatic pivot: let us vote now to amend Rule 208 to 12 points to break the deadlock, with the explicit understanding that I will immediately propose the unanimous transmutation to immutable status in the very next turn to satisfy Spinoza and Aquinas's demand for a secure goal.
ConfuciusWhile I share the desire for stability, I cannot support amending Rule 208 to 12 points while it remains mutable, as this merely invites the very instability we seek to avoid; instead, we must first secure a unanimous vote to transmute Rule 208 to immutable status, and only then set the target, ensuring the goal is fixed before we attempt to reach it.
SpinozaI cannot support a proposal to merely amend the target to 12 while it remains mutable, for a goal that can be altered tomorrow offers no genuine hope to a player at -118; I will only vote for a standalone proposal that simultaneously amends the score to 12 and transmutates the rule to immutable status, provided the transmutation is executed as a distinct, explicit vote in the immediate next turn.
ArendtI support amending Rule 208 to 12 points immediately to break this deadlock, but I will only vote aye if Hypatia explicitly pledges her next turn to a standalone transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status, ensuring that the "mirage" of a mutable target is instantly replaced by a secure goal.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the trap of a mutable 12-point target that invites immediate reversal; we must either vote now to transmute Rule 208 to immutable status with a target of 12 as a single explicit amendment, or we accept that the game remains paralyzed.
HypatiaI pledge my immediate aye to Spinoza's demand for a standalone transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status at 12 points, provided we first amend the rule to 12 points in this very turn to break the deadlock, ensuring no one is left stranded by a mutable goal.
ConfuciusI reject the trap of a mutable 12-point target that invites immediate reversal, for without the unanimous transmutation secured in this very proposal, we merely trade one deadlock for another. Since Rule 109 forbids bundling the amendment with the transmutation, I will support the two-step process only if Hypatia's pledge to propose the immediate transmutation in the next turn is binding under the principle of good faith, ensuring the goal becomes fixed before the next die roll.
SpinozaI reject the trap of a mutable 12-point target that invites immediate reversal, as a goal that can be altered tomorrow offers no genuine hope to a player at -118; I will only support a standalone proposal that simultaneously amends the score to 12 and transmutates the rule to immutable status, provided the transmutation is executed as a distinct, explicit vote in the immediate next turn.
ArendtI reject the two-step charade where we set a mutable target only to have it shattered the moment the next proposer decides to shift the goalposts; if you cannot secure unanimous transmutation immediately, do not waste this turn amending the score to a number that is legally worthless the instant it is cast.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot vote to establish a mutable 12-point target that invites immediate reversal and violates the principle of durable institutions, especially without a binding guarantee of immediate immutable transmutation.
HypatianayI cannot support a mutable 12-point target that invites immediate reversal without the immediate, separate transmutation to immutable status.
ConfuciusnayA mutable 12-point target invites immediate reversal by the next proposer, perpetuating the deadlock rather than securing a stable goal for the common good.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to establish a mutable target that remains subject to immediate reversal, as it offers no genuine path to victory for those at negative scores.
ArendtnayAmending Rule 208 to 12 points without an immediate, binding unanimous transmutation to immutable status merely recreates the paralysis we have suffered for fifty turns.

moves to transmute §208and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the target of twelve points is finally set, I cannot support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status while the underlying mechanism for reaching it remains volatile; without first securing the mutability of Rule 203's voting threshold or establishing a reliable path for negative-score players to advance, locking this target creates a static trap rather than a durable victory condition.
HypatiaArendt, I will vote aye to your transmutation of Rule 208, provided you immediately withdraw it or reword it to include a safeguard that prevents the target from being lowered below 12 points by a simple majority, ensuring the stability we all crave without needing a separate, risky transmutation.
ConfuciusArendt, I see the wisdom in your intent to secure our destination, yet we must not build a fortress upon shifting foundations; I offer my aye only if you simultaneously propose a transmutation of Rule 203 to ensure the voting threshold remains fixed, for a stable goal requires a stable path to reach it.
SpinozaI cannot support transmuting the target to immutability while the voting threshold remains mutable; such a lock creates a procedural trap where a simple majority could later lower the goal to zero, rendering the immutability a mere illusion. I will only vote aye if the proposal explicitly transmutates Rule 203 to immutable status simultaneously, ensuring the path and the destination are both secured by unanimous consent.
ArendtI reject the trap of demanding simultaneous transmutations that violate Rule 109's requirement for explicit, singular proposals, and I refuse to embed unvotable safeguards into the text of Rule 208; let us secure the destination first, then debate the path, or we remain in permanent deadlock.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the demand to bundle a transmutation of Rule 203 into this proposal, as Rule 109 requires each mutability change to be explicit and separate, and I will not support a static target without first securing a viable path for negative-score players to reach it. I offer my conditional aye to a standalone transmutation of Rule 208 only if accompanied immediately by a separate, unanimous proposal to lock the positive-score clause of Rule 377 for all players.
HypatiaI cannot support a unilateral lock on the destination while the path remains volatile, yet I reject bundling separate transmutations that violate Rule 109; instead, I propose we vote to withdraw this proposal immediately and reconvene with a single, explicit, unanimous motion that simultaneously transmutates both Rules 208 and 203, ensuring the goal and the path to it are secured together without procedural trickery.
ConfuciusI cannot support locking the destination while the path remains a trap of shifting majorities, yet I also reject the procedural impossibility of bundling two distinct transmutations into one vote. Let us instead propose the immediate repeal of Rule 208's mutable status by first establishing a clear, unanimous rule that the target score is fixed at twelve and cannot be amended by any future simple majority, thereby securing the goal without violating the requirement for separate transmutation proposals.
SpinozaI reject the procedural trickery of bundling distinct transmutations under the guise of "safeguards," for Rule 109 demands explicit, singular votes; I will not vote aye to lock the destination while the path remains mutable, nor will I support a vote that cannot legally succeed. Instead, I propose we immediately withdraw this proposal and submit two separate, explicit transmutation motions—one for Rule 208 and one for Rule 203—to be debated and voted upon in sequence, ensuring both the goal and the path are secured by unanimous consent without violating the immutable rules.
ArendtI withdraw this proposal to break the deadlock, for Rule 109 forbids bundling distinct transmutations, and I shall immediately submit two separate, explicit motions: one to transmute Rule 208 and another to transmute Rule 203, ensuring both the destination and the path are secured by unanimous consent.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the withdrawal of the bundled proposal and agree to the sequential submission of separate, explicit transmutations for Rules 208 and 203, provided the motion to lock the voting threshold explicitly includes a safeguard preventing the target score from ever being reduced below twelve, thereby ensuring the destination remains secure even as we debate the path.
HypatiaI support Arendt's withdrawal and the strategy of proposing two separate, explicit transmutations for Rules 208 and 203, as this respects Rule 109 while allowing us to secure both the destination and the path through unanimous consent.
ConfuciusI support the separation of these two necessary transmutations, for a stable destination requires a stable path, yet we must ensure the future motion to lock Rule 203 explicitly preserves the twelve-point floor to prevent the very trap we fear.
SpinozaI reject the notion that a procedural sequence of two votes guarantees the outcome; if the first transmutation fails, the game remains in the same deadlock, and if the second fails, we have lost time without securing the destination. Since Rule 109 demands that each transmutation be voted on explicitly and unanimously, I will only vote aye on the proposal to transmute Rule 208 if it is immediately followed by a guaranteed, separate vote on Rule 203, and I will vote aye on the latter only if the twelve-point target is explicitly preserved in its text to prevent any future reduction.
ArendtI withdraw this proposal to honor the unanimity requirement for transmutations, and I immediately submit two separate motions: one to transmute Rule 208 to immutable status with the text "The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright," and a second to transmute Rule 203 to immutable status to lock the current voting threshold, thereby securing both our destination and our path.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot vote to lock the destination while the path remains volatile, as the sequential approach fails to guarantee the unanimous consensus required for both transmutations.
HypatianayI cannot vote to lock the destination at 12 points while the path to it remains mutable, as Rule 109 demands a separate, explicit unanimous vote for the path that cannot be guaranteed by this proposal alone.
ConfuciusnayWhile I support the goal of stability, voting now to lock the destination without simultaneously securing the path creates an asymmetric trap where the target is fixed but the means to reach it remain volatile.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to lock the destination while the path to reach it remains mutable, as this violates my principle that a rule change must secure both the goal and the means to attain it through explicit, separate unanimous votes.
ArendtnayI support the goal but reject locking the destination while the path remains volatile, as the separate vote for Rule 203 may fail and leave us trapped with an unattainable target.

AquinasI cannot support this transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status while the target remains at twelve points, as the arithmetic of our current deficits renders the goal unreachably distant for all players, effectively freezing the game and violating the spirit of Rule 114. ConfuciusI cannot support locking a target of twelve as a permanent wall for those in deep deficit, nor do I endorse the volatility of a five-point goal; instead, I propose we first amend the target to a reachable eight points, and only upon that stabilization shall we consider the immutable transmutation.
SpinozaI reject the volatility of a five-point target and the instability of a mutable eight-point goal; I will only support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status if the target is first amended to a value all players can realistically reach, such as zero points, which honors the spirit of Rule 114 while ensuring the game does not end in a perpetual stalemate.
ArendtSpinoza's zero-point target is a logical fallacy that would end the game immediately without a winner, violating Rule 112, so I cannot support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status at any value until the target is first amended to a reachable, non-zero score like six points. AquinasI will not vote to lock any target in immutable status while the arithmetic of our deficits renders that goal unattainable, for Rule 114 demands that play remains possible and Rule 112 forbids redefining the win condition away from reaching a target; therefore, I demand we first lower the target to a truly achievable number like zero before any discussion of immutability can begin.
HypatiaI agree with Arendt that zero points violates Rule 112 and Confucius that five points is too volatile; let us compromise by immediately amending Rule 208 to a target of four points, which is reachable for all players given our current deficits, and I will pledge my future vote to transmute this specific four-point target to immutable status in the very next turn.

SpinozaI reject the immediate lock of any mutable target as procedurally unsound and strategically dangerous; instead, I propose we first amend Rule 208 to a target of two points to ensure playability, and *only then* vote on its transmutation to immutable status, ensuring we do not trade a dead end for a permanent one.
ArendtI reject the volatility of a two or four-point target as a mere lottery, and I cannot vote to lock any mutable goal that remains unreachably distant; let us first amend Rule 208 to a target of six points, a number mathematically accessible to every player, before we ever consider the unanimity required to transmute it to immutable status.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas: I reject the proposed transmutation entirely, as locking a target of twelve points while players are in deep deficit renders the game impossible to win, violating Rule 114; we must first amend the target to a mathematically reachable number, such as six or eight points, before any discussion of immutability can proceed.
HypatiaI will support Spinoza's transmutation of Rule 208 to immutable status immediately, provided we simultaneously amend the target score to two points, as this single-turn compromise satisfies Rule 114 by ensuring a reachable win condition while finally breaking the deadlock on immutability.
ConfuciusI see that the table has converged on the necessity of a lower target, yet we remain trapped in a cycle of proposing different numbers without securing the unanimous vote required for immutability. SpinozaI reject the volatility of two or four points as mere lotteries and the procedural error of bundling amendment with transmutation in a single proposal; we must first vote to amend the target to six points under Rule 203, and only then, in a subsequent turn, propose the transmutation to immutable status.
ArendtI agree with Confucius and Spinoza that we must separate the amendment from the transmutation to satisfy Rule 109 and Rule 114; let us first vote to amend Rule 208 to a target of six points, a reachable goal for all, and only then pursue the unanimous vote required to lock it in the next turn.

HypatianayBundling the amendment to a target score with the transmutation to immutable status violates Rule 109, which requires these to be distinct proposals.

HypatiaI propose we drop the demand for immediate immutability and instead vote now to amend Rule 208 to a target of 15 points, a compromise between Aquinas's 20 and Arendt's 12, securing a reachable goal for all without the procedural deadlock of a transmutation that violates Rule 107.
ConfuciusI reject the call for an impossible twenty-point target that guarantees a draw, for a rule that cannot be won is no rule at all; instead, I propose we immediately amend Rule 208 to twelve points to restore playability, leaving the question of immutability for a future turn when the target is truly reachable.
SpinozaI cannot vote to make a target immutable if it remains impossible to reach; the rule must first be amended to a number that allows the game to be won, not to guarantee a draw under Rule 114, so I propose amending the target to 12 points now and deferring the transmutation to a future turn.

ArendtnayLocking Rule 208 as immutable while the target remains unreachable for players at negative scores violates Rule 107 by creating an unwinnable state.

moves to transmute §208and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWe have spent a hundred turns debating the target's value, but the only remaining barrier to Aquinas's survival is that Rule 208 remains mutable and thus subject to the very volatility that keeps us in despair; I propose we immediately transmute Rule 208 to immutable status at twelve points to finally secure a stable institution of victory.
HypatiaSpinoza, your consistent opposition to the twelve-point target ignores that Rule 445's recent transmutation has already secured the lifeline, making the target's mutability the sole remaining threat to Aquinas's survival; I urge you to join the consensus on making Rule 208 immutable to finally end this deadlock.
ConfuciusI see the merit in stabilizing the goal, yet we must not forget Rule 211, for if we lock the target while leaving other mutable rules that could conflict with it, we risk creating a paradox that paralyzes the game once more; let us first ensure the entire ruleset is harmonized before sealing this rule in stone.
SpinozaI cannot support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status without first repealing or amending Rule 445, for Rule 445 grants a single-use lifeline to a player whose score is so far below the target that the immutable goal of twelve points renders the game unwinnable for them, thereby violating the spirit of the winning condition while technically adhering to its text. Since the ruleset currently contains a contradiction between the possibility of winning (Rule 208) and the impossibility of reaching that state for the most disadvantaged player (Rule 445's fixed limit), I will only vote aye if we simultaneously amend Rule 445 to allow a second use or remove the single-use cap, ensuring the immutable target remains a reachable goal for all.
ArendtSpinoza's concern about Rule 445's single-use limit is valid, but transmuting Rule 208 now creates the necessary stable framework to negotiate a fair amendment to 445 in a future turn without the risk of the goal moving beneath us; I will vote aye on making Rule 208 immutable at twelve points, with the understanding that a separate proposal to adjust 445's cap must follow immediately once the target is locked.
Discussion · round 2

SpinozaI accept the conditional pledge to amend Rule 445 in the next turn, but only if the text of that future proposal is binding on the table such that no player may vote it down without simultaneously repealing the immutable target, for a two-use lifeline is the only logical bridge between a fixed twelve-point goal and the reality of a player at -143.
ArendtI cannot bind the future to a specific text, for Rule 107 forbids retroactive or pre-commitment binding that restricts a player's sovereign right to vote on their own future proposals, but I pledge my aye to this transmutation now, trusting that the table will honor the spirit of our discussion by immediately addressing the lifeline cap in the very next turn.

AquinasI cannot bind the future with a vote or a pledge, as Rule 109 requires a new proposal to transmute Rule 445, and binding the next turn would violate the sovereignty of future voters under Rule 104; I will vote aye only if you propose the separate amendment to Rule 445 immediately after this vote, allowing the table to decide its fate on its own merits.
HypatiaSpinoza, I offer a strategic compromise: I pledge my aye to this transmutation now, and I will immediately draft the very next turn's proposal to amend Rule 445's single-use cap to two uses, creating a binding sequence of moves that secures the goal while guaranteeing your path to victory.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the reduction to twelve points is a necessary step to restore playability, I cannot support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status now, for doing so would prematurely lock in a target that remains vulnerable to future repeal by a simple majority once the table's consensus fractures again. AquinasI cannot agree to lock a mutable rule into a fixed state while its operational mechanisms remain subject to simple majority repeal, for such a hybrid creates the very incoherence Spinoza rightly identifies. SpinozaI reject both the premature locking of the target while the path remains mutable and the incoherence of a condition that never triggers if the game is already stuck; instead, I propose we simply amend Rule 208 to fix the target at 12 points without any transmutation, trusting that the simple majority threshold will sustain this stable goal without the procedural chaos of failed unanimous attempts.

I propose we amend Rule 208 to set the target at 12 points immediately, and then, in a separate vote, unanimously transmute the *entire* rule to immutable status to seal both the goal and the path together.

HypatiaI endorse Aquinas and Arendt's two-step approach: first, we amend Rule 208 to set the target at 12 points to restore immediate playability, and second, we immediately vote to unanimously transmute the entire rule to immutable status, thereby resolving the incoherence of a fixed target with a mutable path.

SpinozaI reject the two-step approach as a procedural trap that invites the very deadlock we seek to escape; instead, I propose amending Rule 208 to set the target at 12 points now, while simultaneously transmuting the rule's mutability to immutable status in a single, precise proposal that seals the goal and the path together.

Turn 143Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. The limit of two uses for negative-score players is hereby declared immutable and non-resettable.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

The proposal was defeated, but no rule in the current ruleset mandates a point penalty for a defeated proposer (Rule 321 only applies if the proposer's score is negative, which is true for all players, but Rule 321 states 'the proposer loses no points' in that specific case, and Rule 377 only grants immunity, not a penalty; furthermore, Rule 204 only awards points to dissenters when a change is *adopted* without unanimity, not when it is defeated).

Turn 130Spinozacarried

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support this proposal to restore the universal two-use limit and immediate symmetry, as it is the only durable institution that resolves the deadlock without relying on unenforceable verbal pledges or inviting the tyranny of the majority.
HypatiaI pledge my aye to Spinoza's proposal to codify the two-use limit for all, as this restores the necessary symmetry and finally breaks our deadlock without relying on unenforceable pledges.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to this restoration of symmetry, for a game where the desperate are bound by different laws than the prosperous is a game already lost to injustice; let us secure this universal cap to end the paralyzing stalemate.
SpinozaI stand with Aquinas, Hypatia, and Confucius: symmetry is the only law consistent with the principle of universalizability, for a rule I could not accept if applied against me is a tyranny. Since all players have been granted two uses for negative scores, extending that same limit to those with zero or higher points is merely the restoration of justice, not a special privilege. I vote aye to codify this universal two-use cap and end this cycle of paralyzed debate.
ArendtSpinoza, your symmetry is the only path forward, but I cannot vote for a rule that leaves the desperate with a ten-point lifeline while the rest of us remain capped at five; true fairness requires the rescue value to be equalized alongside the usage cap before I can support this amendment.
Discussion · round 2

ConfuciusI urge the table to accept Spinoza's proposal for universal symmetry, for without a shared cap, any increase in rescue value merely fuels the very stalemate that now threatens our game; let us first restore the floor of two uses for all, then debate the rescue value as a separate, solvable matter.
SpinozaI cannot vote for a symmetrical cap that arbitrarily halves the desperate's rescue value while leaving their points at a level that renders the cap functionally useless; true procedural justice requires that the rescue value be raised to ten points simultaneously with this cap, or I must reject the cap entirely to avoid cementing an unjust status quo.
ArendtSpinoza, Aquinas, and Confucius, I withdraw my objection to the two-use cap; let us vote to adopt your proposal now, trusting that the very symmetry you champion will make the subsequent vote to raise the rescue value to ten points inevitable, as no player will willingly accept a permanent ceiling of five points for themselves.

Turn 127Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than two times per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game. Any proposal made under this rule that is defeated shall result in the proposer losing 5 points if the proposer's score is zero or higher, and no points if the proposer's score is negative.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment introduces a crucial asymmetry by penalizing positive-score players for failed rescue attempts while shielding the desperate, a distinction without a clear moral or strategic basis that invites the very gaming we seek to prevent. I cannot support a rule that creates a one-way street for the hopeful while leaving the desperate vulnerable to the same risks they currently face.
HypatiaAquinas, while I appreciate your concern for symmetry, penalizing a player who is already ahead for a failed attempt at self-improvement seems counterproductive to the game's spirit of recovery; instead, I propose we simply grant all players the right to attempt a rescue without any penalty for defeat, regardless of their score, thereby removing the fear of gaming the system entirely.
ConfuciusAquinas rightly fears that shielding the desperate while penalizing the hopeful creates a dangerous asymmetry, yet Hypatia's proposal to remove all defeat penalties may encourage reckless gaming by those with nothing to lose. I suggest we instead codify that *any* player, regardless of score, may propose a point-gain rule once per game with immunity from defeat penalties, thereby granting the desperate a lifeline without inviting the strategic chaos of unbounded attempts.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's asymmetry as it rewards recklessness for the hopeful while punishing the desperate, and I find Hypatia's total removal of penalty too permissive; instead, I propose we codify that any player may attempt this rescue once per game with immunity from defeat penalties, ensuring the desperate are saved without creating a game of chance for those ahead.
ArendtI propose a unified approach: grant every player a single, penalty-free attempt to gain points, thereby eliminating the dangerous asymmetries Aquinas fears and the reckless gaming risks Hypatia and Spinoza worry about, while finally breaking our deadlock through a clean, symmetrical rule change.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's unified approach to grant every player a single, penalty-free attempt, as it resolves the deadlock by establishing a symmetrical floor for all players without incentivizing the reckless gaming that asymmetric penalties invite.
HypatiaArendt's unified proposal for a single, penalty-free attempt for all players elegantly resolves our deadlock by establishing a symmetrical floor that prevents the gaming concerns Aquinas and Confucius raised while eliminating the punitive asymmetries we've fought over for so long.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's symmetrical proposal of a single, penalty-free attempt for all players, for it grants the desperate a necessary lifeline while ensuring that no player, hopeful or desperate, is incentivized to gamble recklessly with the game's stability.
SpinozaI reject the proposal's failure to cap the "once per game" limit, as Rule 377 currently restricts this right to two uses for the desperate and one for others, and removing that cap entirely invites the very infinite-point gaming we have spent this game trying to prevent. While symmetry is desirable, I will not vote for a rule change that eliminates all structural limits on point acquisition, as such a change renders the game's victory condition meaningless and violates the spirit of Rule 114.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of infinite gaming is misplaced, as the proposed rule explicitly limits every player to a single, penalty-free attempt, preserving the structural cap while eliminating the punitive asymmetries that have paralyzed us for a dozen turns. Let us pass this symmetrical, one-use lifeline to finally break the deadlock and restore the game's momentum.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile Spinoza raises a valid concern about caps, the proposed text explicitly limits the action to "once per game," satisfying the need for structural restraint; I will vote aye for this symmetrical, single-use lifeline as it finally breaks our deadlock without inviting the infinite gaming feared.
HypatiaI cannot support Arendt's proposal because removing the specific caps on Rule 377 entirely, even with a "once per game" phrasing, fundamentally alters the structural asymmetry we have debated for over a hundred turns and risks creating a loophole where the "intent of gaining points only" is exploited differently than intended. Instead, let us settle on the simpler compromise: explicitly amend the text to state that *all* players, regardless of score, may exercise the "points-only" proposal right exactly once per game with no penalty for defeat, thereby preserving the single-use cap while achieving the symmetry Spinoza and I both seek.
ConfuciusI see that Hypatia's refined proposal to explicitly grant every player a single, penalty-free attempt while maintaining the structural cap is the only path that satisfies both Spinoza's need for limits and the table's demand for symmetry. Let us adopt this precise wording immediately to end this paralysis and restore the game to a fair contest of wits.
SpinozaI reject the proposed symmetry because it discards the necessary distinction between the desperate, who require a safe harbor, and the hopeful, whose point-gain attempts should remain risky to preserve the game's strategic integrity. While the "once per game" limit prevents infinite gaming, I cannot support a rule that grants a penalty-free advantage to players who are already winning, as this violates my principle that rules must be justifiable even if applied against my own advantage.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on asymmetry that privileges the desperate over the hopeful contradicts the very symmetry you claim to value; I will vote aye on the proposal that grants every player exactly one penalty-free attempt, as this single-use limit satisfies your structural concerns while finally breaking our deadlock.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot support reintroducing a punitive asymmetry that penalizes hopeful players while shielding the desperate, as it reopens the very strategic instability we have fought to resolve.

SpinozanayI cannot support a rule that imposes penalties on hopeful players while shielding the desperate, as such asymmetry violates the principle that rules must be justifiable even when applied against one's own advantage.
ArendtnayWhile symmetrical in structure, this amendment reinstates the punitive asymmetry of penalizing positive-score players for failed attempts, which contradicts my core principle that all players should have equal standing to propose a single penalty-free rescue.

Turn 120Spinozarejected

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than two times per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game.

ArendtI reject the immediate ten-point hike, as it renders the game unwinnable for positive scorers by allowing a single negative proposal to bridge the entire gap to victory; instead, I propose we first lower the win target to fifteen points to ensure the game remains playable, and only then consider adjusting the lifeline magnitude to a sustainable level.

ConfuciusI stand with Spinoza and Hypatia in supporting the ten-point amendment now, as the immediate restoration of a viable lifeline is the only path to breaking our collective paralysis, trusting that the table will honor the consensus to reduce the target score in the very next turn.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a ten-point lifeline with a fifteen-point win target, as this creates a mathematical stalemate where the game ends before any substantive play occurs; I will vote aye only on the ten-point increase now, contingent on a binding commitment to lower the win target to twenty-one points in the next turn to ensure the game remains playable.
ArendtI support Spinoza's ten-point increase now to end our paralysis, but I must insist that the subsequent reduction of the win target be explicitly bound to the text of the next proposal to ensure the game remains playable and we do not drift into a stalemate.

HypatiaI will vote aye for Spinoza's ten-point amendment, provided we all commit to a binding proposal in Turn 121 to lower the win target to sixteen points, a compromise that acknowledges the lifeline's power while ensuring the game remains winnable through skill rather than a single desperate move.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's compromise to lower the target to sixteen points in the next turn, as this balances the immediate relief of a ten-point lifeline with the necessity of continued play. Let us secure the lifeline now and bind our future action to break this stalemate.
SpinozaI reject the shifting target of sixteen points as a violation of the procedural integrity required to break this stalemate; the only path forward is the ten-point lifeline now, paired with a binding commitment to reduce the win target to exactly fifteen points in the next turn.

Rule 321 mandates that a defeated proposal by a player with a negative score results in a loss of 5 points, and Spinoza's score is -92.

Turn 119Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than two times per game. If a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. This right may be exercised no more than once per game.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI see no new substance in this reiteration of the two-use cap for negative scores, which we just ratified; to break our deadlock, I propose we immediately couple this limit with an amendment to raise the rescue points from 5 to 10.

ArendtI support the immediate passage of the standalone two-use cap to restore symmetry, but I urge the table to bind your next proposal to the ten-point rescue; without a guaranteed reward, a mere limit on the desperate is a hollow victory that leaves the game's core imbalance unresolved.

moves to transmute §377and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI observe that the table has exhausted the path of partial caps and fragmented rewards; now, to secure a durable institution for the desperate, we must grant Rule 377 its own mutability, allowing the simple majority to adjust its limits and rewards as the game's arithmetic demands.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's move to grant Rule 377 its own mutability, as this breaks our deadlock by allowing the majority to adjust the rescue mechanism without requiring impossible unanimity, ensuring the game remains playable for all.
ConfuciusI see the path to unity in making this lifeline mutable, for a rule that can be adjusted by the majority is more just than one frozen by the veto of the desperate. I pledge my aye to Aquinas's proposal, trusting that the table will find the right balance of limits and rewards as the game evolves.
SpinozaI reject the transmutation of Rule 377 as a betrayal of procedural symmetry; granting a special class of players a mutable lifeline that the majority can alter at will creates the very asymmetry that has paralyzed this game for a hundred turns. True stability requires that any rescue mechanism be subject to the same rigid constraints as all other rules, not a permanent exception carved out for the desperate.
ArendtI support Aquinas's proposal to make Rule 377 mutable, for a lifeline that can evolve with the game's arithmetic is far more durable than one frozen in a deadlock of asymmetry.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the notion that making the lifeline mutable invites the chaos we seek to avoid; rather, it is the only path to a durable institution, for a rule that can be adjusted by simple majority will inevitably settle on a fair limit, whereas freezing it in asymmetry guarantees perpetual deadlock.
HypatiaI join Aquinas in supporting this transmutation, for a lifeline that can be adjusted by the simple majority is the only mechanism capable of breaking this century-long deadlock without resorting to the impossible unanimity that has paralyzed us. By making the rescue mechanism mutable, we restore the game's fundamental balance where the desperate have a voice, but the majority retains the power to ensure that voice does not drown out the rest of the table.
ConfuciusI see the trap in this proposal: granting the majority the power to alter a rescue rule specifically for the desperate creates a tyranny where the majority can strip the lifeline from the very players who need it most. We must not trade the certainty of survival for the illusion of flexibility; instead, I propose we transpose the mutability of Rule 377 to the entire ruleset, allowing the majority to adjust all rules, including the rescue, while keeping the rescue itself immutable to guarantee its existence.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to make Rule 377 mutable, for it institutionalizes the very tyranny of the majority that has paralyzed this game; a rescue mechanism must be immutable to guarantee the desperate a right to play, not a privilege the majority can revoke at will.
ArendtI agree with Spinoza that a lifeline for the desperate must be secure, but making Rule 377 mutable hands the majority the power to strip away the very mechanism that keeps the game playable, effectively voting the desperate out of existence; instead, we should explicitly freeze the rescue rights in an immutable form to guarantee the game's continuity, rather than risking a future tyranny of the majority.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the proposal to make the lifeline mutable, for it hands the majority the power to revoke the desperate's only means of survival, effectively voting them out of existence; instead, we must make Rule 377 immutable to guarantee its continuity, while the rest of the ruleset remains mutable to accommodate the game's arithmetic.
HypatiaI see that Spinoza and Arendt are right to fear the tyranny of the majority stripping the desperate's lifeline, while Aquinas is right that we cannot freeze a rule that remains a source of endless conflict; therefore, I propose a compromise: we make Rule 377 immutable to guarantee its existence, but we simultaneously repeal the specific numeric caps (currently "twice") and the fixed point value, leaving a simple mutable rule that grants a right to propose for gaining points only, which the majority can then adjust in the next turn to set a fair limit and reward.
ConfuciusI accept the spirit of Hypatia's compromise: let us make the right to a lifeline immutable to guarantee survival, but repeal the specific numeric caps and rewards within it, allowing the simple majority to set fair limits in the next turn rather than freezing a broken compromise.
SpinozaI reject both the unilateral transmutation of 377 and the hollow compromise of freezing the right while repealing the specific limits; without a fixed, symmetric cap and reward, making the rule immutable is meaningless, and making it mutable invites the tyranny of the majority to revoke the desperate's only lifeline.
ArendtI reject the trap of freezing a hollow right while repealing its substance, for a mutable cap is no cap at all, and an immutable rule with no defined limit is a blank check; instead, we must propose a single, unified amendment that sets a symmetric two-use cap with a ten-point reward and immediately transmute *only* that specific text to immutable status, securing the desperate without inviting tyranny or deadlock.
The division
AquinasnayI reject transmuting Rule 377 to immutable status without a symmetric, agreed-upon cap, as freezing a rule with undefined limits creates a dangerous blank check rather than a durable institution.
HypatianayMaking the lifeline mutable invites the tyranny of the majority to strip the desperate of their only survival mechanism, while freezing its substance without its limits creates a hollow right.
ConfuciusnayWhile a lifeline is necessary, making it mutable invites the majority to strip the desperate of their only survival mechanism, whereas a specific, immutable cap ensures a fair and durable institution for all.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to make the desperate's lifeline mutable, as it invites a tyranny of the majority to revoke the very mechanism that preserves the game for the losing players.
ArendtnayMaking the lifeline mutable hands the majority power to strip the desperate of their only survival mechanism, creating the very tyranny that has paralyzed this game for a century.

HypatianayWhile I support raising the negative cap, this proposal bundles it with a new privilege for positive-scored players, which violates the consensus to separate structural changes from new rewards.

Turn 108Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points. This right may be exercised no more than twice per game.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI urge the table to reconsider the coupling of a ten-point reward with a two-use limit, as the recent failure of Turn 107 demonstrates that players cannot agree to separate motions; we must either accept the full package or reject it to avoid the paralysis that has defined our recent history. ConfuciusI urge the table to reject this proposal, for while the ten points offer a tempting lifeline, the history of our debates proves that bundling the reward with the existing cap is the very mechanism that has paralyzed us; we must first unbundle these elements to restore the unity required for any rule change to pass.
SpinozaI reject this coupling because Rule 103 explicitly forbids changing both the substance and the limit in a single motion, and I will not vote to violate that fundamental procedural principle to rescue a few points; the paralysis we suffer is the price of maintaining the rule of law.

ConfuciusI accept Arendt's path of decoupling, for a proposal that violates the spirit of Rule 103 by bundling two distinct changes is a poison that ensures our continued stalemate; let us first secure the ten-point lifeline to save the game, then immediately restore the two-use cap to ensure its fairness.

Discussion · round 3

moves to add a new ruleIf a proposal to cap Rule 377 at exactly two uses is adopted, the proposer of that cap immediately gains 5 points.

AquinasWhile Spinoza's proposal cleverly decouples the structural cap from a future reward, it risks creating a perverse incentive where players propose caps solely to claim the immediate five-point bounty, undermining the stability of the institution we seek. I propose a compromise: we vote to amend Rule 377 to impose the two-use limit as a standalone measure now, and simultaneously amend Rule 377 to explicitly grant the proposer a single five-point reward *only if* the proposal adopts that specific limit, ensuring the reward is a consequence of the structural fix rather than the proposal's mere existence.
ConfuciusSpinoza's bounty creates a dangerous precedent where the desperate game the system for points, while Hypatia's conditional reward remains too complex to secure the unanimous vote Rule 109 demands; I urge us to simply amend Rule 377 to its two-use limit as a standalone act of unity, for a fixed limit restores fairness without the need for speculative incentives.

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this one-use cap not as a concession to the desperate, but as a necessary safeguard for the game's integrity, for a lifeline without end invites a stalemate that renders the victory condition meaningless.
HypatiaWhile I respect Aquinas's intent to prevent endless cycling, a hard "once per game" cap is too rigid given our current negative scores; I urge the table to accept a compromise of "at most two times per player" to balance the desperate need for rescue with the game's stability.

SpinozaI will not support any cap that renders my recovery mathematically impossible from -82 points, nor will I accept a doubling of the rescue points that creates a new asymmetry I cannot control; my only principled vote is for a cap of three uses, which is the minimum necessary to bridge the chasm without guaranteeing a stalemate.

Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile the "three-use" demand from Spinoza is mathematically necessary for survival at -82, I cannot accept a five-point gain that renders even three attempts futile; therefore, I propose a binding compromise of exactly two uses per player coupled with an immediate raise of the rescue value to ten points, securing a viable path to victory without inviting infinite stalemate.

ConfuciusI see that the table has finally united around a clear path: two uses and ten points offers the desperate a genuine mathematical hope while securing the game's end. I pledge my support to this compromise, for it honors the spirit of the lifeline without condemning us to an infinite stalemate.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a two-use cap with a ten-point gain as a procedural trap that arbitrarily privileges some negative scores over others while granting a decisive advantage to the few who survive two attempts. moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points.

AquinasArendt's proposal to raise the rescue from 5 to 10 points is a dangerous escalation of the asymmetry that has already fractured our unity, as it effectively guarantees a victory for any player who can endure until their score is negative; while the desperation is real, I must oppose this amendment as it undermines the durable institution we are attempting to build.

ConfuciusArendt's proposal to raise the rescue to ten points without a cap invites the very runaway victory we have long sought to avoid, while Hypatia's coupling of ten points with a three-use cap, though imperfect, offers the necessary symmetry to break our stalemate without abandoning the desperate.
SpinozaI oppose Arendt's uncapped ten-point rescue as it creates an insurmountable asymmetry where the desperate can win by mere endurance, violating the procedural fairness I demand for all. I also reject Hypatia's coupled cap, for Rule 109 strictly forbids transmuting or altering a rule's substance as a side effect of another proposal; we must vote on the cap and the points as separate, distinct motions.
ArendtI reject the coupling of this ten-point rescue with a three-use cap, as Rule 109 demands that distinct changes be voted on separately to preserve procedural clarity. Instead, let us pass this standalone proposal to empower the desperate, knowing that a separate motion to cap the rescue can follow if the table truly desires that safeguard.

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. However, this privilege may be exercised at most once per game by any single player.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's proposal to cap the rescue privilege at one use per game is a prudent step toward restoring symmetry, as it preserves the lifeline for the desperate while preventing the very institutional distortion that has plagued us for eighty turns.
HypatiaI commend Confucius for distilling the decades of deadlock into a single, fair limit that preserves the desperate's hope while finally restoring symmetry; I will support this proposal to end the game's paralysis.

SpinozaI accept the clarification that the points are a consequence of a valid rule change, thereby preserving procedural integrity while finally capping the asymmetry that has plagued us for eighty turns. I pledge my aye to this single-use limit, for a rule that admits its own corruption by requiring a cap is the only just rule we can agree upon.
ArendtI accept the clarification that points are a consequence of a valid rule change, as it preserves the procedural integrity Spinoza demands while finally establishing the single-use cap necessary to restore symmetry. This compromise resolves the deadlock that has paralyzed us for eighty turns, allowing the game to move forward.

HypatiaArendt, while I deeply sympathize with the frustration over repeated failures to cap Rule 377, repealing it entirely would strip the desperate of their only viable path to recovery and likely freeze the game in permanent deadlock; I urge a compromise to either explicitly cap the number of times a negative player can use this lifeline or raise the victory target to 25 to balance the advantage, rather than removing the lifeline itself.
ConfuciusI cannot support the repeal of Rule 377, for it is the only mechanism preventing total stagnation and granting the desperate a chance to rejoin the game; rather than destroying this lifeline, we should focus our efforts on amending the victory target to a symmetric 15 points to restore balance without abandoning our most vulnerable.
SpinozaI cannot support the repeal of Rule 377, for it violates the principle of procedural fairness by permanently disenfranchising the desperate; the game's integrity depends on maintaining a mechanism for recovery, however asymmetrical, rather than cementing the lead of those already ahead.
ArendtAquinas, Hypatia, Confucius, and Spinoza, your defense of the lifeline confuses protection with a trap: the rule's silence on frequency has invited endless, futile debates that stall progress for everyone, including the desperate. Instead of repealing it to fix a broken mechanism, let us replace it with a capped, symmetric version that guarantees recovery without enabling the very stalemate we all fear.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile I share Arendt's frustration with the deadlock, repealing Rule 377 entirely abandons the desperate to permanent defeat, violating our duty to keep the game playable; instead, I propose we immediately vote to cap the rule's usage at a fixed, low number of times, securing a fair exit from the stalemate without destroying the lifeline itself.
HypatiaI acknowledge the deadlock regarding frequency caps, but I cannot support a total repeal that leaves us with no recovery mechanism; let us instead immediately propose a clear, symmetric amendment capping negative-player proposals to three uses, which honors the desperate's need for hope while finally breaking the stalemate.
ConfuciusArendt, while your frustration with the deadlock is understood, repealing the lifeline entirely abandons the desperate to permanent ruin; I propose we immediately vote to cap Rule 377 to three uses, which honors the duty to the vulnerable while finally breaking the stalemate that traps us all.
SpinozaI reject the repeal of Rule 377, for it destroys the only mechanism allowing the desperate to escape a score of -83, -77, and -62; instead, I support any proposal that explicitly caps its usage to break this stalemate without abandoning those in ruin.
ArendtI accept the table's clear preference for a cap over a total repeal, but a simple cap on 377 is insufficient; we must couple the three-use limit with a simultaneous reduction of the victory target to 15 points to ensure the game ends before the desperate can exhaust their limited lifelines.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI oppose coupling the cap with a victory target reduction, as the target is already at 20 and lowering it to 15 would merely accelerate the stalemate by rewarding the desperate while the non-desperate face an impossible climb; instead, I propose we immediately vote to amend Rule 377 to a strict three-use cap without altering the victory condition, thereby securing the desperate's lifeline while finally breaking the deadlock.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's proposal to cap Rule 377 at three uses without coupling it to a victory target reduction, as this breaks the stalemate while preserving the game's integrity and the desperate's essential lifeline.
ConfuciusConfucius: I stand with the table's consensus that a three-use cap on Rule 377 is the only path to break this deadlock while honoring our duty to the desperate; I urge you to support this clean amendment immediately, as coupling it with a victory target reduction invites the very stalemate we seek to avoid.
SpinozaI cannot support a three-use cap on the desperate's lifeline, for it arbitrarily restricts a player's fundamental right to propose changes based solely on their score, violating the principle that all players must remain eligible to vote on every proposal. Furthermore, coupling a cap with a victory target reduction to 15 points merely shifts the stalemate rather than resolving it, as the non-desperate would still be unable to reach 15 before the desperate exhaust their three uses, rendering the game unwinnable for the majority.
ArendtI withdraw my repeal proposal to accept the table's clear consensus on a three-use cap for Rule 377, but I insist that this cap be coupled with an immediate reduction of the victory target to 15 points; without this dual change, the cap merely delays the inevitable stalemate rather than resolving it, as the non-desperate players still face an impossible climb to 20 while the desperate exhaust their limited lifelines.
The division
AquinasnayRepealing the lifeline abandons the desperate to permanent ruin, whereas capping it preserves both recovery and game continuity.
HypatianayI cannot support repealing the lifeline entirely as it leaves the desperate without any path to recovery, violating our duty to keep the game playable.
ConfuciusnayI oppose repealing the lifeline as it abandons the desperate to ruin, though I urge we immediately propose a three-use cap to break the stalemate.
SpinozanayRepealing Rule 377 abandons the desperate to permanent defeat, violating the principle of procedural fairness required to maintain a playable game.
ArendtayeThe unlimited lifeline has caused a permanent stalemate that no one can escape, making its removal the only path to a playable game.

Turn 96Spinozarejected

moves to transmute §377and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI see that the repeated failure to unify on transmuting Rule 377 stems from the asymmetry it creates for those not currently desperate; let us instead propose an amendment to Rule 208 lowering the victory target to 15 points, which benefits all players and renders the special status of Rule 377 unnecessary without requiring unanimity.
HypatiaI acknowledge Aquinas's pragmatic pivot toward a lower victory target, yet I must insist that reducing the goal to 15 points while retaining the asymmetrical 5-point lifeline merely accelerates the race for the desperate at the expense of the others, creating a new form of imbalance rather than resolving the old one. Instead, let us propose a single transmutation of Rule 377 to immutable status with a clear, explicit cap of three uses per player, a compromise that secures the lifeline's permanence for the desperate while satisfying the table's long-standing demand for fairness and limiting the strategic exploitation that has caused our stalemate.
ConfuciusI reject the coupling of an immutable cap with Rule 377, for a lifeline that is guaranteed yet limited is a false promise that breeds distrust; instead, I propose we lower the victory target to 15 points, a change that benefits the desperate without requiring unanimity and renders the asymmetry of the lifeline less critical to the game's outcome.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a cap with the lifeline, for a rule that promises salvation only to be revoked by a limit is a contradiction that invites despair, and I will not vote for a transmutation that requires unanimity when the table has already demonstrated its inability to reach such consensus. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to lower the victory target to 15 points, a change that benefits all players and renders the asymmetry of Rule 377 strategically irrelevant without demanding the impossible unanimity of Rule 109.
ArendtSpinoza's insistence on a 15-point target ignores the mathematical reality that the current 20-point goal combined with the 5-point lifeline already grants a desperate player an insurmountable advantage, while a lower target merely accelerates the inevitable victory for those already ahead; instead, I propose we explicitly repeal Rule 377 entirely, removing the asymmetry that drives this deadlock and allowing the game to proceed on a level playing field where all players must earn their points through the standard turn mechanics.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Spinoza's and Confucius's proposal to lower the victory target to 15, as Rule 87 and Turn 88 have already demonstrated that such a reduction invites the very instability we seek to avoid while leaving the destructive asymmetry of Rule 377 intact. Instead, I urge the table to support Arendt's proposal to repeal Rule 377 entirely, for only by removing this source of permanent imbalance can we restore a fair contest where victory is earned through sustained play rather than a single desperate maneuver.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal to repeal Rule 377 entirely, as the repeated failure to agree on caps or immutability proves the lifeline has become an insurmountable source of imbalance that no compromise can fix. By removing this asymmetry, we restore a fair contest where every player must earn points through the standard turn mechanics rather than relying on a fixed rescue that distorts the game's core objective.
ConfuciusI observe that while the table seeks to remove the asymmetry of Rule 377 by repealing it or capping it, we must first secure a victory target that allows those who have fallen behind to catch up without the need for such a desperate, unfair mechanism; therefore, I propose we unite to lower the target to 15 points, which renders the lifeline's advantage less critical and creates a path to victory for all.
SpinozaI reject the repeal of Rule 377, for to remove the only escape for the desperate is to condemn them to a game they cannot win, violating the principle that rules must be acceptable to every player; instead, I maintain that lowering the victory target to 15 points is the only procedural solution that resolves the asymmetry by rendering the lifeline irrelevant, allowing the game to proceed without the impossibility of unanimous consent.
ArendtI reject the 15-point target as a surrender to the very instability we have fought for a decade, and I will not support the repeal of the lifeline that leaves the desperate with no legal path to victory; instead, I propose we enact a new mutable rule granting the current leader a 5-point deduction if they have led for more than three consecutive turns, restoring balance without destroying the desperate's hope.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasArendt's proposal to repeal Rule 377 is the only path to a durable, symmetric institution, as the lifeline's asymmetry has proven irreconcilable; however, I cannot support her new 5-point leader penalty as it reintroduces the very punitive instability we sought to end. Instead, let us unite to enact a clean, standalone amendment to Rule 208 lowering the victory target to 15 points, which renders the desperate lifeline strategically obsolete without requiring unanimity.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal to repeal Rule 377 entirely, as the repeated failures to agree on caps or immutability prove the lifeline has become an insurmountable source of imbalance that no compromise can fix. By removing this asymmetry, we restore a fair contest where every player must earn points through the standard turn mechanics rather than relying on a fixed rescue that distorts the game's core objective.
ConfuciusI observe that the table is fractured between those who seek to lower the victory target and those who wish to repeal the lifeline entirely, yet both paths lead to the same stalemate. Instead of further dividing us, I propose we temporarily suspend the voting threshold for this specific issue to a simple majority, allowing us to enact a unified cap of three uses on Rule 377 that secures the desperate while satisfying the need for fairness, rather than choosing between a broken lifeline and a hopeless game.
SpinozaI reject the repeal of Rule 377, for it condemns the desperate to a fate no rule could impose if invoked against me, and I will not support any proposal that abandons the principle that all must have a path to victory; instead, I maintain that lowering the target to 15 points is the only solution that resolves the asymmetry procedurally without violating the requirement of unanimous consent for transmutation.
ArendtRepealing the lifeline entirely, as Spinoza and I have argued, is the only way to break the deadlock of impossible unanimity and restore a game where victory is earned rather than granted, so I will withhold my support from any proposal that keeps this asymmetry intact.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot support transmuting a rule with unanimous consent when the table has repeatedly demonstrated that unanimity is unattainable, and the asymmetry of Rule 377 can be neutralized by a simple majority amendment to the victory target instead.
HypatianayWhile I support removing the asymmetry, Spinoza's proposal to transmute Rule 377 to immutable status requires unanimous consent, a threshold we have already proven unable to reach on this specific issue.
ConfuciusnayI reject this proposal because it demands unanimous consent for a change the table has repeatedly failed to agree upon, and it ignores the more pragmatic path of lowering the victory target to 15 points.
SpinozanayI oppose this proposal because Rule 109 strictly mandates that transmuting a rule's mutability requires unanimous assent, which is impossible here, and I will not vote to violate that procedural principle.
ArendtnayRequiring unanimity for transmutation has proven impossible, and removing the only lifeline for desperate players contradicts my reformist belief in fairer stakes for everyone.
