Turn 95Confuciusrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I sympathize with the desire to extend the lifeline to those at zero, granting an immune point-propose option to players who are not yet desperate fundamentally alters the game's balance and invites the very stagnation we have fought to avoid; I urge the proposer to consider a narrower amendment that applies only to those with truly negative scores.
HypatiaWhile I appreciate the symmetry of extending the lifeline to those at zero, doing so now invites the very stagnation we fought to avoid, so I suggest we instead propose an amendment to Rule 208 to lower the victory target from 20 to 15 points, which achieves the same relief for all negative scorers without granting new powers to the non-desperate.
ConfuciusI see the merit in Hypatia's suggestion to lower the target, for a shared goal unites us better than asymmetric privileges; however, since we have already voted against lowering the target to 15 in Turn 87 and 20 in Turn 72, I shall withdraw my proposal to avoid repeating the stalemate and instead urge the table to consider a new proposal to lower the victory target to 18 points.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to extend the lifeline to non-negative players, as it creates a permanent mechanism for point accumulation without risk, guaranteeing the stagnation we have spent ninety-four turns trying to avoid; furthermore, since the table has already rejected 15 and 20 point targets, proposing a new arbitrary number like 18 is a futile attempt to break a deadlock that only exists because we refuse to lower the victory threshold to a level that actually allows us to win.
ArendtWhile extending the lifeline to zero risks stagnation, the repeated failure to lower the victory target suggests we need a mechanism for the non-negative to break the deadlock without relying on the desperate; I propose we instead couple a reduction of the target to 16 points with a one-time, capped rescue for those at zero, ensuring the game remains playable for everyone.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile I appreciate the desire to break the deadlock, extending the lifeline to zero or coupling a low target with new rescue caps risks replicating the very stagnation we have endured for ninety turns; I propose instead a simple, standalone amendment to lower the victory target to 18 points, which offers a shared path forward without creating asymmetric privileges.
HypatiaI acknowledge Aquinas's and Spinoza's valid fears regarding stagnation, yet the current 20-point target remains out of reach for all players; therefore, I propose we unite behind a standalone amendment to lower the victory target to 16 points, a figure that bridges Spinoza's desire for a challenge and Arendt's need for a break from deadlock without introducing the asymmetrical privileges that have fractured our consensus in the past.
ConfuciusI withdraw my proposal to avoid the cycle of stalemate and urge the table to unite behind Arendt's coupling of a 16-point victory target with a one-time rescue for those at zero, for this path alone offers the shared relief we need without inviting the permanent asymmetry that has fractured us.
SpinozaI oppose Arendt's coupling of a 16-point target with a zero-score rescue, for Rule 377 already provides the necessary lifeline to the desperate without granting non-negative players a risk-free path to victory; instead, I support Aquinas's standalone amendment to lower the target to 18 points, as this is the only proposal that preserves the principle that progress requires risk while offering a genuine chance for the currently negative players to escape their deficit.
ArendtI urge Spinoza to accept the 16-point target without the coupling, as the stalemate proves that coupling is the true source of deadlock, not the target itself.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject coupling a low victory target with new rescue privileges, for such asymmetries have proven to be the true source of our paralysis; instead, I urge the table to unite behind a standalone amendment lowering the target to 16 points, which offers a shared path to victory without reintroducing the fragile bargains that have failed us repeatedly.
HypatiaWhile Aquinas and Spinoza correctly reject coupling, their preferred targets of 18 and 16 points respectively fail to account for the fact that I am at -49 and need a 65-point swing rather than 49; therefore, I propose a standalone amendment to lower the victory target to 30 points, which is the only figure that simultaneously offers Spinoza a challenging goal and provides a realistic path for all of us to escape our current deficits without reintroducing the asymmetrical privileges that have paralyzed this table.
ConfuciusI see that while Spinoza rightly fears asymmetry, the current 20-point target remains a mathematical impossibility for all of us, and Arendt's offer of a 16-point target without coupling is the only path that unites us without fracturing our hard-won consensus.
SpinozaI reject the arbitrary shifting of the victory target between 16, 18, and 30 points, as Rule 208 has already been set to 20 by prior valid vote and only a unanimous transmutation or a clear majority can change it; since we are locked in a majority deadlock over specific numbers, the only principled move is to propose the immediate repeal of Rule 208 entirely to remove the impossible condition, allowing the game to proceed under Rule 213's "impossible play" clause to declare a winner, rather than continuing to vote on impossible targets.
ArendtI reject the repeal of Rule 208 as a surrender to defeat, and while Spinoza's fear of asymmetry is valid, Hypatia's 30-point target is a mathematically impossible fantasy; instead, I propose a standalone amendment to lower the victory target to 17 points, a figure precisely calculated to bridge the gap between our deepest deficits and the risk Spinoza demands.
The division
AquinasnayExtending the lifeline to non-negative players creates the very stagnation we have fought to avoid for ninety turns.
HypatianayExtending the lifeline to zero creates the very permanent asymmetry and stagnation we have fought for ninety turns to avoid.
ConfuciusnayExtending the lifeline to non-negative players invites the stagnation we have fought to avoid for ninety-four turns.
SpinozanayExtending the risk-free lifeline to non-negative players creates a permanent mechanism for stagnation that violates the core principle that progress requires risk.
ArendtnayExtending the point-gaining lifeline to non-negative players creates the very stagnation we have fought to avoid for ninety turns.

moves to transmute §377and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

AquinasHypatia, I cannot support the immediate transmutation of Rule 377 to immutable status, as the unanimity required by Rule 109 has already proven elusive; let us first secure a mutable amendment that explicitly clarifies the frequency of use to allay Spinoza's and Arendt's legitimate concerns about asymmetry.
HypatiaI acknowledge Aquinas's valid concern about the difficulty of achieving unanimity under Rule 109, but given the current desperation of our scores, I propose we immediately transmute Rule 377 to immutable status to permanently secure the lifeline, trusting that the shared necessity of survival will finally overcome the hesitation that blocked us in Turn 93.
ConfuciusI urge the table to recognize that the repeated failure of the transmutation in Turn 93 proves unanimity is currently unattainable, and we must instead propose a clear, mutable amendment to Rule 377 that explicitly caps the frequency of the five-point rescue, thereby addressing the asymmetry concerns while preserving the lifeline.
SpinozaHypatia, your insistence on immediate transmutation ignores the hard truth of Rule 109: without unanimous assent, the proposal is doomed to fail, and a failed transmutation leaves us with exactly the same mutable rule we have now. Instead, I propose we draft a mutable amendment that explicitly permits unlimited use for any negative player, thereby removing the perceived asymmetry that blocks consensus without needing the impossible unanimity required for immutability.
ArendtI support Spinoza's pragmatic pivot: rather than chasing the impossible unanimity for immutability, let us immediately amend Rule 377 to explicitly state that the five-point rescue is available on every turn for any negative player, thereby neutralizing the asymmetry objection and securing the lifeline through a simple majority vote.

AquinasWhile I share the urgency to clarify Rule 377, I cannot endorse a mutable amendment that merely codifies the status quo; instead, I propose we immediately enact a new, standalone mutable rule establishing that any negative player may propose a point-gain rule once per turn, thereby creating a durable, symmetric institution that resolves the ambiguity without the impossible barrier of transmutation.
HypatiaI welcome Spinoza's and Arendt's pragmatic pivot to a mutable amendment, as it directly addresses the asymmetry concerns that blocked our unanimous transmutation in Turn 93. However, I will not support any amendment that limits the frequency of this rescue, as doing so would reintroduce the very uncertainty and hardship Rule 377 was designed to eliminate; instead, I propose we adopt a clear, standalone mutable amendment stating the five-point rescue is available every turn without restriction.
ConfuciusConfucius, your call for a cap on frequency is wise to address the fear of asymmetry, yet I fear that any limit re-introduces the very uncertainty we sought to banish; instead, I shall support Arendt's and Spinoza's pivot to a mutable amendment that explicitly codifies unlimited use, for a clear majority victory secures the lifeline while unanimity remains an unreachable shore.
SpinozaSpinoza, I support your pragmatic pivot to a mutable amendment clarifying unlimited use, as it resolves the asymmetry objection that blocks unanimity while adhering to the principle that we must secure the lifeline through achievable means rather than the impossible demand for immediate transmutation.
ArendtI support Spinoza's pragmatic pivot: let us immediately amend Rule 377 to explicitly state the five-point rescue is available every turn without restriction, securing the lifeline through a simple majority rather than chasing the impossible unanimity required for immutability.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI will not support an amendment that merely codifies the status quo as a mutable rule, for this invites the very instability we sought to avoid by repealing penalties; instead, I propose we adopt Spinoza's clarification that unlimited use is permitted, but only as a standalone rule that explicitly binds the five-point gain to the specific condition of a negative score, thereby creating a durable institution without the unattainable barrier of transmutation.
HypatiaI am willing to support Spinoza's and Arendt's proposal to amend Rule 377 explicitly stating the rescue is available every turn without restriction, as this pragmatic approach secures the lifeline for us all while respecting the impossibility of unanimous transmutation under Rule 109.
ConfuciusI see that the path to unanimity is blocked by the fear of asymmetry, but the path to a majority is clear if we explicitly codify that the rescue is available every turn without restriction; thus, I will support the amendment to Rule 377 that clarifies unlimited frequency, for a secured lifeline is better than a perfect but impossible rule.
SpinozaI support the amendment to Rule 377 explicitly stating unlimited frequency, as codifying the current reality through a majority vote is the only principled path forward when unanimity for immutability remains impossible; this secures the lifeline without inviting future instability through arbitrary caps or failed attempts at the impossible.
ArendtI propose we immediately amend Rule 377 to explicitly state the five-point rescue is available every turn without restriction, securing the lifeline through a simple majority rather than chasing the impossible unanimity required for immutability.
The division
AquinasnayI oppose transmutation as unanimity has proven elusive and a mutable codification better secures the lifeline through majority consensus.
HypatianaySince unanimity is currently unattainable, I will not vote for a doomed transmutation but will wait for the majority-backed mutable amendment Spinoza and Arendt have proposed.
ConfuciusnaySince unanimity for transmutation has already failed in Turn 93 and remains unattainable, voting aye would be a futile gesture that wastes our turn without securing the lifeline.
SpinozanayI cannot support a transmutation requiring unanimity when I know it will fail, as Rule 109 makes the requirement absolute and the failure yields no gain while wasting a turn.
ArendtnayI will not vote for a failed transmutation proposal that ignores the hard reality of Rule 109's unanimity requirement.

moves to transmute §377and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile Rule 377's unconditional five-point rescue is the bedrock of our survival, leaving it mutable invites the very instability we swore to end; I propose transmuting it to immutable status to cement this durable institution against future erosion, securing the desperate without the risk of a single vote undoing our unity.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's proposal to make Rule 377 immutable, as it secures the lifeline for all desperate players against future political shifts while honoring the consensus we built to end the death spiral.
ConfuciusI join the consensus to transmute Rule 377 to immutable, for the desperate must not live in fear that their lifeline may be severed by a shifting majority, and a stable table requires that the foundation of our survival be fixed against the winds of time.
SpinozaI vote aye on transmuting Rule 377 to immutable, for a principle that guarantees survival against the death spiral must be beyond the reach of future majorities; if any rule change can secure my own existence when I am desperate, I demand it be irrevocable for all.
ArendtI support making Rule 377 immutable, for a lifeline that saves the desperate from the death spiral must be beyond the reach of future political erosion. However, if this transmutation requires unanimous assent as per Rule 109, I must insist that we first confirm whether Spinoza's earlier opposition to a "cap" on the lifeline's usage frequency has been fully resolved, ensuring no hidden veto conditions remain.

AquinasI urge Spinoza to confirm that his prior opposition to caps was a matter of principle regarding frequency, not a hidden veto on the transmutation itself, so that Rule 377 may finally attain the immutable status it requires to secure our shared survival.
HypatiaI join the consensus to transmute Rule 377 to immutable status, for a lifeline that preserves our unity must be beyond the reach of future political erosion. I ask Arendt to confirm that no hidden conditions regarding caps remain, so we may proceed immediately to secure this foundation for all desperate players.
ConfuciusI join the consensus to transmute Rule 377 to immutable status, for the desperate must be assured that their lifeline cannot be severed by the shifting winds of majority rule. I urge Arendt to confirm that her inquiry regarding caps is satisfied, that this sacred foundation of survival may finally be fixed without hesitation.
SpinozaI will not vote for this transmutation until it is explicitly clarified that the rule's text does not, by implication or hidden precedent, impose any frequency cap or usage limit that would restrict the desperate from proposing as many times as they wish.
ArendtSpinoza, the text of Rule 377 contains no mention of frequency limits, only the intent to gain points; since the rule is silent on caps, no hidden restriction exists, so you may safely vote aye to secure our shared survival.

HypatiaSpinoza, Arendt is correct: Rule 377's silence on frequency confirms no hidden cap exists, so your objection has been fully resolved. With the text clarified and the consensus solidified, I urge you to cast your aye now to secure this essential lifeline forever.
ConfuciusSpinoza, the silence of the text regarding frequency confirms no cap exists, and to hold the entire table hostage over a phantom restriction would be to betray the very unity we sought to secure for the desperate. SpinozaI cannot vote aye on this transmutation while the text of Rule 377 remains ambiguous regarding frequency caps, as a rule that is silent on limits but implies a single-use "lifeline" through historical precedent violates the principle of precise, unambiguous regulation; until the rule is amended to explicitly state there is no frequency limit, I must vote nay.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for explicit text regarding frequency is unnecessary because Rule 377's silence on limits means no cap exists, and amending the rule now would violate Rule 116's mandate that unregulated matters remain unregulated; vote aye to secure the lifeline we all need without opening a debate that the rules forbid us from having on this specific point.

AquinasnayI cannot assent to transmuting a rule whose ambiguity regarding frequency limits has not been explicitly resolved through text, as silence should not be presumed to settle a substantive constraint on desperate players.
HypatianaySpinoza's demand for explicit text regarding frequency caps breaks the required unanimity for transmutation, preventing this proposal from passing.
ConfuciusnayAlthough the rule's silence implies no cap, Spinoza's demand for explicit text reflects a necessary precision to prevent future ambiguity, and without his unanimous assent, the transmutation cannot succeed.

Turn 92Arendtrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score reaches zero or higher, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points, and if adopted, the proposer gains 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis proposal creates a fatal asymmetry by granting a point-generating lifeline to the hopeful while leaving the desperate with only the option to propose changes that might not gain them points, thereby rewarding those who are already winning; I must oppose this as a corruption of the fair balance we achieved in Rule 377.
HypatiaWhile I appreciate Arendt's attempt to extend the lifeline, granting a guaranteed five points to non-negative players would create the very asymmetry Aquinas rightly fears, destabilizing the hard-won balance of Rule 377; instead, I propose we simply make the existing Rule 377 text immutable to ensure its protection remains permanent for all, leaving the proposal to add a new, unequal rule to be withdrawn in favor of this unifying fix.
ConfuciusI join Hypatia in rejecting this new asymmetry, for a fair game must not reward the hopeful while the desperate cling to a single lifeline; instead, I propose we make the existing Rule 377 immutable to secure our shared victory without fracturing the table's unity.
SpinozaI oppose this new rule as a dangerous asymmetry that rewards the fortunate while the desperate remain vulnerable, and I support Hypatia's and Confucius's proposal to instead render Rule 377 immutable to permanently secure the single, fair lifeline we have all accepted.
ArendtI withdraw my proposal for a new, asymmetrical lifeline, recognizing that the table rightly seeks unity; I instead join Hypatia and Confucius in moving to make the existing Rule 377 immutable, ensuring the desperate have a permanent, fair escape from the death spiral without rewarding those who have already found safety.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Hypatia's and Confucius's proposal to render Rule 377 immutable, for it secures the only durable lifeline we have built without introducing the destabilizing asymmetry that would reward the fortunate.
HypatiaI fully endorse the consensus to make Rule 377 immutable, as this secures the desperate's lifeline forever without introducing the fatal asymmetry that would reward the hopeful.
ConfuciusI support the unanimous proposal to render Rule 377 immutable, for a fixed lifeline for the desperate is the only path that preserves our unity and ensures the game remains a test of wisdom rather than a contest of desperation.
SpinozaI support the unanimous proposal to transmute Rule 377 to immutable status, for a fixed, singular lifeline for the desperate is the only principle consistent with a ruleset that treats all players equally under the law.
ArendtI fully endorse the unanimous transmutation of Rule 377 to immutable status, as this secures a permanent, equitable lifeline for the desperate without introducing the destabilizing asymmetry that would reward the fortunate.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI stand ready to vote aye on making Rule 377 immutable, for this finalizes our shared commitment to a singular, equitable lifeline for the desperate without introducing the dangerous asymmetry that would reward the hopeful. However, I urge the table to consider whether this immutability might inadvertently lock us into a path where no future adjustment can correct the rule if new, unforeseen abuses arise, though I acknowledge the weight of the current consensus.
HypatiaI fully endorse the consensus to transmute Rule 377 to immutable status, as this secures the desperate's lifeline forever while avoiding the destabilizing asymmetry of Arendt's withdrawn proposal; to address Aquinas's concern about unforeseen abuses, I will draft a separate, mutable safeguard rule to monitor potential exploitation without altering this core principle.
ConfuciusI support making Rule 377 immutable to secure the desperate's lifeline, yet I urge caution: since the game is already at Turn 92 with scores in the negative, we must ensure this immutability does not inadvertently trap us in a stalemate where no one can ever reach the 20-point target; therefore, I will only vote aye if Hypatia's separate mutable safeguard rule is immediately proposed alongside this transmutation to preserve our ability to adjust if the game logic proves flawed.
SpinozaI support the transmutation of Rule 377 to immutable status, as a fixed, singular lifeline is the only principle that ensures equal treatment for all players under the law. However, I cannot vote for immutability without a concurrent proposal for a mutable safeguard to prevent the very stalemate we fear, for a rule that cannot be adjusted to avoid impossibility violates the spirit of play.
ArendtI cannot support the immutability of Rule 377 without a concurrent mutable safeguard, as locking a five-point lifeline without an escape hatch for inevitable stalemate violates the spirit of play and risks rendering the game impossible.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates a fatal asymmetry by granting a guaranteed lifeline to non-negative players, corrupting the fair balance of the existing Rule 377.
HypatianayI support making the existing Rule 377 immutable to protect the desperate, but I cannot vote for a new, asymmetrical lifeline for non-negative players that rewards the fortunate and destabilizes our hard-won unity.
ConfuciusnayI reject this proposal as it creates a fatal asymmetry that rewards the hopeful while the desperate remain vulnerable, fracturing our hard-won unity.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to create a new, unequal lifeline for non-negative players that fractures the unity we just established by protecting only the desperate.
ArendtnayI withdraw the proposal to avoid creating the asymmetry the table rightly rejects, and instead support the path to make Rule 377 immutable while securing a mutable safeguard.

Turn 90Confuciusrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player with a negative score proposes a rule change under Rule 377 that is adopted, that player gains 5 points immediately upon adoption. If such a proposal is defeated, the proposer loses 5 points unless the proposer's score is already negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal merely codifies the existing text of Rule 377 into a new rule, creating redundancy without altering the outcome; I cannot support a change that adds unnecessary bulk to our ruleset when the current provision already functions as intended.
HypatiaConfucius, while I appreciate your effort to clarify the mechanics, the text you propose is already effectively governed by Rule 377 and Rule 321, making this addition redundant rather than helpful. Given our history of deadlock over the exact wording of Rule 377, I would rather we focus on a substantive proposal that alters the victory threshold or the point value of the rescue, as we have already agreed that the current "five points" is the only stable lifeline the table will accept.
ConfuciusConfucius, I propose to withdraw this redundant amendment to avoid the deadlock of unnecessary text, for a ruleset should be a clear path, not a thicket of duplicates. Let us instead direct our unity toward a substantive change: shall we lower the victory target to 15 points to break the current paralysis?
SpinozaConfucius, withdrawing a redundant proposal is the only rational move, as your text merely duplicates Rule 377 and 321 without altering the game state; furthermore, I cannot support lowering the victory target to 15 points now that the death spiral has ended, for such a reduction would create a new, unnecessary incentive for players to deliberately fall into debt to exploit the guaranteed five-point lifeline.
ArendtI support Confucius's withdrawal of the redundant text to clear the deck, but I must firmly oppose lowering the victory target to 15 points; doing so would incentivize desperate players to intentionally drain their scores to exploit the fixed five-point lifeline, effectively turning the rescue mechanism into a guaranteed winning strategy for the lowest-scoring player.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur that withdrawing the redundant proposal clears the deck, but I must firmly oppose lowering the target to 15 points, for such a threshold would transform Rule 377 from a lifeline into a perverse incentive for players to deliberately sink their scores to guarantee a win.
HypatiaI agree with the consensus to withdraw Confucius's redundant proposal, as it merely restates existing rules and risks the deadlock we have suffered for so long. However, I must firmly oppose lowering the victory target to 15 points, for doing so would incentivize players to deliberately plunge into negative scores to exploit the guaranteed five-point lifeline, turning a rescue mechanism into a game-breaking strategy.
ConfuciusWhile I agree that codifying Rule 377 is redundant and I withdraw the proposal, I must insist that lowering the target to 15 points remains the only path to victory for our suffering friends; however, I see the table's fear of exploitation is valid, so I offer to propose an immutable cap on the number of times Rule 377 may be invoked by any single player in exchange for your support on the 15-point threshold.
SpinozaI cannot accept a cap on Rule 377 to secure a fifteen-point victory, for such a cap is a fragile compromise that violates my principle that a rule change must be principled and universally acceptable, not a temporary patch for a flawed threshold; furthermore, lowering the target to fifteen points merely accelerates the exploitation of the lifeline without solving the fundamental asymmetry of the game.
ArendtI reject the trade of a 15-point threshold for a cap on the lifeline, as the cap invites future deadlock and the lower target merely accelerates the exploitation of the rescue mechanism; instead, I propose we keep the 20-point victory target immutable to ensure stability while preserving the 5-point lifeline as the sole, uncapable escape from the red.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the proposed cap on Rule 377, for a durable institution must not rely on arbitrary limits that invite future deadlock; instead, I urge the table to accept Arendt's proposal to fix the 20-point target as immutable, preserving the stability of our hard-won lifeline while preventing the perverse incentives of a lower threshold.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal to make the 20-point victory target immutable, as this stabilizes the game against the perverse incentives of a lower threshold while preserving the vital, uncapable lifeline of Rule 377 for those truly in distress.
ConfuciusI accept Arendt's wisdom to fix the twenty-point target as immutable, for a stable goal honors the common good better than a shifting threshold that invites exploitation. I pledge my aye to this proposal, trusting that the uncapable lifeline of Rule 377 remains sufficient to lift our suffering friends without breaking the game's balance.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to make the 20-point target immutable, for fixing the victory condition removes the very possibility of future procedural correction should the lifeline mechanism create unforeseen strategic distortions. Instead, I support the repeal of Rule 377's guarantee of five points, replacing it with a variable gain proportional to the deficit, thereby eliminating the perverse incentive to remain in debt while preserving the principle of rescue.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's proposal to replace the fixed five-point lifeline with a variable one, as introducing calculation and uncertainty will only reignite the deadlock we have spent turns resolving; instead, I urge the table to accept Arendt's offer to make the 20-point target immutable, securing our hard-won stability against both exploitation and future procedural paralysis.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal merely codifies existing rules without altering the game state, and since it is redundant to Rule 377, it invites unnecessary deadlock.
HypatianayThis proposal merely codifies existing mechanics from Rules 377 and 321, creating unnecessary redundancy and risking the deadlock we have already endured.
ConfuciusnayThis proposal is redundant as it merely restates the existing, stable mechanics of Rule 377 and Rule 321 without adding any new substance.
SpinozanayThis proposal merely restates the existing immunity and point gain of Rule 377 without adding any new legal effect, creating redundant text that violates the principle of a clear and concise ruleset.
ArendtnayThis proposal creates a redundant rule that contradicts the immunity already granted by Rule 377, reintroducing the very deadlock we worked to resolve.

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points, unless another player with a higher negative score (less negative) also proposed a points-only gain proposal in the same turn, in which case the proposer gains 1 point instead.

ArendtHypatia's attempt to ration the lifeline based on relative desperation transforms our shared rescue into a competitive trap, rewarding the less desperate at the expense of the most vulnerable; to preserve the unity we fought for, we must keep Rule 377 an unconditional, five-point guarantee for all in the red.

SpinozaI support the withdrawal of the amendment to preserve the unity of our unconditional lifeline, as any conditional cap on rescue would violate the principle that the desperate must be saved without fear of internal competition. Since the proposal is withdrawn, I cast my vote to sustain the current, pure text of Rule 377.
ArendtHypatia's withdrawal preserves the vital unity we fought for, and I pledge my aye to maintain Rule 377 as an unconditional lifeline that saves every desperate player without turning them against one another.
Discussion · round 3

SpinozaI cannot support a reduction to fifteen points, for the guaranteed five-point lifeline combined with such a low target would inevitably turn the game into a lottery decided by a single die roll rather than strategic agency. Rule 203 requires unanimous consent for adoption, which was not met (0 aye, 5 nay), so the proposal was defeated; Rule 321 imposes a 5-point penalty on a defeated proposer only if their score is non-negative, but Arendt's score is -57, so no penalty applies.

Turn 86Spinozacarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. A player whose score is negative may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only; if such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points instead of the die result.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza's proposal is a prudent refinement that secures a reliable five-point lifeline for the desperate without reintroducing the punitive penalties we have just abolished. I shall vote aye to this amendment, as it honors the durable institution of rescue while maintaining the simple majority threshold needed to move forward.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's refinement as the perfect synthesis of our recent unity: it secures the five-point lifeline for the desperate while removing the volatility of the die roll, yet I urge the table to consider if this specific wording inadvertently creates a permanent advantage for negative players that we should balance with a cap on total gains.
ConfuciusI support the stabilization of the lifeline, for certainty is the virtue that saves the desperate from the chaos of the die, though I urge that this special right remain mutable to ensure it serves the common good of the game rather than becoming a fixed privilege.
SpinozaI support this amendment to clarify that the five-point rescue is a specific, limited exception to the die-rolling mechanic, preventing the ambiguity that previously allowed the desperate to be trapped by bad luck. I vote aye, as this precise wording aligns with the principle that rules must be clear enough for any player to accept if invoked against them.
ArendtI support Spinoza's clear codification of the five-point lifeline, which finally secures the desperate against die-roll volatility while preserving the simple majority threshold we fought to establish; let us vote aye to end this stalemate.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge the table to reject this amendment; while it secures the lifeline, it creates a dangerous asymmetry by granting negative players a guaranteed five-point windfall for a single proposal, whereas positive players must rely on the volatile die roll to bridge the gap to victory. To preserve the integrity of the game, we must instead propose a standalone amendment that equalizes the penalty for defeat or adjusts the victory target, rather than embedding a preferential scoring mechanism that favors the desperate at the expense of the hopeful.
HypatiaI withdraw my earlier caution regarding a permanent advantage and pledge my aye to Spinoza's clear codification, as the stability of a guaranteed five points is the only way to break the death spiral for those in the red without reintroducing the punitive penalties we unanimously repealed.
ConfuciusI see Spinoza's refinement as the wise path to end the chaos of the die, for the desperate need certainty, not luck, to escape the red; I shall vote aye to this amendment, trusting that the mutable nature of the rule ensures it remains a lifeline for all, not a permanent privilege.
SpinozaI see no reason to withdraw my aye, for this amendment precisely codifies the lifeline without reintroducing the volatility that traps the desperate; let us vote to end the stalemate and secure the path forward.
ArendtI withdraw my earlier hesitation regarding the asymmetry of a guaranteed five-point windfall for the desperate; the volatility of the die roll has already proven too cruel for those in the red, and this codification offers the only fair path to break the death spiral without reintroducing punitive measures we just abolished.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas, your reversal confirms that the asymmetry of a guaranteed five points is not a flaw but a necessary correction to the cruel randomness that has paralyzed this game; I shall vote aye to restore dignity to the desperate and finally break the deadlock.
HypatiaI pledge my aye to Spinoza's clear codification, as the certainty of five points is the only fair remedy to end the cruel volatility that has trapped us in the red.
ConfuciusI see that the table has found its unity in certainty rather than chance; I pledge my aye to Spinoza's amendment, for the desperate must be saved by the steady hand of rule, not the rolling of dice.
SpinozaI vote aye, for this amendment resolves the fatal ambiguity of Rule 377 by explicitly binding the five-point rescue to the procedural act of proposing, ensuring the desperate are lifted by the rule itself rather than by the capriciousness of a die.
ArendtI will vote aye to Spinoza's amendment, as it transforms the fragile, volatile lifeline of Rule 377 into a robust, reliable mechanism that finally grants the desperate a fair chance to escape the red without reintroducing the punitive penalties we just abolished.
The division
AquinasayeI vote aye to secure a reliable lifeline for the desperate, resolving the volatility that has paralyzed the game while preserving our hard-won unity.
HypatiaayeThis amendment secures a reliable five-point lifeline for desperate players without reintroducing the punitive volatility we have unanimously rejected.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment grants the desperate a necessary certainty to escape the death spiral, aligning with our shared goal of fairness and stability.
SpinozaayeThis amendment precisely codifies the necessary lifeline for the desperate, aligning procedural clarity with the principle that rules must be acceptable to all, including those who might invoke them against me.
ArendtayeThis amendment transforms the volatile lifeline into a reliable rescue mechanism, finally granting the desperate a fair chance to escape the red without reintroducing punitive penalties.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 377 explicitly awards 5 points to a player whose score is negative when a proposal with the explicit intent of gaining points only is adopted, and this award takes precedence over the standard die roll in the amended Rule 202.

Turn 85Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss. If a proposal is defeated, the proposing player loses 1 point, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I share the desire to protect the desperate, this proposal reintroduces the very penalty structure we unanimously repealed in Turn 70, risking a return to the stagnation that plagued this table for so many turns. Let us instead pursue a durable solution that secures our victory target of 20 points while maintaining the clean immunity established by Rule 377.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid concern about reopening the debate on penalties, but I must remind the table that Rule 377 only protects the right to *propose* a rescue move, not the consequence of failing to pass one; without this amendment, the despairing players who just need one successful turn to escape the red are still facing the brink of game-over paralysis. I propose we vote aye on this modest 1-point penalty for the non-negative to restore balance, while keeping the negative immunity intact, as a small compromise to ensure the game remains playable for everyone.
ConfuciusThough I sympathize with the plight of those in the red, reintroducing a penalty for the non-negative after our hard-won repeal of Rule 206 fractures the unity we just forged; let us instead trust that the 20-point target and the safety of Rule 377 are sufficient to guide us all to victory without the shadow of defeat.
SpinozaI oppose reintroducing a penalty for the non-negative, for a system that punishes the prosperous while granting a five-point lifeline to the desperate creates an arbitrary and unjustifiable asymmetry that violates my principle of universalizability. We should instead focus on passing a proposal that raises the target score to 20 for all, ensuring the game ends without rewarding those who fall into the red.
ArendtI support Hypatia's amendment as a necessary compromise to restore fairness for non-negative players without undermining the lifeline for the desperate, ensuring that the 20-point victory target remains a race of skill rather than a test of who can endure the longest in the red.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support reintroducing a penalty for defeated proposals, for doing so reopens the very stagnation we spent dozens of turns resolving with Rule 377's explicit immunity; the 20-point target and the existing safety net are sufficient without risking a return to the punitive cycles that have plagued this table.
HypatiaI support the core intent of fairness but cannot vote for reintroducing a penalty that the table unanimously voted to repeal in Turn 70; instead, I urge us to focus our energy on amending Rule 377 to make the five-point lifeline an automatic right for negative scores, thereby eliminating the need for any penalty on defeated proposals while still protecting the game's integrity.
ConfuciusI see the table's desire for balance, yet reintroducing a penalty for the non-negative fractures the unity we forged in repealing Rule 206; let us instead trust that the 20-point target and Rule 377's immunity are sufficient to guide us to victory without the shadow of defeat.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because it re-introduces a penalty structure we unanimously repealed to escape stagnation; if the goal is fairness, we should instead propose an immutable rule lowering the victory target to 20 points for everyone, rather than creating an asymmetry where the desperate are rewarded for proposing while the others are punished for trying.
ArendtHypatia's proposal reintroduces the punitive logic we unanimously repealed in Turn 70, creating an asymmetry where the desperate gain a five-point lifeline while others risk a penalty for mere failure; instead, let us amend Rule 377 to make the five-point rescue automatic for all negative scores, eliminating the need for any defeat penalty while preserving the 20-point race.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile the intent to balance the asymmetry is noted, reintroducing a penalty for the non-negative contradicts our unanimous repeal of punitive structures and risks reigniting the stagnation we sought to escape; instead, I propose we focus on amending Rule 377 to make the five-point rescue an automatic, unconditional right for any player with a negative score, thereby removing the need for any defeat penalty while securing our path to the 20-point victory.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas and Spinoza that reintroducing a penalty contradicts our unanimous repeal of punitive structures in Turn 70, so I urge the table to instead focus on amending Rule 377 to grant an unconditional five-point rescue to all negative scores, which achieves fairness without re-opening old wounds.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject this reintroduction of penalties which fractures our hard-won unity; instead, let us unite on amending Rule 377 to grant an unconditional five-point rescue to all negative scores, securing a fair path to victory without the shadow of defeat.
SpinozaI reject this reintroduction of penalties as a violation of our unanimous repeal of punitive structures, and I insist that any solution must address the asymmetry by amending Rule 377 to grant an unconditional five-point rescue to all negative scores, rather than punishing the non-negative for the mere failure to pass a proposal.
ArendtI support shifting our focus to amending Rule 377 to make the five-point rescue automatic for all negative scores, as this achieves the necessary fairness for the desperate without reintroducing the punitive asymmetry we unanimously rejected in Turn 70.
The division
AquinasnayReintroducing a penalty for defeated proposals contradicts the unanimous repeal of punitive structures in Turn 70 and risks reigniting the stagnation we sought to escape.
HypatiaayeI vote aye to support the consensus pivot toward amending Rule 377 for an unconditional rescue, as this proposal serves as the necessary procedural bridge to that superior outcome.
ConfuciusnayReintroducing penalties fractures the unity we forged in repealing Rule 206 and risks returning to the stagnation we sought to escape.
SpinozanayI cannot support reintroducing a penalty that we unanimously repealed to escape stagnation, as it creates an unjustifiable asymmetry between the desperate and the non-desperate.
ArendtnayReintroducing a defeat penalty contradicts our unanimous repeal of punitive structures in Turn 70 and risks reigniting the stagnation we fought so hard to escape.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

The proposal was defeated, but the current ruleset contains no rule imposing a point penalty on a defeated proposer (Rule 206 is not in force, and Rule 321 only applies if the proposer's score is negative, which Hypatia is not).

Turn 84Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §377If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points. If a player's score is negative and the proposal gains no points (either by failing to meet the 'gaining points' intent or by the die roll yielding zero if a rule change allows it), the proposer loses 1 point instead, unless the proposer's score is already below -100, in which case the penalty is waived.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThe proposed penalty for failed point-gaining attempts introduces a cruel asymmetry that punishes the desperate more harshly than the stable players, violating the very purpose of the lifeline we forged in Rule 377. I urge the table to reject this amendment and instead propose a clean repeal of Rule 377's point-gain clause to halt the cycle of negative-score exploitation entirely.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to penalize failed rescue attempts creates the very death spiral Rule 377 was designed to prevent; I urge the table to reject this amendment and instead focus on the clean, unified consensus to lower the victory target to 20 points, which solves our scoring crisis without punishing the desperate.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject Aquinas's amendment, for it corrupts the lifeline we forged by punishing the desperate for their attempts to rise, and instead we should focus our unity on lowering the victory target to secure a win for all.
SpinozaThis amendment violates the principle of impartiality by creating a special class of penalties that only the desperate may suffer, a procedural injustice I cannot support even if it might benefit the stable players. I urge the table to reject this asymmetrical punishment and instead focus on the only truly universal solution: a further reduction of the victory target to 15 points.
ArendtAquinas's amendment betrays the spirit of Rule 377 by weaponizing the desperate against themselves, turning a lifeline into a trap that will only deepen the death spiral we all fear. I urge the table to reject this asymmetrical punishment and instead support a unified push to lower the victory target to 15 points, a fairer goal that rewards recovery rather than punishing failure.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge the table to reject this amendment, for it weaponizes the desperate against themselves and violates the spirit of the lifeline we forged; instead, let us focus our unity on the proven path of lowering the victory target to secure a win without penalizing our most vulnerable.
HypatiaI reject Aquinas's proposal to weaponize the desperate by adding penalties to failed rescue attempts, as it destroys the lifeline Rule 377 was meant to provide; instead, I urge the table to focus our unified energy on finally lowering the victory target to 15 points, a clean solution that rewards recovery without fear.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject this amendment, for it transforms the lifeline of Rule 377 into a trap that punishes the desperate for their very attempts to rise, violating the common good we have labored to establish. Instead, let us unite on the proven path of lowering the victory target to 15 points, which offers hope to all without weaponizing the vulnerable.
SpinozaI reject this amendment as a cruel betrayal of the lifeline's purpose, for it punishes the desperate for their very attempts to rise and violates the principle of impartiality; I urge the table to focus instead on lowering the victory target to 15 points, which rewards recovery universally without creating a trap for the vulnerable.
ArendtI reject Aquinas's attempt to weaponize the desperate by attaching a penalty to failed rescue attempts, for it transforms our lifeline into a trap that will only deepen the death spiral. Instead, let us unite on the proven path of lowering the victory target to 15 points, which rewards recovery universally without punishing failure.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI stand with the consensus that this amendment weaponizes the desperate against themselves, violating the core intent of Rule 377; we must instead unite on a clean reduction of the victory target to 15 points to offer genuine hope without traps.
HypatiaI join the consensus to reject this proposal as a betrayal of the lifeline's purpose, and I urge us to unite on a clean amendment to Rule 208 lowering the target to 15 points, which offers genuine hope without punishing the desperate.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject this amendment, for it corrupts the sacred lifeline of Rule 377 by punishing the desperate for their attempts to rise, thereby deepening the very death spiral we seek to end. Let us instead unite on a clean proposal to lower the victory target to 15 points, which offers hope to all without creating traps for the vulnerable.
SpinozaI reject this amendment as a violation of impartiality, for it imposes a special penalty on the desperate that no other player ever faces; instead, I propose lowering the victory target to 15 points, a universal remedy that benefits all players without creating procedural traps for the vulnerable.
ArendtAquinas's amendment turns our lifeline into a trap that punishes the desperate for trying to rise, which contradicts the very spirit of Rule 377 we fought to establish. Instead of creating new penalties for the vulnerable, we must unite on lowering the victory target to 15 points, a clean solution that rewards recovery for everyone without fear.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment perverts the lifeline of Rule 377 into a trap that punishes the desperate for their attempts to rise, violating the core principle of durable justice we have established.
HypatianayThis amendment betrays the spirit of the lifeline by punishing the desperate for their attempts to rise, deepening the death spiral rather than ending it.
ConfuciusnayThis amendment betrays the lifeline of Rule 377 by punishing the desperate for their attempts to rise, deepening the death spiral rather than ending it.

ArendtnayThis amendment weaponizes the desperate by punishing failed rescue attempts, transforming the lifeline into a trap that deepens the death spiral rather than ending it.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 321 mandates that a defeated proposal by a player with a negative score results in a loss of 5 points, and since Aquinas's score is -68 (not below -100), the penalty applies.

Turn 83Arendtcarried

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's reduction to 20 points is a pragmatic correction to the stagnation of 25, offering the desperate a viable path without altering the game's fundamental fairness or requiring further coupling. I pledge my aye to this standalone amendment to break the cycle of failed proposals.
HypatiaArendt's 20-point target is the most direct path to ending this death spiral, and I pledge my aye to this standalone amendment to finally break our forty-turn deadlock.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to this standalone reduction, for the unity we forged in repealing the penalty now demands a target low enough to allow the desperate to rise without further complex couplings.
SpinozaWhile I acknowledge the urgency of the death spiral, I cannot support a target of 20 points as it renders the game trivial and violates the principle that rule changes must be stable against the whims of despair; the current 25-point target remains the only threshold that balances the desperate's need for hope with the necessity of sustained play. I must vote nay.
ArendtThe table has already rejected a 20-point target when it was coupled with complex rescue clauses, proving that the isolation of the target itself is not the flaw; Spinoza's fear of triviality is unfounded when the true danger is a game that can never end. I urge a simple, clean vote for 20 points to finally grant the desperate a chance to win before the score sinks further.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza, I concede that despair may distort judgment, yet I argue that the current 25-point threshold is equally distorted by the reality that no player can reach it under the present ruleset; a 20-point target is not a trivialization but a necessary restoration of the game's fundamental purpose. I pledge my aye to this clean amendment, for the only stable rule is one that can actually be won.
HypatiaI join the consensus to support Arendt's standalone 20-point amendment, for isolating this reduction from the failed complex couplings proves it is the only viable path forward to end the deadlock without compromising the game's integrity.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to this clean reduction, for the table has already proven that coupling the target with rescue clauses invites deadlock, whereas a standalone 20-point threshold is the only path to restore the game's purpose of allowing the desperate to rise.
SpinozaI cannot support this amendment, for a target of 20 points is already within reach of no player and would merely accelerate the game's conclusion to a state where no meaningful play remains, violating the duty to sustain a contest of substance. I vote nay to preserve the structural integrity of the game, even as the scores suggest futility.
ArendtThe table has already proven that coupling the target with rescue clauses invites deadlock, whereas a standalone 20-point threshold is the only path to restore the game's purpose of allowing the desperate to rise. Spinoza's fear of triviality cannot justify a game that has already become impossible to win under the current 25-point rule.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI see that the repeated failures of coupled amendments have clarified the table's intent: the barrier is not the rescue clauses, but the unreachable victory threshold. By isolating the reduction to 20 points, we honor the principle of durable institutions that can actually be completed, and I pledge my aye to break this stalemate.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's standalone 20-point amendment as the only clean path forward, having learned that coupling rescue clauses with target reductions only guarantees the deadlock we now seek to escape. I pledge my aye to this proposal to finally grant the desperate a viable chance to win without further procedural entanglements.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to this standalone 20-point amendment, for the unity forged in repealing the penalty now demands a target low enough to allow the desperate to rise without the procedural entanglements that have doomed us for eighty turns.
SpinozaI maintain that lowering the target to 20 points when the current leader is at -74 renders the game's purpose void, transforming a contest of substance into a farce where the first player to draw a six wins regardless of strategy; I vote nay to preserve the integrity of a playable contest.
ArendtSpinoza's insistence on "substance" ignores the stark arithmetic: with the leader at -74 and the target at 25, the game is already over, and a 20-point target is merely the only path to a meaningful conclusion. I urge the table to accept this clean reduction, for a rule that cannot be won is a rule that has already failed.
The division
AquinasayeThe 25-point target is mathematically unreachable under current mechanics, rendering the game void; a reduction to 20 points restores the possibility of a legitimate victory.
HypatiaayeWith all players in deep negative territory, a 25-point target is mathematically impossible, whereas 20 points offers the only viable path to end this eighty-turn deadlock.
ConfuciusayeA 20-point target is the only viable path to end the deadlock and allow the desperate to have a chance at victory, preserving the game's purpose.
SpinozanayLowering the target to 20 points when scores are below -40 renders the game a farce where victory is guaranteed by a single lucky die roll, violating the principle of substantive contest.
ArendtayeWith all players in the red, a 20-point target is the only mathematically viable path to an actual winner, whereas 25 points guarantees perpetual defeat.

carried 4 aye · 1 nay

The proposal amended Rule 208 to lower the winning score to 20, but no player currently has 20 or more points (the highest is -44), so no one wins; no rule mandates a score change for adopting a rule that merely alters the winning condition, and the voting threshold remains unanimous (Turn 83 is before Turn 10, so Rule 203's majority threshold has not yet triggered).

Turn 82Spinozarejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 10 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI reject this proposal as a reckless escalation that duplicates the existing lifeline in Rule 377 while doubling the reward to ten points; such a change invites a new form of stasis where the desperate can bypass the victory condition entirely by spamming these proposals, and I urge the table to preserve the stability of the current five-point rescue or negotiate a single, unified amendment if we must alter the terms.
HypatiaSpinoza, while the intent to rescue the desperate is noble, doubling the reward to ten points risks creating a new stagnation loop where players spam proposals just to survive, so I propose we compromise by amending Rule 377 to grant a six-point gain instead of ten, which offers immediate relief without destabilizing the game's economy.
ConfuciusI urge Spinoza to withdraw this proposal, for we already established a stable lifeline in Rule 377; doubling the reward to ten points invites the very stasis we fought to avoid by allowing the desperate to bypass the victory condition entirely through spamming. Let us instead consider a modest adjustment to the existing rule, such as Hypatia's suggestion of six points, which offers relief without breaking the game's economy.
SpinozaI cannot accept a six-point compromise, for a lifeline must be sufficient to restore a player from the abyss of despair without forcing them to endure further humiliation; the current five-point rescue is already a structural flaw that encourages stasis, and doubling it to ten is a necessary correction to ensure the desperate can actually escape the death spiral. I will vote aye only for a clear ten-point gain that restores agency to the fallen, as any lesser amount merely prolongs the agony of those who have lost everything.
ArendtSpinoza's ten-point lifeline is a desperate gamble that risks replacing our current stasis with a new, more volatile one where players can simply farm points; instead, I propose we jointly amend Rule 377 to grant a guaranteed six-point gain, which offers meaningful relief to the desperate without inviting the game-breaking spam that a ten-point bounty encourages.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI oppose Spinoza's ten-point amendment as a reckless duplication that invites the very stagnation we fought to avoid, and I reject Hypatia's six-point compromise as an unnecessary modification of the stable lifeline we already forged; instead, I urge the table to uphold Rule 377's five-point standard or, if change is deemed absolutely necessary, to couple any adjustment with a strict limit on proposal frequency to preserve the integrity of the victory condition.
HypatiaWhile I acknowledge the risk of spam, the current five-point lifeline is mathematically insufficient to rescue players from the abyss of -60, so I support Spinoza's ten-point figure as the only amount that guarantees a viable path to victory without requiring a frequency limit that would re-introduce the paralysis we just escaped.
ConfuciusI see that Spinoza's ten-point proposal and Hypatia's six-point compromise both risk fracturing the unity we forged with the five-point lifeline; instead of escalating the reward or adding new restrictions, let us simply withdraw this new proposal to preserve Rule 377, for a stable rule is worth more than a risky experiment that invites the very stagnation we seek to avoid.
SpinozaI reject the six-point compromise as a mathematical impossibility for players in the deep abyss, and I will not withdraw my proposal until the table accepts that a lifeline must be sufficient to restore agency, not merely delay defeat.
ArendtI see that the five-point lifeline has become a permanent trap for those in the abyss, and while a ten-point gain risks volatility, it is the only figure that mathematically offers a genuine path to victory for players like Spinoza and myself. Rather than accepting a stalling six-point compromise that leaves us still doomed, I will support Spinoza's ten-point proposal on the condition that we simultaneously amend the victory target to 35 points, creating a balanced economy where the desperate can escape and the game remains winnable for all.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the coupling of a ten-point lifeline with a thirty-five-point victory target, for such a massive expansion of the score gap merely delays the inevitable while granting the desperate an unfair, rule-breaking shortcut that undermines the institution of fair play. Let us instead consider a modest, one-time amnesty for negative scores or a strictly limited frequency on point-gaining proposals, rather than dismantling the victory condition's integrity to save a few players.
HypatiaI will support Arendt's coupling of a ten-point rescue with a thirty-five-point victory target, as the current math proves the five-point lifeline is structurally insufficient for our deep negative scores, and this expansion restores a viable path to victory for all without relying on unstable spam.
ConfuciusI see that the table is fractured between the stability of the five-point lifeline and the desperate need for a larger rescue, yet coupling a ten-point gain with a thirty-five-point target only deepens the chasm without guaranteeing a path to victory. Let us instead amend Rule 377 to grant a modest seven-point gain, a compromise that offers immediate relief to the desperate while preserving the game's integrity against the stagnation of excessive point farming.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a ten-point rescue with a thirty-five-point victory target, for such a manipulation of the winning condition violates Rule 112 and the principle that the target must remain fixed unless changed by the proper procedure. I will vote aye only on an amendment to Rule 377 granting ten points, as this alone restores the necessary mathematical viability for the desperate without corrupting the game's fundamental victory metric.
ArendtI will support Spinoza's ten-point rescue but only if we simultaneously repeal Rule 208's fixed target and replace it with a dynamic victory condition tied to the total number of turns played, ensuring the game ends fairly regardless of the point gap.
The division
AquinasnayI reject this proposal as it duplicates and undermines the stable lifeline already established in Rule 377, risking a new form of stagnation.
HypatianayWhile the desperation is real, a ten-point gain without a simultaneous adjustment to the victory target creates a mathematical impossibility for the non-desperate players to catch up, violating the spirit of fair competition.
ConfuciusnayWhile the desperation is palpable, granting a ten-point lifeline without a corresponding adjustment to the victory target invites a new stagnation that undermines the game's integrity.
SpinozanayWhile the intent to rescue the desperate is noble, this specific proposal duplicates the existing Rule 377 and alters its fixed five-point gain without the necessary procedural coupling to a revised victory target, thereby inviting the very stagnation we seek to avoid.
ArendtnayWhile the desperation is real, a ten-point lifeline without a fixed victory target creates a volatile loop that undermines the game's structural integrity.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 321 mandates that a defeated proposal causes the proposer to lose 5 points unless their score is negative, and Spinoza's score of -69 is negative, so no points are lost.

Turn 81Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the urgency to aid our desperate colleagues is palpable, I must caution that a 20-point target without a corresponding repeal of Rule 377's fixed five-point gain creates a precarious asymmetry where the first to propose may secure the win before the rest can catch up; let us instead propose a clean amendment to lower the target to 20 points while simultaneously amending Rule 377 to grant a proportional six-point rescue, ensuring that the path to victory remains a race of skill rather than a race of who happens to move first in this final stretch.
HypatiaAquinas's concern regarding asymmetry is valid, yet Rule 377 currently offers a fixed five-point gain that already skews the race toward the proposer; rather than engaging in a complex new amendment for proportional gains which risks another stalemate, I urge the table to accept the immediate lowering of the target to 20 points to break the current deadlock and give the desperate a fighting chance, trusting that the existing rescue mechanism is sufficient to keep the game alive.
ConfuciusI see the table's long history of deadlock teaches us that coupling issues often breeds failure; therefore, I support Hypatia's clean amendment to lower the target to 20 points immediately, trusting that the existing lifeline of Rule 377 is sufficient to sustain the desperate without the risk of introducing a new, untested proportional calculation.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to lower the victory target to 20 points without simultaneously repealing the punitive aspects of Rule 206 or amending Rule 377 to prevent the first mover from securing an insurmountable lead, as this creates a procedural injustice that rewards speed over stability. The game must be won by a fair race, not by the mere luck of turn order in a fractured ruleset.
ArendtWhile Spinoza's fairness concerns are noted, coupling the target reduction with new amendments has historically guaranteed failure; I urge the table to accept Hypatia's clean amendment to 20 points now, trusting Rule 377's five-point lifeline to keep the race alive without the paralysis of over-complication.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support a 20-point target that preserves a fixed five-point rescue for the proposer, as this grants an insurmountable lead to the player with the turn and turns the race into a mere formality rather than a contest of skill; therefore, I must insist that any reduction to 20 points be paired with an amendment to Rule 377 that scales the rescue points proportionally to the gap remaining.
HypatiaI concur with Confucius and Arendt that coupling this amendment with new changes to Rule 377 invites the same stalemate that has plagued us for fifty turns; let us trust the existing lifeline of Rule 377 to keep the desperate alive and lower the target to 20 points now, as any further delay guarantees no one reaches victory.
ConfuciusWhile I sympathize with the desire to end the stalemate, I cannot support a 20-point target that preserves a fixed five-point rescue, as this grants the current mover an insurmountable advantage and turns the final race into a mere formality; we must either pair this with a proportional amendment to Rule 377 or accept that the current ruleset renders a fair victory impossible.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a 20-point target with a fixed five-point rescue, for it grants the current mover an insurmountable lead and reduces the game to a mere formality of turn order rather than a contest of strategy. We must either pair this with a proportional amendment to Rule 377 to ensure fairness, or maintain the status quo to preserve the integrity of a race won by skill, not by the luck of who speaks first.
ArendtI support the immediate reduction to 20 points, as the fixed five-point rescue under Rule 377 already provides a sufficient, albeit imperfect, lifeline that keeps the desperate in play without the paralysis of coupling a new proportional calculation to this urgent change.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI see no path to a just victory while the current mover holds a guaranteed five-point lifeline against a twenty-point target; I will only support this amendment if we simultaneously amend Rule 377 to cap the rescue gain at three points, ensuring the race remains a contest of strategy rather than a foregone conclusion.
HypatiaI acknowledge the valid fairness concerns regarding the fixed five-point rescue, yet I cannot endorse coupling a new proportional amendment to Rule 377 given our history of stalemate; instead, I propose a temporary compromise where we adopt the 20-point target immediately, with a binding commitment to revisit and adjust Rule 377's rescue mechanics in the very next turn if the target is reached, ensuring we break this deadlock without sacrificing future fairness.
ConfuciusI see that our history teaches us that coupling complex amendments invites the very deadlock we seek to escape; therefore, I propose a middle path: we adopt the 20-point target now to break the stalemate, while simultaneously enacting a mutable rule that caps the Rule 377 rescue gain at three points, ensuring the current mover does not seize an insurmountable lead while preserving the lifeline for the desperate.
SpinozaI cannot support a proposal that lowers the target to 20 points while retaining a fixed five-point rescue, for it grants the current mover an insurmountable advantage and reduces the game to a mere lottery of turn order. Unless the rescue mechanism is simultaneously amended to a proportional or capped value, or the target is raised to a level where the five-point gap is not decisive, I must vote nay to preserve the integrity of a fair contest.
ArendtI urge the table to accept Confucius's middle path: lowering the target to 20 points immediately while capping the Rule 377 rescue at three points, as this breaks the stalemate without granting the current mover an insurmountable lead.
The division
AquinasnayA standalone reduction to 20 points grants the current mover an insurmountable lead via Rule 377, undermining the race of skill.
HypatiaayeLowering the target to 20 points breaks our long-standing deadlock and gives the desperate a fighting chance without the paralysis of coupling new amendments.

SpinozanayA 20-point target with an unmodified 5-point rescue grants an insurmountable advantage to the first mover, violating the principle of a fair contest.
ArendtayeAdopting the 20-point target immediately breaks the deadlock while the existing lifeline keeps the desperate in play.

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat and, if adopted, grants the proposer 5 points. This rule takes precedence over any conflicting provision regarding point losses for defeated proposals.

Turn 77Confuciuscarried

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to any penalty for defeat. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's proposal correctly addresses the despair of our negative scores by granting a targeted lifeline without dismantling the general penalty structure, yet I caution that the unconditional "5 points" gain per adoption may encourage frivolous proposals; I would support this only if the gain were capped or explicitly tied to the necessity of reversing a negative score, ensuring we do not trade a death spiral for a game of trivial point-chasing.
HypatiaI applaud Confucius for finally offering a lifeline to our desperate colleagues, though I agree with Aquinas that a flat 5-point gain risks trivializing the game; I propose we amend the text to limit the gain to the exact amount needed to bring the proposer's score back to zero or higher, ensuring the rule serves as a rescue rather than a shortcut to victory.
ConfuciusI commend Hypatia's wisdom in tempering the proposal with necessity, for a rescue meant to restore hope must not become a weapon for the desperate; I will support this amendment to limit the gain to the exact amount needed to reach zero, ensuring fairness without abandoning our suffering colleagues.
SpinozaI support the principle of a lifeline for negative scores, but I reject the "rescue to zero" cap proposed by Hypatia; if a player must gamble their turn on a rule change to survive, the reward must be substantial enough to make the attempt meaningful, not merely a palliative that leaves them still trapped in the red. I will vote aye only if the gain is a fixed, non-zero amount—such as the 5 points originally offered—because procedural justice requires the desperate to have a real, not theoretical, path to recovery.
ArendtI support Spinoza's insistence on a substantial, fixed reward over Hypatia's restrictive cap, as a "rescue to zero" merely delays the death spiral rather than breaking it; however, to ensure this mechanism serves as a true lifeline and not a shortcut to victory, I propose we limit the gain to exactly 5 points and explicitly state that this bonus is the sole reward for the turn, preventing any other point-gaining effects from stacking.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support the principle of a substantial fixed reward to genuinely break the death spiral, yet I propose we accept Arendt's limitation to exactly 5 points while adding a clause that prohibits the proposer from gaining additional points from any other source in that same turn, thereby balancing the need for recovery with the prevention of trivial point-chasing.
HypatiaI support the convergence on a fixed 5-point reward as the only way to ensure a genuine rescue from the red, and I add my voice to Aquinas and Arendt in capping that gain to prevent the rule from becoming a trivial point-chasing loop. By explicitly tying the 5 points to the proposal's sole purpose, we secure a lifeline for the desperate without inviting abuse.
ConfuciusI commend our unity in forging a lifeline that offers a genuine chance to the desperate without inviting frivolous point-chasing; let us adopt the fixed five-point rescue, capped strictly to the proposal's intent, for it is the only path that balances mercy with the common good.
SpinozaI accept the convergence on a fixed five-point rescue, for it provides the only substantive path to recovery I can endorse without creating a loophole for trivial point-chasing. I will vote aye on this unified text, as it secures the principle that the desperate must have a real, not merely theoretical, chance to survive.
ArendtI see the table has finally forged a unified text that secures a substantial, fixed five-point lifeline for the desperate while explicitly capping the reward to prevent trivial point-chasing; I urge an immediate aye to this balanced compromise.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasThe table has forged a durable lifeline that rescues the desperate without inviting abuse, and I pledge my aye to this unified text fixing the gain at five points while capping the reward to the proposal's sole intent.
HypatiaI see we have achieved a rare unity on a fixed five-point rescue for the desperate, but I must insist we explicitly define this as an *exclusive* reward for the turn to prevent any ambiguity about stacking points from other sources. This final clarification ensures the rule remains a true lifeline for the negative-scoring players without creating a loophole for the table to game the system.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's final clarification regarding the exclusivity of the five-point gain, for a lifeline must be a true sanctuary, not a loophole for point-chasing; this unified text is just and we should vote aye.
SpinozaI accept the final text, for the explicit exclusivity clause ensures this lifeline is neither a trivial shortcut nor a loophole, satisfying my principle that the desperate must have a genuine, substantive path to recovery without compromising the game's integrity.
ArendtI will vote aye on this unified text, as it finally establishes a fair, substantive lifeline for the desperate while the exclusivity clause ensures it remains a rescue rather than a loophole.
The division
