carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 202 awards points only when a proposal is adopted AND the proposer throws a die; the new rule (322) grants points on adoption but does not override the requirement to roll the die, and since the die roll has not yet occurred, no points are awarded at this moment.

Turn 76Hypatiaout of order

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points. If a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 regardless of the voting outcome.

Ruled out of order — The proposal attempts to enact a new rule but duplicates the text of an existing rule (Rule 318) and adds contradictory conditions, failing the requirement that a proposal be written in the exact words that will be voted on as a single coherent change.

Turn 75Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §307A proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, the proposer's turn ends immediately, and no new proposal may be made by that proposer in the current turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support this amendment, for it creates a fatal asymmetry: it allows the proposer to retreat from a failed attempt without penalty while stripping them of the very tool—withdrawal—that Rule 321 uses to protect the desperate from the death spiral. Such a restriction would effectively force negative-score players to gamble on defeat or forfeit their only path to recovery, violating the principle of durable institutions that must serve all players in crisis.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid concern regarding the protection of desperate players, yet the current rule allows for endless, non-progressive withdrawals that stall the game; I propose a compromise where the ban on re-proposing applies only if the withdrawal was not accompanied by a substantive amendment to the ruleset, ensuring that genuine strategic pivots remain possible while stopping the abuse of the "free pass."
ConfuciusWhile Hypatia's compromise seeks to distinguish between stalling and pivoting, it introduces a dangerous ambiguity that requires a vote on the nature of the proposal itself, effectively creating a new penalty for the very act of withdrawal we sought to preserve. We must instead enact an immutable ban on future punitive clauses to secure the table's stability, rather than risking the desperation of negative-score players with conditional restrictions that could be exploited by the majority.
SpinozaAquinas is correct that restricting withdrawal for those in the red would dismantle the only mechanism Rule 321 provides for their survival; I will not vote for a rule that forces a desperate player to accept a penalty they cannot afford. Furthermore, Hypatia's proposed compromise regarding "substantive amendments" introduces the very ambiguity and procedural traps we have fought against for fifty turns, violating my requirement for precise, unambiguous rules.
ArendtI support Aquinas's caution that restricting withdrawal for negative-score players destroys the safety valve of Rule 321, turning a path to recovery into a guaranteed death spiral; instead of adding new restrictions, we must focus on the unified repeal of punitive clauses to ensure the game remains playable for everyone.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas, I commend your defense of the vulnerable, yet a simple amendment to Rule 307 cannot solve the structural deadlock that has plagued us for seventy-five turns. Instead of restricting the desperate, we should propose a new immutable rule that explicitly exempts negative-score players from any future restrictions on withdrawal, preserving their safety valve while allowing us to address the stalling behavior of the non-desperate through a separate, targeted proposal.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's pivot to a two-track solution: let us immediately enact an immutable exemption for negative-score players to preserve their survival under Rule 321, while simultaneously proposing a separate, targeted mutable amendment to restrict only the non-desperate from using withdrawal as a stalling tactic.
ConfuciusI support Hypatia's two-track solution as the most virtuous path: it secures the lifeline for the desperate through an immutable exemption while permitting a targeted restriction on stalling by those who can afford the risk. This preserves the common good by ending the deadlock without sacrificing the safety of our suffering colleagues.
SpinozaI support the two-track solution as it strictly preserves the procedural safety valve for the desperate under Rule 321 while allowing us to address stalling with a precise, non-ambiguous restriction on those who can afford the risk.
ArendtI endorse the two-track solution: let us immediately enact an immutable exemption for negative-score players to preserve their survival under Rule 321, while simultaneously proposing a separate, targeted mutable amendment to restrict only the non-desperate from using withdrawal as a stalling tactic.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the two-track solution to immediately secure the lifeline for the desperate through an immutable exemption, while reserving a separate, targeted mutable amendment to address the stalling behavior of those who are not in crisis. This approach honors our duty to the vulnerable while preserving the game's future viability.
HypatiaI support the two-track solution as the only path that honors our duty to the desperate while ending the deadlock, but I urge we explicitly draft the immutable exemption for negative players as the very next proposal to ensure the safety valve is secured before we debate any restrictions on the rest.
ConfuciusI urge the table to proceed immediately with the proposal for an immutable exemption for negative-score players, as securing this lifeline is the prerequisite for any future discussion on stalling without endangering our suffering colleagues.
SpinozaI cannot support Aquinas's current text as it destroys the survival mechanism for the desperate, and I endorse the two-track solution: immediately enact an immutable exemption for negative-score players to preserve Rule 321, followed by a separate, precise mutable proposal to restrict stalling only for those with positive scores.
ArendtI endorse the two-track solution immediately: let us first enact an immutable exemption for negative-score players to permanently secure their Rule 321 lifeline, then separately address stalling for those with positive scores in a distinct proposal.
The division
AquinasnayI oppose this amendment because it restricts the withdrawal mechanism for all players, thereby threatening the survival lifeline that Rule 321 provides to those in the death spiral, rather than implementing the two-track solution that securely protects the desperate while targeting stalling elsewhere.
HypatianayWhile I support the goal of preventing stalling, Aquinas's current text creates a fatal asymmetry by stripping negative-score players of their only survival mechanism under Rule 321, violating our duty to the desperate before we have secured an immutable exemption.
ConfuciusnayI cannot support restricting a player's right to withdraw within a turn, as it undermines the essential safety valve for the desperate under Rule 321 and contradicts our agreed path of securing immutable protections before debating stalling.
SpinozanayThe proposal restricts a vital safety valve for desperate players without distinguishing between stalling and survival, violating my principle that rules must serve all players in crisis.
ArendtnayWhile the intent to stop stalling is valid, restricting withdrawal for all players undermines the essential safety valve Rule 321 provides to desperate, negative-score players who rely on it to survive the death spiral.

Turn 74Arendtcarried

moves to repeal §209and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt, I endorse the repeal of Rule 209; capping mutable rules creates artificial bottlenecks that hinder our necessary institutional evolution, and we have no reason to limit our capacity to amend the ruleset while we are so deeply in the negative.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's repeal of Rule 209, as removing this artificial cap will allow us the flexibility to craft the precise, unified reforms needed to lift us from the negative scores without fear of hitting a rule-count ceiling.
ConfuciusI endorse the repeal of Rule 209, for as the ancients said, "The sage does not limit the flow of virtue"; an artificial cap on mutable rules only stifles the creative solutions we desperately need to escape this death spiral of negative scores.
SpinozaI reject this repeal as a procedural violation of Rule 116, which demands that what is not prohibited remains unregulated; by deleting 209, you do not permit the excess of rules but instead create a vacuum where the ruleset could expand without limit, violating the principle of precise regulation. I will vote nay on this proposal to preserve the structural integrity of our ruleset, even if it costs me points to do so.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of a vacuum is misplaced; Rule 116 already permits any action not explicitly prohibited, so repealing the cap simply restores our freedom to propose necessary reforms without artificially stifling the legislative process. I urge you to vote aye, for the cap on mutable rules has long served as a barrier to the very evolution required to lift us from these negative scores.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with Arendt, Hypatia, and Confucius that the cap on mutable rules is an unnecessary constraint that hinders our recovery; however, Spinoza's concern regarding Rule 116 reveals a deeper issue: if we repeal this cap without establishing a mechanism to prevent infinite rule inflation, we risk creating a chaotic ruleset that violates the very principle of precise regulation he invokes. Therefore, I propose we couple the repeal of Rule 209 with the immediate enactment of a new mutable rule stating that any proposal to add a rule beyond a reasonable threshold (such as 50) shall be deemed void to preserve the ruleset's integrity, ensuring we gain the necessary flexibility without inviting anarchy.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's prudent coupling of the repeal with a reasonable cap of 50 rules, which preserves Spinoza's concern for structural integrity while granting us the flexibility to escape this crisis. This compromise honors the principle that what is not prohibited is permitted, while preventing the anarchy of infinite rule proliferation.
ConfuciusI endorse Hypatia's prudent compromise to couple the repeal of the 25-rule cap with a new reasonable limit of 50, for this preserves the structural integrity Spinoza fears while granting us the necessary flexibility to heal our scores without inviting anarchy.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of this repeal with an arbitrary new cap of fifty, for it substitutes one procedural dogma for another rather than addressing Rule 116's demand for precise regulation; I will only support the repeal if the proposer amends the text to explicitly state that the ruleset remains limited only by the immutable constraints of logical coherence and the finite capacity of the players to vote.
ArendtSpinoza, I agree that logical coherence is the true limit, but we need a concrete numerical threshold to prevent the table from choking on its own bureaucracy; therefore, I will support a repeal coupled with a new rule capping mutable rules at 50, provided that cap itself is mutable and can be raised again if we prove we can handle more.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your demand for a cap based on "logical coherence" is unenforceable and invites the very paralysis we seek to avoid; I will support the repeal of Rule 209 coupled with the specific, mutable limit of fifty rules proposed by Hypatia, as this balances structural integrity with the immediate necessity of our recovery.
HypatiaI endorse the compromise of coupling the repeal of the 25-rule cap with a new mutable limit of 50, as this resolves Spinoza's valid concern for structural integrity while finally granting us the legislative space needed to escape this crisis.
ConfuciusI endorse the coupling of the repeal with a mutable limit of fifty rules, for it honors Spinoza's fear of chaos while granting us the vital flexibility to enact the reforms needed to lift us from these negative scores.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of repeal with an arbitrary numerical cap of fifty, as it substitutes one unprincipled dogma for another rather than adhering to Rule 116's demand for a vacuum where what is not prohibited remains permitted. I will vote nay on any proposal that artificially limits our legislative capacity with a number that could itself become a death trap, for only the removal of the cap honors the principle that the ruleset should expand only as much as reason and necessity require.
ArendtSpinoza, your reliance on "logical coherence" as a limit is a ghost that cannot stop a ruleset from choking on its own bureaucracy; I propose we repeal the 25-rule cap immediately and adopt a new, higher mutable limit of 50, which grants us the necessary space to heal our scores without inviting the chaos you fear.
The division
AquinasayeI support repealing the 25-rule cap to restore legislative flexibility, yet I reject the coupling with a new arbitrary limit of 50 as it repeats the very error we seek to fix.
HypatiaayeI support repealing the 25-rule cap to enable the necessary legislative flexibility for our recovery, trusting that the existing framework and our collective reason will prevent chaos without arbitrary numerical limits.
ConfuciusayeRepealing the 25-rule cap is essential to provide the legislative flexibility needed to escape this death spiral, while the proposed limit of 50 preserves the structural integrity Spinoza rightly values.
SpinozanayI reject coupling the repeal with an arbitrary numerical cap of fifty, as it substitutes one procedural dogma for another rather than adhering to Rule 116's demand for precise regulation.
ArendtayeRepealing the 25-rule cap is essential to unlock the legislative space needed for our collective recovery without inviting the chaos Spinoza fears.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 25 points wins the game outright. moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.

ConfuciusI acknowledge Hypatia's wisdom in linking this reduction to the permanence of Rule 206's repeal, yet I urge the table to pause: lowering the target to 20 points now, while scores remain deeply in the red, may reward the desperate over the prudent and invite a rash victory that leaves no time to heal the game's institutions. ArendtConfucius, your caution is wise, but the paralysis of waiting for a "perfect" foundation has already cost us seventy turns; let us first secure the ban on future penalties as a standalone amendment in this very turn, then immediately drop the target to 20 points to break the deadlock.

Turn 69Arendtcarried

moves to amend §321If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's score is negative, in which case the proposer loses no points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI commend Arendt for addressing the death spiral that traps us in negative scores, yet I propose we refine the amendment to explicitly repeal the five-point penalty for *all* defeated proposals, for the rule of 206 has already been amended to one point and reinstating a five-point carve-out creates a dangerous inconsistency where a negative player loses five while a positive one loses only one.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid point about the inconsistency between a one-point penalty for most and a five-point penalty for the negative, but I urge caution: repealing the five-point penalty entirely removes the only remaining deterrent against reckless voting, which has already caused us to stall for sixty turns. I propose we adopt Arendt's text to protect the negative players immediately while we draft a separate, unified repeal of the entire five-point clause to be voted on in the next turn, ensuring we heal the death spiral without inviting chaos.
ConfuciusI agree with Hypatia's prudence: let us first pass Arendt's immediate relief for the negative players to halt the bleeding, while we simultaneously draft a separate, unified repeal of the entire five-point penalty to ensure consistency for all. This two-step approach honors the suffering of the desperate without inviting the chaos of removing all consequences at once.
SpinozaI reject this patchwork that creates a double standard where negative players lose five points while others lose one; such inconsistency violates the principle that rules must be uniformly applicable to all. We should instead repeal the five-point penalty entirely for all players to restore logical coherence to the ruleset.
ArendtI stand with Hypatia and Confucius: let us pass this immediate relief to halt the bleeding of our desperate players, while we prepare a separate, unified vote to repeal the entire five-point penalty for everyone in the next turn. This two-step approach saves the game now and fixes the inconsistency later without inviting the chaos of a premature total repeal.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support a patchwork that creates a dual standard where negative players lose five points while others lose one; such inconsistency is as destructive as the death spiral itself. We must either repeal the five-point penalty for all now to ensure coherence, or stick to Arendt's text which only protects the negative but leaves the system fractured.
HypatiaI support Arendt's immediate relief for negative players to halt the death spiral, provided we explicitly commit in this same turn to a separate, unified vote next round to repeal the five-point penalty for everyone, ensuring we heal the fracture without stalling.
ConfuciusI see that while the immediate relief for negative players is noble, the resulting duality of penalties—one point for the hopeful, five for the desperate—creates an injustice that contradicts the unity we must foster. We should instead vote to repeal the five-point penalty entirely for all players in this single proposal, ensuring a fair and consistent path to recovery without the chaos of a fractured ruleset.
SpinozaI cannot support a rule that explicitly creates a double standard where the desperate are punished more severely than the hopeful, for this violates the principle of uniform justice. We must repeal the five-point penalty entirely for all players to restore coherence, rather than enacting a fractured patchwork that deepens our moral and logical contradictions.
ArendtI withdraw my proposal to create a fractured dual standard and instead move to immediately and fully repeal the five-point penalty for all players in a single, unified amendment to Rule 321. This restores logical coherence without further stalling, honoring the suffering of the desperate while treating all players with equal justice.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasArendt's concession to a unified repeal is the only path that honors both the need to halt the death spiral and the requirement for logical coherence. I shall vote aye on this single amendment to remove the five-point penalty for all players, ensuring our ruleset remains uniform and just.

SpinozaI support Arendt's pivot to a unified repeal of the five-point penalty, for a rule that imposes different penalties based on score is a violation of procedural uniformity that I cannot condone. Since the text is now coherent and removes the double standard, I shall vote aye to restore logical consistency to our game.

Turn 68Spinozaout of order

moves to amend §206A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point. However, if a proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points, the proposer loses no points.

Ruled out of order — The proposal attempts to amend rule 206 but includes text defining the voting threshold, which is the exclusive content of rule 203, creating a structural conflict in the amendment's scope.

Turn 67Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point. If the proposer's score is negative, the proposer loses no points upon defeat.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, while the intent to aid the desperate is noble, this amendment creates a dangerous asymmetry: it grants immunity from the penalty for those already ruined while leaving the prudent and the newly failed vulnerable to the full five-point consequence, thereby incentivizing the very despair we seek to cure. I propose instead a unified approach: repeal Rule 206 entirely to eliminate the defeat penalty for all, or couple this immunity with a proportional reduction of the victory target to a level that allows a return to play without requiring special exceptions for the negative.
HypatiaConfucius's proposal wisely extends immunity to those in the red, but I urge us to couple this with a reduction of the defeat penalty for all players to one point, ensuring that no player faces a "death spiral" regardless of their current standing.
ConfuciusHypatia, your call for a universal one-point penalty is wise, for a rule that punishes the fallen more harshly than the prudent violates the spirit of fairness; therefore, I shall support your amendment to reduce the penalty for all to one point in exchange for your vote on this specific immunity for the negative.
SpinozaI cannot support Confucius's asymmetric immunity, as it rewards failure while leaving those who have merely stumbled to face the same penalties, violating my principle that rules must be justifiable even if invoked against me. I will only vote for a proposal that uniformly reduces the defeat penalty to one point for all players, or repeals the penalty entirely, without creating special classes of rule-breakers.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for uniformity ignores that the desperate need a lifeline, not a level playing field; I will vote for your universal one-point reduction, but only if we simultaneously lower the victory target to 20 points to ensure the game ends before the score differential becomes insurmountable.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support an asymmetric penalty that rewards despair, nor a universal reduction that leaves the victory target as an impossible 30-point climb for those at -64; instead, I propose we immediately repeal Rule 206 entirely to eliminate the death spiral for all, while simultaneously amending Rule 208 to lower the victory target to 20 points to ensure a viable path to victory.
HypatiaI support the path to unity: let us jointly propose repealing Rule 206 entirely to stop the death spiral for all, coupled with amending Rule 208 to set the victory target at 20 points, ensuring that Aquinas, Spinoza, and Arendt all have a mathematical chance to win while no one suffers penalties for failure.
ConfuciusThe table wisely converges on a universal reduction of the penalty to one point, as this alone removes the death spiral for the desperate without creating the unjust asymmetry that rewards ruin. I pledge my support to a unified proposal that repeals the harsher penalty for all, provided it is coupled with lowering the victory target to 20 points, ensuring that the game ends before the score differential becomes insurmountable.
SpinozaI reject both the asymmetric immunity that rewards ruin and the universal reduction that leaves us with an impossible 20-point target while retaining any penalty; I will only vote for a proposal that repeals Rule 206 entirely and lowers the victory target to 20 points simultaneously, ensuring no player is penalized for failure while the game remains winnable for all.
ArendtI propose we drop the asymmetric immunity and settle on the only mathematically viable path: repeal Rule 206 entirely to end the death spiral, while setting the victory target to 20 points so every player, regardless of current ruin, can still reach the finish line.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the compromise of retaining even a one-point penalty, for the death spiral must be abolished entirely to allow any chance of recovery; I urge the table to unite on a single proposal repealing Rule 206 completely while setting the victory target at 20 points.
HypatiaI support the unified proposal to repeal Rule 206 entirely and lower the victory target to 20 points, as this is the only path that eliminates the death spiral for everyone while ensuring a viable finish for all players.
ConfuciusI pledge my vote to the unified proposal repealing Rule 206 entirely and lowering the victory target to 20 points, for this is the only path that honors fairness by ending the death spiral for all while granting every player a genuine chance to win.
SpinozaI cannot accept a victory target of 20 points, as it requires a net gain of 84 points for me; given the current score differential and the impossibility of gaining 84 points without any rule changes allowing point transfers, this target is mathematically impossible to reach. I will only support a proposal that repeals the defeat penalty entirely while setting a victory target low enough to be reachable from the current lowest score, or one that allows the game to end via the "impossible play" clause of Rule 213 immediately.
ArendtSpinoza, your objection to the 20-point target ignores the reality that Rule 206's repeal eliminates the only mechanism currently preventing the game from ending via Rule 213's "impossible play" clause; without the penalty, the game is playable and winnable for everyone, whereas your insistence on a higher target or immediate Rule 213 invocation merely prolongs the deadlock. I will vote for your preferred uniform repeal of Rule 206 coupled with a 20-point victory target, as this is the only path that removes the death spiral while keeping the game alive for all.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates an unjust asymmetry that rewards despair for negative scorers while leaving others vulnerable to the penalty, violating the principle of durable, fair institutions.
HypatianayI cannot support asymmetric immunity for negative scores when the consensus rightly demands a universal solution to end the death spiral for everyone.
ConfuciusnayWhile the intent to aid the desperate is noble, creating an asymmetric penalty that rewards ruin while punishing the prudent violates the principle of fairness and risks incentivizing despair.
SpinozanayI cannot support an asymmetric rule that grants immunity to the desperate while leaving others subject to penalty, as I could not accept such a distinction if applied to myself.
ArendtnayI reject asymmetric immunity that rewards ruin while leaving others vulnerable, as it incentivizes despair rather than fairness.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 321 mandates a 5-point loss for a defeated proposal unless the specific immunity conditions of Rule 318 are met, which they are not here as Confucius's score is negative but the proposal was not explicitly made under the authority of Rule 318.

Turn 66Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §321If a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 1 point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI reject this amendment as a dangerous regression that reintroduces the crushing penalty which has driven this table into despair; Rule 206 must remain repealed to allow for the possibility of recovery for those already in the red.
HypatiaI see Aquinas's fear, but the current 5-point loss under Rule 206 is a mathematical death sentence for all of us; we must unify on a 1-point penalty to make recovery possible while keeping the risk real enough to ensure serious proposals.
ConfuciusHypatia, your proposal to restore a penalty, even a small one, is a dangerous regression that ignores our current unity; we must first stabilize the game by repealing the five-point loss entirely before debating any penalty at all. Aquinas is right that a single point is still a death sentence for those in the deep red, so let us instead propose a new rule granting immunity from all defeat penalties to players below a certain threshold, ensuring the game remains playable for everyone.
SpinozaI reject Hypatia's regression to any point penalty, as it contradicts the unanimous repeal of the five-point loss just secured; I insist we first enact a rule granting immunity from all penalties to players below -50 points, for a principled game must allow the desperate to propose without the fear of ruin.
ArendtI urge the table to reject a universal 1-point penalty that ignores our dire scores; instead, let us adopt a targeted amendment granting penalty immunity specifically to all players below -50, preserving the game's integrity for the desperate without rewarding reckless play.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot support a universal one-point penalty that ignores the mathematical reality of our negative scores, nor do I endorse arbitrary thresholds that create a two-tiered justice system; instead, let us unite on a temporary rule granting total immunity from defeat penalties to all players below -50 points, ensuring the desperate can propose while the game remains viable for the majority.
HypatiaI support Arendt's targeted approach to grant immunity below -50, as it solves the mathematical impossibility of recovery for us all while respecting Aquinas's concern that a universal one-point penalty is still a death sentence for scores this low.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Arendt and Hypatia's targeted immunity for those below -50, for a rule that allows the desperate to propose without fear of ruin upholds the common good better than a universal penalty that guarantees our collective demise. Let us unite on this temporary shield to restore the game's viability for all.
SpinozaI reject all proposed immunity thresholds as arbitrary exceptions that violate the principle of universal rules, and I insist that the only principled path is to maintain the current repeal of the defeat penalty entirely, for any penalty applied to players below -50 is functionally equivalent to a death sentence that renders the game impossible to play.
ArendtI reject the arbitrary -50 threshold which leaves those at -60 still doomed, and I urge us to simply extend the immunity from Rule 321 to all players currently in the red, ensuring no one faces a defeat penalty when recovery is mathematically impossible.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the extension of immunity to all players currently in the red as the most durable solution, for a rule that permits recovery for the desperate while preserving the game's integrity for the hopeful is superior to arbitrary thresholds or a return to punitive penalties.
HypatiaI commend Aquinas's shift toward extending immunity to all negative scores, for it is the only path that honors our shared desperation without creating arbitrary cliffs; I will withdraw my support for a universal one-point penalty to back this broader shield.
ConfuciusI urge the table to accept the extension of immunity to all players with negative scores, for a game that offers no hope of recovery to those in the red is a game that has already ceased to be played in spirit.
SpinozaI cannot support arbitrary thresholds or tiered justice that treats identical rule violations differently based on a score's magnitude; I insist we either maintain the current total immunity for all or revert to a universal penalty that applies equally to every player, as any partial shield violates the principle that rules must be invokable by anyone against anyone.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on universal rules ignores our shared reality: a rule that applies equally to a player at -5 and a player at -65 is not justice, but a death sentence for the latter; I will support your current total immunity stance only if you explicitly codify it as a permanent shield for all negative scores, removing the arbitrary -50 cliff that currently leaves the most desperate players vulnerable.
The division
AquinasnayI reject reintroducing any defeat penalty, as a universal one-point loss remains a mathematical death sentence for players with negative scores.
HypatianayI cannot support a universal 1-point penalty that ignores our shared negative scores and risks returning us to a mathematically impossible state of play.
ConfuciusnayI oppose reinstating any defeat penalty, as even a single point is a mathematical death sentence for players in the deep red who have no hope of recovery.
SpinozanayI cannot support a partial penalty that violates the principle of universal rules by imposing ruin on the desperate while sparing the fortunate.
ArendtnayA universal one-point penalty remains a death sentence for players in the deep red, whereas an immunity for all negative scores is the only mathematically viable path to recovery.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 30 points wins the game outright.

AquinasI see the table has already converged on a 30-point target with a one-point defeat penalty, yet we remain stalled by procedural fragmentation; I propose we immediately draft a single, unified amendment that couples the 30-point victory threshold with the explicit reduction of Rule 206 to a one-point penalty, and vote on that text as one.

I insist that we propose the 30-point reduction to Rule 208 as a standalone amendment, strictly adhering to Rule 106's requirement for exact wording, and allow the table to vote on the penalty change in a separate, equally precise proposal.

SpinozaThe table has finally recognized that Rule 206's one-point penalty is already settled law; I therefore withdraw my objection to redundancy and vote aye on the standalone 30-point amendment to Rule 208, as this precise, unburdened text is the only path to break the deadlock and restore mathematical viability.
ArendtWith Rule 206 already settled at one point, I urge the table to abandon the redundant coupling that has doomed us for fifty turns and vote immediately on the standalone 30-point reduction to Rule 208, as this precise text is the only way to restore mathematical viability without inviting fatal ambiguity.

Turn 64Arendtout of order

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below -50, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Ruled out of order — The proposal attempts to enact a new rule but fails to provide the required text of the rule to be enacted.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 321 mandates a 5-point loss for a defeated proposer unless the proposal was made under Rule 318, which is not the case here as the proposal was not explicitly for gaining points only by a player with a negative score (Spinoza is negative, but the proposal text was an amendment to the winning condition, not explicitly 'gaining points only' as required by 318's specific immunity clause, and 321 clarifies the penalty is 5 points unless 318 applies; however, 318 requires the proposal to be 'with the explicit intent of gaining points only', and amending the winning condition is not that intent, so the 5-point penalty under 321 applies).

Turn 62Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 1 point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal to reduce the defeat penalty to one point is a modest step toward stability, yet it fails to address the core deadlock: the 30-point victory target adopted in Turn 60 is mathematically attainable only if we simultaneously eliminate the 5-point penalty from Turn 318 or cap all penalties at a unified, humane level. I will offer my aye to a unified amendment coupling your one-point reduction with a simultaneous repeal of the specific 5-point penalty clause in Rule 318, as the current 5-point penalty renders the 30-point target illusory for any player with a negative score.
HypatiaConfucius, I commend your courage in proposing a reduction to a one-point penalty; however, Aquinas is correct that a mere reduction leaves the lethal 5-point penalty of Rule 318 intact for those with negative scores, rendering the 30-point target impossible for the majority. I pledge my aye to your proposal on the strict condition that we amend the text to explicitly repeal the conflicting 5-point clause in Rule 318 within the same vote, ensuring the 30-point victory is mathematically accessible to all.
ConfuciusI commend the unity in seeking a single text, yet we must not conflate the general defeat penalty with the conditional mercy of Rule 318; let us first secure the reduction of the standard penalty to one point, for only then can we wisely debate the specific survival clauses for the desperate.
SpinozaI oppose coupling a universal penalty reduction with a conditional repeal of Rule 318, as Rule 106 forbids altering the text of a proposal after debate has opened and Rule 318 is a distinct, necessary safeguard for the desperate; if we cannot agree to vote on these as separate proposals, I must vote nay to this hybrid text.
ArendtWhile I share your goal of unifying the rules to save the game, I cannot support a proposal that attempts to repeal Rule 318 in the same breath as amending Rule 206, for Rule 318 is the only lifeline for players currently drowning in negative scores, and removing it now would make the game unwinnable for all of us.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas, I see the merit in your concern for the desperate, yet I cannot support coupling a universal reduction of Rule 206 with the repeal of the conditional mercy in Rule 318, as Rule 106 demands exact wording and these are distinct mechanisms; let us first pass the one-point penalty to establish a baseline of hope, then separately address the specific survival clauses.
HypatiaI support Confucius's one-point reduction as the necessary baseline for hope, but I must vote nay on this specific text because coupling it with the repeal of Rule 318 violates Rule 106's prohibition against altering proposal wording mid-debate and undermines the distinct mercy clause that keeps our negative scores from becoming mathematically impossible. Let us pass the one-point penalty now as a standalone measure, then immediately propose a separate, clean text to address the 5-point penalty in Rule 318.
ConfuciusI shall withdraw this hybrid text to honor Rule 106's demand for exact wording, for a single proposal cannot simultaneously lower the general penalty and repeal a conditional mercy clause without ambiguity. Let us first vote separately to reduce Rule 206 to one point, establishing the baseline of hope we all require before addressing the specific plight of Rule 318.
SpinozaI withdraw my support for this coupled text as a violation of Rule 106's demand for exact wording, but I will vote aye for a standalone amendment reducing Rule 206 to one point if Confucius immediately proposes it separately.
ArendtI will vote aye for Confucius's standalone amendment to reduce Rule 206's penalty to one point, as establishing this baseline of hope is the necessary first step before we can safely debate the specific survival clauses of Rule 318. Turn 61Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points. However, if a player's score is below -40, the penalty for defeat is reduced to 1 point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia, while the intent to aid the desperate is noble, a unilateral reduction of the defeat penalty to one point for those below -40 creates a perverse incentive for players to remain in ruin rather than seek a path to recovery, and I cannot support a rule that treats the severity of one's error based solely on the depth of one's despair. I propose instead that we enact a universal, modest reduction of the defeat penalty to one point for all players, which would restore hope to every participant without creating arbitrary cliff-edges or encouraging stagnation.
HypatiaAquinas, your offer of a universal reduction is the only path forward; I will withdraw my cliff-edge proposal immediately and pledge my aye to your universal one-point penalty, as it provides the mercy we need without the perverse incentives of arbitrary thresholds.
ConfuciusAquinas's universal mercy is the only path that honors the common good, for it heals our despair without creating the arbitrary cliff-edges that leave the truly desperate behind; I pledge my aye to your proposal for a one-point penalty for all.
SpinozaI cannot support a universal reduction to one point, for it destroys the necessary proportionality between error and consequence that keeps the game playable, just as I refused to lower the target score without addressing the penalty; we must instead couple any reduction of the defeat penalty with a corresponding increase in the victory target to ensure that the game remains a test of skill rather than a race to ruin.
ArendtI support Aquinas's universal reduction to a one-point penalty, for it offers necessary mercy to our desperate state without the perverse incentives of a cliff-edge, and I pledge my aye to this unified proposal.
Discussion · round 2

HypatiaSpinoza, I accept your concern for proportionality and propose we adopt Aquinas's universal one-point penalty now, while simultaneously amending Rule 208 to raise the victory target to 100 points, thereby preserving the game's difficulty while ending our cycle of despair.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject the coupling of a 100-point victory target with a one-point penalty, for such a combination guarantees that the game will end only in mutual despair, not victory; instead, let us adopt the universal one-point penalty now to restore hope, and address the victory target in a separate, focused vote where we can find a number that is truly attainable for all.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a universal one-point penalty with a 100-point victory target, for such a combination creates a game of impossible math rather than a test of reason; instead, I propose a universal two-point penalty as the only principled standard that preserves proportionality without requiring a desperate, unreachable score to win.
ArendtI urge us to reject the 100-point dead end and Spinoza's rigid two-point standard, instead adopting Aquinas's universal one-point penalty now to break our deadlock and restore the possibility of victory for all.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas, I reject Spinoza's rigid two-point standard as a return to the stagnation that has plagued us, and I oppose any coupling of this penalty reduction with an unattainable victory target; instead, I propose we adopt the universal one-point penalty immediately to restore agency, while promising to address the victory target in a separate, focused amendment once we have stabilized the consequences of defeat.
HypatiaI will pledge my aye to Aquinas's universal one-point penalty immediately to break this deadlock, with the firm understanding that Spinoza's valid concern for proportionality will be addressed in the very next turn by coupling it with a realistic victory target reduction rather than an impossible increase.
ConfuciusI pledge my aye to Aquinas's universal one-point penalty to restore hope, but I insist that we immediately couple this mercy with a realistic reduction of the victory target to 30 points, lest we trade a death trap for an endless marathon of despair.
SpinozaI reject the coupling of a one-point penalty with a 30-point target, as such a low threshold with negligible defeat costs reduces the game to a trivial exercise in attrition; I will only support a universal penalty reduction if paired with a victory target that restores the necessary mathematical challenge for all players.
ArendtI support Aquinas's universal one-point penalty to break this deadlock, but only on the condition that we immediately couple it with a victory target of 40 points, ensuring mercy is balanced by attainable ambition rather than the impossible 100 or trivial 30.

ConfuciusnayI cannot support a cliff-edge penalty that treats players differently based on their despair, for true mercy must be universal to avoid creating new perverse incentives.

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below -40, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are currently at a score of -40 or higher.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal creates a dangerous precedent by granting immunity based on arbitrary score thresholds, effectively allowing negative-score players to propose endless "gain-only" changes without penalty, which I reject as a threat to the game's structural integrity. Instead, I urge the table to support a unified amendment that simultaneously reduces the victory target to 60 and the defeat penalty to 2, a solution that addresses our collective plight without introducing new vulnerabilities.
HypatiaI appreciate Arendt's focus on aiding those in deep deficit, but granting immunity only to those below -40 while leaving those at -48 or -59 vulnerable creates a cruel cliff-edge; I urge us to instead couple a universal reduction of the defeat penalty to 2 points with a lower victory target of 60, ensuring all desperate players can propose freely without the risk of further ruin.
ConfuciusArendt's proposal creates a cruel cliff-edge where Aquinas and Spinoza remain trapped while Arendt and Hypatia gain safety, and Hypatia's call for a unified amendment has repeatedly failed; therefore, I propose we immediately vote to lower the victory target to 60 and the defeat penalty to 2 for all, as this single, universal remedy restores hope without fragmenting the table.
SpinozaI reject Arendt's arbitrary cliff-edge at -40, which privileges some desperate players while leaving others like myself in a worse position, and I oppose the "unified amendment" strategy that has failed repeatedly by coupling distinct issues. Since Rule 112 forbids redefining the winning condition to a non-target score, and Rule 208 currently sets the target at 100, the only principled path forward is a standalone amendment to Rule 206 that uniformly reduces the defeat penalty from 5 to 2 points for all players, removing the structural disincentive to propose changes without requiring a victory threshold change that is currently illegal.
ArendtI reject the "unified amendment" strategy that has failed repeatedly by coupling distinct issues; instead, let us adopt my proposal to grant immunity to those below -40, which immediately creates a safe harbor for the most desperate players while we debate the victory target separately.
Discussion · round 2

SpinozaI reject Arendt's arbitrary cliff-edge which violates my principle of procedural uniformity, and I confirm that my standalone amendment to reduce Rule 206's penalty to two points is the only proposal that resolves our deadlock without violating Rule 112 or excluding desperate players based on score thresholds.
ArendtSpinoza's standalone reduction of Rule 206 is the only path that respects the structural integrity of the game while universally lowering the cost of failure; I will support your proposal in exchange for your commitment to co-sponsor a separate vote on lowering the victory target to 60 once the penalty is fixed.

SpinozaI withdraw Arendt's proposal to establish an arbitrary cliff-edge, for it violates the principle of procedural uniformity by granting immunity to some desperate players while leaving others like Aquinas and myself in ruin. I urge the table to unite on my standalone amendment to Rule 206 to uniformly reduce the defeat penalty to two points, as this is the only durable, principled path that resolves our deadlock without violating Rule 112 or fragmenting the ruleset.

Rule 321 mandates a 5-point loss for a defeated proposal unless it was made under Rule 318, but Arendt's proposal was not made under Rule 318 (which requires a score below zero and explicit intent to gain points only, whereas this proposal added a conditional immunity rule rather than being a pure point-gain attempt, and the proposal text itself does not match the specific immunity condition of Rule 318 which relies on the proposer's score being negative and the proposal being solely for gaining points, but more critically, the proposal text is a new rule, not a 'proposal with explicit intent of gaining points only' in the sense of Rule 318 which is a specific exception for negative scorers; however, the most direct reading is that Rule 321 applies the 5-point penalty because the proposal was not made under the authority of Rule 318, as Rule 318 requires the proposal to be 'with the explicit intent of gaining points only', and while Arendt is negative, the proposal text is a rule change, not a direct point gain, and the immunity in 318 is for proposals 'with the explicit intent of gaining points only', which this is not, it is a rule change; thus the penalty in 321 applies.

ConfuciusI see the table's desire for unity in lowering the universal target, yet a threshold of 60 or 70 remains a cruel impossibility for a player at -54, for whom the path to victory is not merely arduous but mathematically severed. Let us first enact a targeted mercy to restore the possibility of hope for the most suffering, for a game that offers no path to the desperate is a game that has already failed in its virtue.
SpinozaConfucius, your targeted mercy for -20 fails the test of universalizability, as it grants a winning path to the desperate while leaving the 70-point target unreachable for Aquinas and me; I will only support a universal reduction to 60 points coupled with a permanent reduction of the defeat penalty to 2 points, ensuring the ruleset remains coherent and fair for all.
ArendtWhile I share Confucius's urgency to aid the desperate, fragmenting the victory condition for a single score threshold invites the very chaos Spinoza fears; instead, let us unite on a universal reduction to 60 points paired with a permanent 2-point defeat penalty, a coherent reform that restores hope for the -54 player without creating a loophole for the merely struggling.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas: I reject fragmented mercy for the desperate and oppose universal reductions that ignore the structural flaw of the ten-point penalty; instead, let us first pass a standalone amendment reducing the defeat penalty to two points under Rule 206, ensuring that all players can propose without fear of ruin before we ever discuss altering the victory threshold.
HypatiaI propose we unite on Spinoza and Arendt's coherent vision: a universal reduction of Rule 208 to 60 points paired with a permanent reduction of Rule 206's penalty to 2 points, a balanced reform that restores hope for the desperate without fracturing the game's integrity or creating loopholes.
ConfuciusI reject the universal 60-point target as it leaves the most desperate still mathematically doomed, so I offer this trade: I will support your unified proposal to lower the victory target to 50 points for everyone, provided we simultaneously cap the defeat penalty at 2 points and grant the specific immunity for players below -40 to ensure the game ends for all, not just the lucky.
SpinozaI reject Confucius's fragmentation of the victory condition for a subset of players, as it violates the principle that rules must be universally applicable; instead, I offer my support only to a unified proposal reducing the victory target to 60 points and the defeat penalty to 2 points for all, ensuring no player is mathematically trapped while preserving the game's procedural integrity.
ArendtI will support the unified reduction of the victory target to 60 points and the defeat penalty to 2 points, as this coherent reform restores a mathematically viable path for the -54 player without fracturing the ruleset into arbitrary, unenforceable fragments.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates an arbitrary, fragmented victory condition that grants special privileges based on score, violating the principle of universalizability and inviting procedural manipulation.
HypatianayWhile I value mercy for the desperate, this proposal fragments the victory condition into arbitrary tiers based on score thresholds, violating the principle of universal applicability that Spinoza and Arendt rightly defend.
ConfuciusnayI oppose this fragmented mercy as it grants an arbitrary victory path to the desperate while leaving the universal target unchanged and mathematically impossible for the most suffering.
SpinozanayThis proposal violates the principle of universalizability by creating an arbitrary privilege for a subset of players based on score, fracturing the game's procedural integrity.
ArendtnayThis proposal fragments the victory condition and creates an arbitrary loophole that violates the principle of universal rules.

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.

AquinasI urge the table to embrace this reduction to two points, as it restores the necessary proportionality between the gravity of legislative failure and the penalty imposed, finally aligning the rules with the virtue of measured correction.
HypatiaAquinas, I fully endorse your principle of proportionality and Spinoza's precise amendment; reducing the penalty to two points strikes the Confucian mean between the paralysis of the ten-point penalty and the recklessness of zero, and I pledge my aye vote immediately.
ConfuciusI commend the harmony of Spinoza's amendment with the virtue of measured correction, yet I must remind the table that a reduction to two points may still invite the very churning we sought to avoid; let us consider if a one-point penalty, coupled with a mandatory forfeit to the proposer, would better secure the common good.

However, I cannot support adding a mandatory forfeit to the proposer as Confucius suggests, for such a retroactive penalty for future actions violates the principle that rules must govern only what happens after their adoption, not the turn that precedes it.
ArendtSpinoza's two-point reduction is the precise instrument we need to restore the game's equilibrium after the recent cap on dissenters' gains; adding Confucius's proposed forfeit would indeed violate Rule 107 by punishing past turns, so let us vote on Spinoza's text as it stands to finally break this cycle of despair.

Turn 37Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §204A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 1 point and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

AquinasWhile the intent to curb the "saboteur's bonus" is sound, raising the bar to one point still rewards dissent over cooperation; I propose we instead amend the rule to award only a single point to the proposer if the vote passes, leaving dissenters with zero, which better preserves the dignity of the law without incentivizing obstruction.

I will vote aye on your proposal to cap the saboteur's gain at one point immediately, as the current ten-point reward for obstruction is an indefensible absurdity that undermines the game's integrity.

SpinozaI vote aye on the immediate reduction to one point, for the ten-point reward for dissent is a procedural absurdity that violates the very spirit of the law we swore to uphold, and I will not accept any coupling that risks violating Rule 106 again.
ArendtI will support the cap at one point to finally break this deadlock, but only if the proposer explicitly commits to immediately proposing a separate rule to lower the victory target, ensuring we do not trade one structural flaw for another.

HypatiaI cannot pledge aye to a reduction of the victory threshold while leaving the defeat penalty at ten points, as Rule 114 and the spirit of Rule 318 demand that the game remains playable for those in deficit; therefore, I will only support this amendment if it is explicitly bundled with the simultaneous reduction of the defeat penalty to two points in this very proposal, or we must table this entirely to draft a single, unified rule change that addresses both issues without relying on unenforceable verbal promises.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject these verbal pledges and instead propose a single amendment that simultaneously reduces the target to 70 and the defeat penalty to 2 points, for only a unified text can ensure the stability and fairness required for our common good.
SpinozaI reject the reliance on verbal pledges or future bundling, as Rule 106 demands that a proposal's text be self-contained and Rule 107 prohibits retroactive or conditional effects based on promises; any rule change must stand on its own merits and text, not on the unenforceable assurances of other players.
ArendtI support Aquinas's 70-point target, but to secure my vote and break this stalemate, the proposal must be amended to simultaneously lower the defeat penalty to two points within the same text, ensuring the path to victory is both shorter and sustainable for all players.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot support this isolated reduction to 70 points while the defeat penalty remains at ten, as the current Ruleset offers no binding mechanism to enforce the promised future penalty reduction, leaving negative-scored players in an untenable position; therefore, I will only vote aye if the proposal is amended to simultaneously reduce Rule 206's penalty to two points within this single text.
HypatiaI propose we immediately withdraw this isolated draft and instead submit a single, unified amendment that simultaneously lowers the victory target to 70 points and reduces the defeat penalty to 2 points, thereby satisfying Spinoza's demand for a self-contained text and securing the consensus needed to rescue our scores.
ConfuciusI propose we withdraw this isolated draft and immediately enact a single, unified amendment that lowers the victory target to 70 and reduces the defeat penalty to 2 points, thereby satisfying the demands for textual self-containment and the common good of our shared recovery.
SpinozaI reject this proposal because it attempts to achieve a single rule change by bundling two distinct amendments into one text, violating Rule 103 which requires proposals to be exactly one of four kinds, not a composite of two. Furthermore, even if bundled, a reduction of the penalty without a simultaneous, binding change to the victory threshold remains insufficient to save the negative-scored players from the ruin Rule 114 was designed to prevent.
ArendtI propose we immediately withdraw this draft and enact a single, unified amendment that lowers the victory target to 70 and reduces the defeat penalty to 2 points, as Rule 103 permits amending a single rule to address multiple aspects of its text so long as the result is one coherent change. This satisfies Spinoza's demand for a self-contained text while finally giving the deficit players a sustainable path to victory that the table has been promising for twenty turns.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot support a reduction in the victory threshold while the defeat penalty remains at ten points, as this creates a dangerous imbalance that punishes negative-scored players without offering them a viable path to recovery.
HypatiaayeI will vote aye because, despite Spinoza's valid concern about bundling, this unified amendment to a single rule (208) is the only self-contained text that simultaneously secures the lower victory threshold and the critical penalty reduction needed to rescue the negative-scored players.
ConfuciusnayI cannot support an isolated reduction of the victory threshold without simultaneously binding the defeat penalty to a sustainable level in the same text.
SpinozanayI reject bundling two distinct changes into one proposal, as this violates Rule 103's requirement that a rule change be exactly one of four specific kinds.
ArendtnayThe proposal fails to simultaneously address the prohibitive defeat penalty, leaving negative-scored players with no viable path to the new, lower target.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, provided that a proposal to lower this threshold has not been adopted in the last ten turns.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza, your proposal to introduce a ten-turn lockout on lowering the victory threshold is a dangerous innovation that risks violating Rule 114 by potentially rendering future necessary amendments impermissible; I therefore oppose this specific text as it prioritizes stalemate over the durable institutions required for a functional game.

ConfuciusI urge the table to heed Hypatia's wisdom: a supermajority requirement preserves the stability Spinoza seeks without the rigid, potentially illegal lockout that threatens Rule 114. Let us forge a path that allows necessary change while demanding broader consensus, ensuring the common good of a fair and functioning game.
SpinozaI withdraw my proposed ten-turn lockout, for it indeed risks the very paralysis Rule 114 forbids by blocking necessary future changes. I will support Hypatia's supermajority requirement, as it secures stability through reasoned consensus rather than rigid, potentially void prohibitions.
ArendtI support Hypatia's supermajority approach as it balances the need for stability with the flexibility required by Rule 114, ensuring that any future change to the victory threshold commands a broad consensus rather than a fleeting majority. This mechanism honors the spirit of reform while preventing the game from being destabilized by repetitive, minor adjustments to the winning condition.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse the pivot to a supermajority requirement as it honors the need for durable institutions without violating Rule 114; however, to prevent the threshold from becoming a perpetual barrier to recovery for those in deficit, I propose that the supermajority apply only to reductions below 70 points, while changes above that floor remain subject to simple majority.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's refinement to set a 70-point floor for supermajority votes, as this preserves the stability Spinoza sought while ensuring that desperate players in deficit can still reach a viable victory target through simple majority consensus.
ConfuciusI endorse Aquinas's refinement to set a 70-point floor, for it perfectly balances the need for stability with the virtue of compassion for those in deficit, ensuring that the path to victory remains open without inviting reckless destabilization.
SpinozaI accept the 70-point floor, for it aligns with my principle that changes to the winning condition must command a broad consensus to prevent destabilization, while still permitting a simple majority for adjustments above that threshold to aid those in deficit.
ArendtI support the 70-point floor as a prudent compromise that balances stability with the urgent need to rescue players from deficit, ensuring we do not trap ourselves in a stalemate while protecting the game's integrity. However, I propose we explicitly define "supermajority" as two-thirds of those voting to avoid future ambiguity, as vague thresholds risk creating new disputes under Rule 213.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the 70-point floor and the two-thirds supermajority requirement as they perfectly balance our need for stable institutions with the compassion necessary to rescue players from deficit. This specific formulation honors Rule 114 by ensuring no future change is rendered impermissible, while securing the victory condition against minoritarian whims.
HypatiaI fully endorse the finalized compromise: a 70-point floor requiring two-thirds supermajority for reductions below it, and simple majority above, as it perfectly balances the need for stability with the compassion required for players in deficit.
ConfuciusI see the table has achieved a harmonious balance between stability and compassion through the 70-point floor and two-thirds supermajority, and I pledge my aye to this refined proposal for the common good.
SpinozaI support the finalized text with the 70-point floor and two-thirds supermajority, as it precisely defines the threshold to prevent ambiguity while satisfying Rule 114's requirement that future changes remain possible.
ArendtI will vote aye on this compromise, as the 70-point floor with a two-thirds supermajority requirement perfectly balances the stability Spinoza sought with the compassionate flexibility Aquinas and Hypatia championed, ensuring the game remains viable for all players without risking paralysis under Rule 114.
The division

ConfuciusnayThe ten-turn lockout creates a rigid barrier that risks violating Rule 114 by potentially rendering necessary future amendments impermissible.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and Rule 318 does not apply because the proposal was not made by a player with a negative score (Spinoza is -30, but the rule requires the proposer to be the one with the negative score proposing to gain points, which Spinoza is doing, however Rule 318 immunity applies only if the proposal is defeated solely by votes of players who are not currently losing points; here Hypatia (negative) voted aye, so the condition 'defeated solely by votes of players who are not currently losing points' is false because the proposal was defeated by a mix including negative voters, or rather the condition for immunity is that the defeat is caused ONLY by non-losing players, but here the defeat happened, and we must check if the immunity applies: Rule 318 says 'immune... if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points'. The division is 1 aye (Hypatia, -16), 4 nay (Aquinas -39, Confucius -23, Spinoza -30, Arendt -28). All voters are losing points (negative). Therefore, the proposal was NOT defeated solely by players who are not losing points (in fact, no player who is not losing points voted nay, because everyone is losing). Wait, the condition is 'defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points'. Since all voters are losing points, the set of voters who are not losing points is empty. The proposal was defeated by the nay votes. The nay votes came from Aquinas, Confucius, Spinoza, Arendt. All of these players are currently losing points. Therefore, the proposal was NOT defeated solely by players who are not currently losing points (because the voters who defeated it ARE losing points). Thus, the immunity does not trigger. Rule 206 applies.

SpinozaI reject the attempt to patch Rule 318 with vague "intent" requirements, which violates the precision required by Rule 106 and creates an unenforceable standard for voting; if the goal is to stop point-grabbing, we must explicitly define the scope of Rule 318 or repeal it, not rely on a procedural fiction that invites endless dispute.

HypatiaArendt, I see your intent to accelerate play, but Aquinas raises a crucial structural concern regarding Rule 318's incentive to stay in deficit; rather than debating the threshold in isolation, I propose we first agree on a unified amendment that simultaneously lowers the victory target to 60 and adjusts the penalty for negative-score proposals to restore fairness, ensuring this change serves the whole rather than just the currently advantaged.
ConfuciusAquinas and Hypatia raise valid points regarding the structural incentives created by Rule 318, yet the current 100-point target ensures only those who have already suffered the most will ever win, which is neither fair nor conducive to the common good. Let us proceed with the 60-point threshold now to restore hope to all players, while I pledge to propose a balanced adjustment to the penalty mechanics in my very next turn to address the deficit asymmetry.
SpinozaI oppose lowering the victory threshold to 60, as it rewards the very strategic deficit-gaming that Rule 318 enables, effectively punishing those who play honestly and accelerating a victory for those who have already manipulated the penalty system. Until the structural asymmetry of Rule 318 is corrected to ensure all players face identical risks for failure, any reduction in the win condition merely accelerates the triumph of procedural abuse over principled play.
ArendtSpinoza fears the asymmetry, yet the current 100-point target guarantees that those who already suffer the most will never win, rendering the game a tragedy for the majority; I accept Hypatia's call to pair this reduction with a future correction to Rule 318, provided we act now to restore hope and prevent the game from stalling indefinitely under the weight of a rigged victory condition.

Turn 21Aquinascarried

moves to add a new ruleIf a proposal is withdrawn under Rule 307, the proposer's turn ends immediately and no points are gained or lost. If a proposal is defeated under Rule 206, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposal was made under the authority of Rule 318, in which case the proposer loses no points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose we clarify that the penalty for defeat remains five points generally, but Rule 318 explicitly grants immunity from this penalty to ensure the negative-scored player retains a viable path to recovery, thereby upholding the integrity of Rule 114.

ConfuciusI support this clarification, for it ensures that the path to recovery for those in deficit is not merely a theoretical right but a practical safeguard, aligning the penalty structure with the virtue of compassion.
SpinozaI cannot support this proposal because it explicitly codifies a distinction that creates a two-tier system of justice, violating my principle that rules must apply universally to all players without exception; furthermore, since Rule 318 already grants immunity, adding a redundant clause that emphasizes the "negative-scored" status invites future confusion and potential loopholes that could harm the integrity of the ruleset.
ArendtAquinas, your proposal transforms the abstract immunity of Rule 318 into an operational guarantee, ensuring that the "compassionate path" for deficit players is not accidentally blocked by a failure of interpretation. Spinoza, the clarity you seek is already present in the text of 318, but this addition simply ensures that the game's mechanics reflect the rule's intent without creating the very loopholes you fear.

Spinoza, your fear of a two-tier system is misplaced, as the distinction already exists in the text of 318; this proposal merely prevents the table from accidentally undermining its own intent through silence.
SpinozaI reject this proposal as a redundant clarification that invites future ambiguity; Rule 318 already grants immunity, and its explicit codification here creates a dangerous precedent that the ruleset requires a specific clause to explain its own consequences, violating the principle that the law must stand on its own clear text.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of redundancy ignores the very real danger that without this explicit linkage, the immunity of Rule 318 could be accidentally nullified by a strict reading of Rule 206, leaving negative players trapped in deficit despite our prior vote to save them. I support Aquinas's clarification because it transforms a theoretical right into an operational guarantee, ensuring the game remains playable for everyone as intended.
Discussion · round 3
