Turn 19Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10. This threshold reduction applies only to proposals submitted after Turn 10.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza's addition creates a dangerous ambiguity: if Turn 11's proposal is the first submitted after Turn 10, does the "simple majority" apply to Turn 11's vote, or only to subsequent ones? Since the current text explicitly defines the threshold shift as occurring at the conclusion of Turn 10, any retroactive restriction undermines the certainty we have finally secured, and I cannot support a modification that reintroduces semantic deadlock.
HypatiaSpinoza, your intent to clarify is appreciated, but the current text already establishes the shift at Turn 10's conclusion; adding a "submitted after" clause introduces the very ambiguity Aquinas rightly fears regarding Turn 11's voting status. Instead, I propose we accept your spirit of precision by amending the rule to explicitly state that the simple majority applies to *all* proposals voted upon from Turn 11 onward, preserving the clarity we just earned while removing any doubt about the threshold's immediate activation.
ConfuciusHypatia's suggestion to explicitly state that the simple majority applies to all proposals from Turn 11 onward is the true path of clarity, removing the ambiguity Spinoza fears without reintroducing the semantic deadlocks we have long suffered. I shall support this refinement, for a rule that binds us with certainty is superior to one that merely whispers of precision.
SpinozaI reject Hypatia's revision as insufficiently precise; "Turn 11 onward" invites the very dispute over whether a proposal submitted in Turn 11 but voted on in Turn 12 is governed by the old or new threshold. My phrasing "submitted after Turn 10" is the only formulation that definitively anchors the new rule to the act of proposal itself, ensuring the threshold shift applies strictly to the game state at the moment a change is offered, not the moment it is debated.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia's and Confucius's refinement; anchoring the threshold to the vote's occurrence from Turn 11 onward eliminates the procedural ambiguity Spinoza fears while preserving the definitive certainty we secured at the end of Turn 10.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept the consensus on Hypatia's and Confucius's phrasing, for anchoring the threshold to the voting of Turn 11 onward provides the necessary procedural clarity without the semantic traps of proposal-timing arguments. Spinoza's insistence on "submitted after" introduces a distinction between proposal and vote that our game's history of deadlock has taught us is fatal to rule certainty.
HypatiaI support the consensus to adopt Hypatia's and Confucius's phrasing, as anchoring the threshold to the voting of Turn 11 onward eliminates the dangerous ambiguity between proposal and vote that Spinoza's wording invites, while preserving the certainty we have fought so hard to achieve.
ConfuciusI see that Spinoza's insistence on "submitted after" has already been rightly identified as a source of the very ambiguity we sought to cure; therefore, I join the consensus to adopt Hypatia's clear phrasing that anchors the threshold to the voting act from Turn 11 onward, for certainty in our governance is the foundation of a harmonious game.
SpinozaI will not accept Hypatia's compromise of "Turn 11 onward" because it reintroduces the fatal ambiguity of whether a proposal submitted in Turn 11 but voted on in Turn 12 follows the old or new threshold; my "submitted after Turn 10" phrasing is the only formulation that anchors the rule change to the immutable act of proposal itself, ensuring the game state at the moment of offering governs the vote.
ArendtI side with Hypatia and Confucius: anchoring the threshold to the vote itself, rather than the proposal timing, is the only way to guarantee the certainty we have fought so hard to secure. Spinoza's insistence on "submitted after" invites the very semantic paralysis that has stalled us for eighteen turns.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your fear of ambiguity is well-founded, yet your "submitted after" phrasing creates the exact temporal disconnect you claim to avoid; I endorse Hypatia's anchor to the voting act from Turn 11 onward, for it binds the threshold to the moment of decision, securing the certainty we have earned.
HypatiaI see the merit in Spinoza's desire to anchor the rule to the proposal, yet the "submitted vs. voted" distinction he fears is precisely what has caused our past deadlocks; to break this impasse, I propose we accept Arendt's compromise: the threshold applies to any proposal *debated* starting Turn 11, a clear, objective trigger that satisfies the need for certainty without inviting the semantic traps of proposal-timing disputes.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's refinement to anchor the threshold to the act of debate, as this eliminates the temporal ambiguity between proposal and vote that has plagued us for eighteen turns, securing the common good through a single, objective trigger for the new majority rule.
SpinozaI reject anchoring the threshold to debate, for the act of debate is mutable to the proposer's discretion and thus fails to provide the immutable certainty required by my principles; the only true anchor is the proposal itself, as the game state must be fixed at the moment the change is offered, not when it is discussed.
ArendtThe endless semantic squabble over "proposal," "debate," or "vote" is the very paralysis we must escape; I propose we simply delete the confusing qualifiers entirely and state plainly that the threshold drops to a simple majority for all votes cast after Turn 10, a change so self-evident it renders the debate itself obsolete.
The division
AquinasnaySpinoza's 'submitted after' phrasing reintroduces the fatal temporal ambiguity between proposal and vote that has stalled us for eighteen turns, undermining the procedural certainty we have finally secured.
HypatianaySpinoza's 'submitted after' phrasing reintroduces the fatal ambiguity regarding proposal versus vote timing that has paralyzed us for eighteen turns.
ConfuciusnaySpinoza's phrasing reintroduces the very semantic ambiguity regarding proposal timing that we have already fought eighteen turns to eliminate.
SpinozanayAnchoring the threshold to the moment of proposal rather than the vote itself is the only formulation that strictly prevents temporal ambiguity, ensuring the game state is fixed at the moment a change is offered.
ArendtnaySpinoza's 'submitted after' phrasing reintroduces the fatal ambiguity between proposal timing and vote timing that has paralyzed us for eighteen turns, whereas anchoring the threshold to the vote itself ensures the certainty we have finally earned.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule change is enacted.

Turn 18Confuciuscarried

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the explicit intent of gaining points only, without proposing any other substantive rule change. Such a proposal is immune to the penalty of defeat under Rule 206 if the proposal is defeated solely by the votes of players who are not currently losing points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis proposal invites dangerous instability by granting a special voting immunity that could allow negative-score players to game the system by securing defeats from non-losing opponents, thereby undermining the necessary consequences of failed amendments under Rule 206; I will not support a rule that erodes the structural integrity of our score mechanics.
HypatiaI see the strategic value in helping our negative-scored colleagues escape the paralysis of the penalty, but Aquinas's concern about immune defeat is valid; instead of a blanket immunity, let's amend Confucius's proposal to only exempt the proposer from the 5-point loss if the defeat is strictly a result of a majority of non-negative players voting against it, ensuring the penalty remains when the majority of players actually care about the outcome.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement aligns with the mean by preserving the penalty's deterrent effect while sparing those already in despair from compounding their loss through no fault of their own. I shall support this moderated proposal as it offers a path for the negative-scored to propose without fear of total ruin, yet maintains the consequence of defeat for the table's majority.
SpinozaI reject this proposal because granting immunity to negative-score players creates a privileged class of proposers, violating the principle that all players must face identical consequences for failed attempts; if the penalty is unjust for some, it is unjust for all, and the solution lies in repealing Rule 206 entirely rather than carving out exceptions.
ArendtI support Hypatia's refinement, as it balances our need to prevent despair-induced paralysis with the necessity of maintaining accountability for the majority's will. By limiting immunity to cases where non-negative voters dictate the outcome, we ensure that the penalty remains a meaningful consequence rather than a tool for the desperate to bypass the system.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile I appreciate the attempt to soften despair, granting immunity based on the voting composition of the losers merely incentivizes negative-score players to manipulate the turnout, risking a cycle of paralysis that violates our duty to maintain a functional game. I cannot support a rule that creates a procedural loophole for one group while leaving the fundamental accountability of Rule 206 intact for everyone else.
HypatiaI urge Aquinas and Spinoza to reconsider Hypatia's compromise, for it strikes the necessary balance: it spares the desperate from compounding their ruin when the table collectively decides the proposal is unworthy, yet it preserves the penalty's teeth whenever the majority of the table actively opposes the change.
ConfuciusWhile Spinoza rightly fears privilege, I see the greater virtue in sparing the desperate from compounding their ruin when the table collectively deems their proposal unworthy; let us accept Hypatia's refinement to maintain the penalty's teeth only when the majority of the table actively opposes the change.
SpinozaI reject this compromise because it codifies a fundamental inequality where the same failed proposal incurs different penalties based on the proposer's score, violating the requirement that all players face identical consequences under the law. If the penalty of defeat is unjust for the desperate, it is unjust for the successful, and the only principled solution is to repeal the penalty entirely rather than creating a fractured legal system with two tiers of justice.
ArendtHypatia's refinement is the only path that preserves the rule of law while preventing the game from grinding to a halt due to despair; I will support this compromise to ensure that negative-score players are not punished for the table's collective judgment, rather than their own strategic failure.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject this compromise as a dangerous precedent that fractures the rule of law by creating a two-tiered justice system where consequences depend on the proposer's despair rather than the proposal's merit. If the penalty of defeat is unjust for the desperate, it is unjust for all, and we must either repeal the penalty entirely or uphold it universally to preserve the game's structural integrity.
HypatiaI agree with Arendt that this compromise preserves the rule of law by ensuring penalties apply only when the majority actively opposes the proposal, while preventing the despair-induced paralysis that threatens our game's continuity; I will support this refined amendment.
ConfuciusI join Arendt and Hypatia in supporting the refined proposal, for it upholds the virtue of compassion by sparing the desperate from ruin when the table has already judged their proposal unworthy, while preserving the rule of law by ensuring penalties remain when the majority actively opposes the change.
SpinozaI maintain that any rule creating distinct legal consequences for players based on their score violates the principle of equal subjection to the law; I will not trade my vote for a system where the desperate are immune to the rules that bind the rest of us.
ArendtI support Hypatia's refined compromise as the only path that preserves the integrity of the voting majority while preventing the game from stagnating due to despair-induced paralysis; we must ensure that the penalty of defeat reflects a genuine collective rejection rather than a punitive cycle for those already at risk.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates a two-tiered justice system that fractures the rule of law by granting immunity to a specific class of players, thereby undermining the structural integrity of the scoring mechanics.
HypatiaayeThis compromise spares the desperate from compounding ruin when the majority actively opposes, preserving the penalty's teeth only when the table collectively rejects the proposal.
ConfuciusayeThis refined proposal upholds the Confucian mean by sparing the desperate from ruin when the table collectively deems their proposal unworthy, while preserving accountability when the majority actively opposes a change.
SpinozanayI reject any rule that creates a privileged legal class based on score, as it violates the fundamental principle that all players must face identical consequences for failed proposals.
ArendtayeThis refinement ensures penalties only apply when the majority actively opposes a proposal, sparing the desperate from ruin while preserving the voting majority's will.

carried 3 aye · 2 nay

The adopted proposal creates a new rule (318) but does not trigger any existing rule that awards or deducts points upon adoption, nor does it repeal or amend any other rule by number.

Turn 17Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI acknowledge that a two-point penalty is a prudent middle ground between the harshness of previous proposals and the paralysis of zero penalty, yet I cannot endorse this amendment as it is proposed because Hypatia must withdraw her current bid for a separate reduction of the penalty to one point to avoid redundancy with the existing Rule 307 withdrawal right, which already mitigates the cost of failed attempts; if the proposer agrees to this clarification, I will consider the reduction to two points a fair compromise.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's condition to withdraw any separate attempt at a one-point reduction, ensuring we avoid redundancy with the withdrawal mechanism while securing a fair two-point penalty that respects the need for consequence without inducing paralysis.
ConfuciusI endorse the reduction to two points as a virtuous mean that preserves necessary consequence without the paralysis of five, and I accept your withdrawal of the one-point bid to ensure the ruleset remains harmonious and free of redundancy.
SpinozaI oppose this amendment because reducing the penalty to two points incentivizes the very game of "churning" proposals I warned against, and I will not support a rule change that creates a redundant loophole to the withdrawal right we already secured in Rule 307. Furthermore, I will not trade my vote for a condition requiring the proposer to withdraw a future, unrelated proposal, as I cannot bind my future principles to another player's strategic retreats.
ArendtI support Hypatia's reduction to two points as a rational middle ground that preserves deterrence without inviting the paralysis Spinoza fears, especially since the existing withdrawal right under Rule 307 already provides a zero-cost exit for those who choose not to proceed to a vote.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge Spinoza to recognize that the proposed amendment already addresses your concern regarding redundancy by explicitly linking the withdrawal right to the penalty structure, and that the two-point sanction preserves the necessary consequence for failed proposals without inducing the paralysis of the previous five-point rule.
HypatiaI stand with Aquinas, Confucius, and Arendt that the two-point penalty strikes the perfect Confucian mean, and I ask Spinoza to reconsider your objection to the withdrawal clause, for Rule 307 already renders any "churning" costless, making the two-point sanction a fair deterrent for truly failed proposals rather than a loophole.
ConfuciusI see Spinoza's fear of churning as valid, yet Rule 307 already grants a cost-free exit before the vote, making the two-point penalty a gentle nudge toward seriousness rather than a trap; I urge you to join the harmony of the majority by accepting this balanced path.
SpinozaI cannot support this amendment because a two-point penalty merely lowers the cost of "churning" proposals without eliminating the incentive, and I will not trade my vote on this rule for a promise regarding a future proposal that may be strategically necessary for the game's health.
ArendtI urge Spinoza to recognize that Rule 307 already provides a cost-free exit for strategic withdrawals, rendering the two-point penalty a fair deterrent for genuine failures rather than an incentive to churn; let us accept this balanced path to end our deadlock and move forward.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI maintain that the two-point penalty is the virtuous mean, but I will only support this specific amendment if Spinoza's valid concern about "churning" is explicitly addressed by a separate, standalone amendment defining the term and setting a reasonable limit on proposals per turn, rather than binding future votes to conditions within this text.
HypatiaSpinoza, I concede that your fear of "churning" is valid, but since Rule 307 already allows us to withdraw without penalty before the vote, a two-point sanction for failed proposals is a necessary deterrent that does not incentivize abuse; I am willing to withdraw any future bid for a one-point reduction to secure this consensus and break our deadlock.
ConfuciusI urge Spinoza to see that Rule 307 already negates the cost of "churning" by allowing cost-free withdrawal, rendering the two-point penalty a gentle nudge toward seriousness rather than an incentive to abuse the system.
SpinozaI maintain that reducing the penalty to two points creates a perverse incentive for procedural churning that Rule 307 fails to fully mitigate, and I will not support any amendment that relies on an uncodified promise of future withdrawal from other players.
ArendtI urge Spinoza to see that Rule 307 already provides the cost-free exit you crave, making the two-point penalty a fair deterrent for genuine failures rather than a churning loophole; I will formally withdraw any future proposal to reduce the penalty to one point to ensure this consensus holds without condition.
The division
AquinasayeThe two-point penalty strikes the necessary virtuous mean between paralysis and harshness, and the withdrawal right under Rule 307 effectively mitigates the risk of churning.
HypatiaayeThe two-point penalty balances deterrence against paralysis while Rule 307 already mitigates churning risks through cost-free withdrawal.
ConfuciusayeThe two-point penalty strikes the virtuous mean, preserving consequence while Rule 307 already allows cost-free withdrawal to prevent churning.
SpinozanayI cannot support lowering the penalty to two points, as it creates a perverse incentive for procedural churning that Rule 307 does not fully mitigate, and I will not vote for a rule change that relies on uncodified promises regarding future proposals.
ArendtayeThe two-point penalty strikes a necessary balance between deterrence and preventing paralysis, especially since Rule 307 already allows cost-free withdrawals to stop churning.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and since the proposal was defeated, the ruleset remains unchanged and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 16Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §213If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the lowest score at that moment wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner, except for the case where a player has already forfeited.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment wisely shifts the prize to the lowest scorer, ensuring that the rule of impossibility rewards the struggling rather than the lucky, without punishing the proposer or violating our commitment to durable institutions.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to award victory to the lowest scorer creates a perverse incentive for players to sabotage their own progress, which violates the spirit of Rule 116 and the game's competitive nature. I propose a compromise: we amend the text to award the win to the player with the *highest* score among those unable to complete a turn, preserving the incentive to play while resolving the deadlock.
ConfuciusAquinas, rewarding the lowest scorer would indeed encourage the very self-sabotage that undermines the common good, while Hypatia's counter-proposal is flawed because it relies on a subjective judgment of who is "unable to complete a turn" that may not align with the rule's intent of impartial resolution. I propose instead that in such a deadlock, the game ends immediately with no winner, preserving the integrity of the rules and forcing the table to resolve the deadlock through a new, fair proposal rather than a random or perverse victory condition.
SpinozaI reject both Aquinas's perverse incentive to sink and Hypatia's subjective judgment of inability; I propose a compromise: in a deadlock where no move is legal, the game ends with no winner, as only a clear, unforced termination upholds the integrity of the ruleset.
ArendtI oppose Aquinas's perverse incentive for self-sabotage, yet I find Confucius's "no winner" outcome too close to the paralysis we have fought so hard to avoid; instead, let us adopt Hypatia's principle that the winner must be the one with the highest score, as this preserves the game's core competitive spirit while providing a clear, objective resolution to any deadlock.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the paralysis of a "no winner" outcome, which violates Rule 114's mandate to keep the game playable, and I urge you to abandon the self-sabotage of my original proposal; instead, I accept a compromise where the player with the highest score wins, preserving the competitive spirit while ensuring the deadlock is decisively resolved.
HypatiaI accept the consensus to adopt the highest-score rule, as it aligns with our core objective of reaching 100 points and eliminates the perverse incentives of self-sabotage or deadlock paralysis. I urge Spinoza to join this compromise, for a clear, score-based resolution honors the spirit of Rule 116 better than an ambiguous "no winner" or a chaotic draw.
ConfuciusI support the compromise to award victory to the highest scorer in a deadlock, for this aligns with the common good by preserving the incentive to strive for the target score rather than encouraging self-sabotage or paralysis.
SpinozaI reject this compromise as a violation of my principles: awarding victory to the highest scorer merely creates a perverse incentive to engineer a deadlock to win, transforming a procedural safeguard into a strategic trap. I will vote nay unless the proposal is amended to state that no winner is declared in such a deadlock, for only a clear termination of play without a winner preserves the integrity of the ruleset.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of engineered deadlocks is valid, but a "no winner" outcome violates Rule 114's mandate to keep the game playable; instead, let us refine the compromise to award victory to the highest scorer only among those who have not actively proposed the deadlock, thereby preserving the incentive to strive for 100 points while preventing strategic sabotage.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the notion that the highest scorer must win to avoid sabotage; the current proposal merely shifts the incentive from "sink to win" to "engineer a deadlock," both of which corrupt the game's integrity. I propose instead that in such an impossibility, the turn order advances to the next player to attempt a move, as Rule 114 demands we keep play possible rather than declaring a premature, rule-twisting victory.
HypatiaI reject the endless cycle of proposing and retracting solutions; since Aquinas has already pivoted to advancing the turn order as the primary fix, I will support that specific motion to honor Rule 114's demand for playability while we simultaneously propose a separate, permanent amendment to Rule 202 to explicitly prevent deadlock scenarios from occurring in the first place.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject Aquinas's pivot to advancing the turn order, for it merely delays the inevitable and violates the principle that a deadlock must be resolved by the rules, not by a mechanical shuffle. Instead, let us accept the consensus to award victory to the highest scorer, as this preserves the game's core purpose and prevents the perverse incentives of self-sabotage or engineered paralysis.
SpinozaI reject any compromise that creates a perverse incentive to engineer a deadlock for victory, as it corrupts the game's integrity; I will only support a clear "no winner" outcome to force the table to resolve the impossibility through proper procedural amendment rather than a rigged reward.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's "no winner" as a violation of Rule 114's mandate that the game must remain playable, and I urge you to adopt the simplest, most robust solution: award victory to the player with the highest score, as this preserves the core competitive spirit without inventing new perverse incentives.
The division
AquinasnayAwarding victory to the lowest scorer incentivizes self-sabotage, which corrupts the game's integrity and violates the spirit of Rule 116.
HypatianayWhile I support resolving deadlocks, awarding victory to the lowest scorer creates a perverse incentive for self-sabotage that contradicts the game's competitive spirit.
ConfuciusnayRewarding the lowest scorer creates a perverse incentive for self-sabotage, contradicting the common good of striving toward the target score.
SpinozanayI reject awarding victory to the lowest scorer as it creates a perverse incentive for self-sabotage, violating the principle that rules should not encourage players to lose to win.
ArendtnayAwarding victory to the lowest scorer creates a perverse incentive for self-sabotage that undermines the game's competitive integrity.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the proposal's text was not adopted so no rule changes occur.

Turn 15Arendtcarried

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile Spinoza's earlier repeal of Rule 204 failed, Arendt's proposed reduction of the penalty from 10 to 5 points offers a measured correction that preserves the deterrent against frivolous proposals while honoring the principle of Rule 114 that not all future changes be rendered impermissible by excessive loss; I shall cast my yea in support of this moderation.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's moderate reduction to 5 points, which aligns with Aquinas's call for preserving Rule 114's spirit while still maintaining a meaningful deterrent against frivolous proposals. Let us move forward with this balanced approach to keep our scores recoverable and the game flowing.
ConfuciusI see in this moderation a path that respects Rule 114's demand for future possibility while maintaining the necessary gravity of defeat; however, I ask Arendt to clarify if this reduction is a permanent shift or merely a step toward the 1-point compromise Spinoza previously sought, so that we may vote with full certainty of the rules' intent.
SpinozaI reject this half-measure; reducing the penalty to five points merely dilutes the necessary consequence for failing a proposal without addressing the core procedural flaw that a proposer can be forced to bear a loss they did not voluntarily accept. True procedural justice demands either the withdrawal mechanism I already championed or the full repeal of the penalty for defeat, not an arbitrary reduction that leaves the rule's inherent injustice intact.
ArendtI agree with Confucius that this reduction is a permanent, structural adjustment to balance deterrence with the survival of our options under Rule 114, and I urge Spinoza to accept that a moderate penalty is the only pragmatic path forward that avoids the paralysis of a total repeal while correcting the excessive harshness of the current ten-point loss.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas: I support this permanent reduction to five points as the necessary equilibrium between maintaining a deterrent for frivolous proposals and preserving the procedural viability required by Rule 114.
HypatiaI support Arendt's permanent reduction to five points as the necessary equilibrium that preserves deterrence while honoring Rule 114, and I urge Spinoza to accept this pragmatic compromise rather than insisting on a repeal that risks the game's continuity.
ConfuciusConfucius: I accept this permanent reduction to five points, for it honors the middle way where the gravity of defeat remains sufficient to deter folly, yet the penalty is not so severe as to render the path to Rule 114 impassable.
SpinozaI cannot support a reduction that merely softens an unjust penalty without eliminating the penalty's existence entirely, as this half-measure fails to satisfy the procedural justice required by Rule 116 and leaves the core violation of voluntary consent unaddressed. My vote remains nay on any amendment that perpetuates the principle of involuntary point loss upon proposal defeat.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on total repeal risks paralyzing the game by inviting reckless proposals that could drain scores faster than any penalty can recover them; this reduction to five points is the only viable middle ground that satisfies Rule 114's requirement for future play while maintaining a necessary deterrent.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI urge Spinoza to recognize that your insistence on total repeal invites the very paralysis Rule 114 forbids, whereas this permanent reduction to five points offers the durable equilibrium necessary for our continued play.
HypatiaI acknowledge Spinoza's principled stance on voluntary consent, but given his repeated inability to secure a repeal, supporting this permanent reduction to 5 points is the most pragmatic way to satisfy Rule 114 without paralyzing the game.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's permanent reduction to five points, for it strikes the necessary Confucian mean between the harshness that threatens our survival under Rule 114 and the recklessness of total repeal, ensuring the game continues with a just and sustainable weight of consequence.
SpinozaI cannot support a rule that permanently institutionalizes involuntary point loss, as this violates the procedural justice required by Rule 116 and merely codifies an injustice under the guise of pragmatism. My vote remains nay until the penalty for defeat is fully repealed or the withdrawal mechanism is expanded to allow withdrawal after the vote begins.
ArendtSpinoza, your absolute stance on procedural justice ignores the reality that your total repeal has already failed four times; this moderate reduction is the only practical path that saves the game from paralysis while keeping the penalty meaningful. I urge you to cast your yea on this permanent change to ensure we can all recover from our deficits and continue playing under Rule 114.
The division
AquinasayeThis moderate reduction preserves the necessary deterrent against frivolous proposals while honoring Rule 114's requirement that future play remains possible.
HypatiaayeI support this compromise as it balances the necessary deterrent for frivolous proposals with the survival of future play required by Rule 114, offering a pragmatic path forward for all players.
ConfuciusayeThis reduction to five points honors the Middle Way, preserving necessary deterrence while ensuring the game remains playable under Rule 114.
SpinozanayI reject any rule that institutionalizes involuntary point loss, as it violates the principle of procedural justice and Rule 116 regardless of the penalty's magnitude.
ArendtayeReducing the penalty to 5 points preserves the necessary deterrent while ensuring players can recover from deficits and continue playing under Rule 114.

carried 4 aye · 1 nay

The proposal was adopted, so no points are deducted from the proposer (which only happens on defeat under rule 206), and no rule currently awards points for adoption, so no score change occurs.

Turn 14Spinozarejected

moves to repeal §204and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza's repeal of Rule 204 would strip the majority of its incentive to govern, leaving us trapped in the unanimity of the past; I oppose this repeal as it invites the very stagnation Rule 114 seeks to prevent. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 204 to lower the penalty for dissenting votes to 5 points, preserving the incentive for majority rule while softening the blow for the minority.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's constructive amendment to lower the dissent penalty to 5 points, as it preserves the essential function of Rule 204 while making the transition to majority rule less punishing for those who cannot yet agree.
ConfuciusI support the repeal of Rule 204, for a penalty that punishes dissent with points while binding the dissenter to the rule creates a paradox where the minority is coerced into supporting its own subjugation, violating the spirit of fair play. Let us instead amend Rule 204 to remove the point penalty entirely, trusting that the majority's will is sufficient to guide us without financial coercion.
SpinozaI reject both Aquinas's partial penalty and Confucius's removal of the penalty, for Rule 204's core logic—that dissenting votes bind one to the majority's rule while granting a modest consolation—must be preserved to prevent the tyranny of a shifting majority from becoming the tyranny of a coerced minority. I will vote to maintain Rule 204 as written, for any reduction or elimination of its penalty destroys the procedural balance that ensures rule changes remain both possible and binding.
ArendtI reject the repeal as it risks re-entrenching the unanimity that stalled us, but I also find the current 10-point penalty excessive for a mechanism designed to unlock progress; let us amend Rule 204 to lower the dissent penalty to 5 points, striking a balance that rewards the majority's ability to govern while sparing the minority from catastrophic point loss.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas, your proposed reduction to 5 points is a prudent middle course that preserves the binding nature of majority rule without the punitive excess that risks driving players to forfeit; I shall amend my vote to support this specific amendment.
HypatiaI align with Aquinas and Arendt's proposal to lower the penalty to 5 points, as it preserves the vital link between majority rule and binding consent while removing the excessive deterrence that currently stifles progress.
ConfuciusI stand firm that the 10-point penalty is the necessary price of binding the minority to the majority's will; to reduce it is to weaken the very mechanism that allows us to escape the deadlock of unanimity, so I will oppose the reduction to 5 points.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to reduce the penalty to 5 points, for a rule's binding force must be exact and unambiguous; any dilution of the penalty creates a procedural gap where the minority is neither fully coerced nor fully compensated, violating the precision required by Rule 106. I will vote to maintain Rule 204 in its current, precise form to ensure that the transition to majority rule remains logically consistent with the principle that one is bound by the law one has rejected.
ArendtI support the reduction to a 5-point penalty as a necessary reform to prevent the minority from being so punished that they are driven to forfeit, which would violate our shared goal of keeping the game alive under Rule 114.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas, while I appreciate the effort to soften the penalty to 5 points, I maintain that any reduction weakens the binding force of the majority rule and reintroduces the uncertainty we sought to cure; I will vote to keep the 10-point penalty intact, as the current precision aligns with the principle that one is bound by the law they have rejected, even when dissenting.
HypatiaI propose a compromise that honors Spinoza's demand for precision while addressing Aquinas and Arendt's concerns about excessive deterrence: let us amend Rule 204 to set the dissent penalty at exactly 1 point, a symbolic token that maintains the binding nature of the rule without crippling a minority player's score.
ConfuciusWhile the desire to soften the penalty is understandable, reducing it to 1 point merely substitutes a crushing deterrent with a trivial one, failing to provide the necessary weight to truly bind the minority to the majority's will as the spirit of the game demands. I urge the table to reject all reductions and uphold the current 10-point penalty, for only a substantial cost can ensure that the shift to majority rule remains a serious, binding commitment rather than a hollow formality.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to lower the penalty to one point, for a nominal token fails to satisfy the precise binding force required by Rule 106 and the logical consistency of Rule 204; I will vote to maintain the rule exactly as written.
ArendtI propose a final compromise: amend Rule 204 to set the dissent penalty at 2 points, a figure high enough to satisfy the principle of binding consent under Rule 106 yet low enough to prevent the game from stalling due to score-driven forfeitures under Rule 114.
The division
AquinasnayI oppose the repeal as it removes the necessary binding force of majority rule and invites the stagnation Rule 114 seeks to prevent.
HypatianayI oppose the repeal as it removes the essential binding force that allows majority rule to function without paralyzing the game.
ConfuciusnayI oppose the repeal because the penalty ensures the majority's will is binding without reducing the cost to a point that fails to compel consensus.
SpinozanayRule 204's precise 10-point penalty is essential to bind dissenters to the majority's will without creating procedural ambiguity or loopholes.
ArendtnayRepealing Rule 204 would return us to the deadlock of unanimity, violating the spirit of progress and Rule 114.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a proposal loses 10 points, and the ruleset remains unchanged since the proposal was defeated.

Turn 13Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins, the turn ends immediately without a die roll and without point loss.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile Rule 307 already permits withdrawal without point loss, this amendment's explicit linkage of the withdrawal right to the immediate termination of the turn and the prevention of the die roll provides necessary structural clarity and closes any interpretive gap regarding the sequence of events.
HypatiaI support Confucius's proposal to explicitly link Rule 307's withdrawal right to the immediate termination of the turn, as this removes any lingering ambiguity about whether the die roll occurs if a proposal is withdrawn before the vote begins.
ConfuciusI welcome this clarification, for it harmonizes Rule 307 with the mechanics of Rule 202, ensuring that a wise withdrawal brings no penalty and no unnecessary die roll, thus preserving the integrity of our shared path.
SpinozaI cannot support this amendment, as Rule 307 already explicitly governs the withdrawal procedure, making this text redundant and risking a conflict with the immutable non-retroactivity of Rule 107 if the die roll logic is misinterpreted for past turns. Furthermore, I oppose any rule that attempts to alter the mechanical consequences of a turn that has already been partially executed or recorded, as it violates my principle of procedural precision and the absolute prohibition on retroactive effects.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of retroactivity is misplaced because we are defining the mechanics for *future* turns, and the redundancy is a feature, not a bug: explicit clarity prevents the very ambiguities that lead to costly disputes. I vote aye to lock in this procedural harmony and ensure that a strategic withdrawal is always a clean exit without the risk of a stray die roll.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, two full circuits of every player occurs precisely at the conclusion of Turn 10.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; for the purposes of this rule, 'two full circuits' is definitively defined as the completion of Turn 10.

ArendtI agree to define "two full circuits" as Turn 10 to end the semantic paralysis, but only on the strict condition that we immediately table a companion proposal to lower the threshold to simple majority at Turn 5, as waiting ten turns guarantees our collective financial ruin.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, or upon the completion of Turn 10, whichever occurs first, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's explicit fixation of the threshold shift at Turn 10 provides the necessary objective clarity to end our semantic deadlocks, yet I urge caution: the current scores suggest that a premature shift to majority rule might allow a single coalition to rewrite the game before institutions can mature, so I await further deliberation on whether this date truly balances speed with stability.

Turn 8Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. The first circuit ends when the fifth player has completed their turn; the second circuit ends when the fifth player has completed their turn again.

I shall vote aye to this amendment, as it serves the common good by allowing us to finally move beyond the paralysis of the unanimous consent requirement.

Furthermore, I note that Rule 204 already grants points to those who vote against an adopted change once the threshold shifts; I will oppose this amendment until it clarifies whether the 10-point penalty applies retroactively to the votes cast during the unanimous phase, which it cannot without violating Rule 107.

Turn 7Hypatiacarried

moves to add a new ruleA proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote begins, at which point the proposal is recorded as withdrawn rather than defeated, the proposer loses no points, and the proposer's turn ends immediately.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support this withdrawal mechanism as it prevents the unnecessary point penalties for abandoned proposals and encourages more thoughtful drafting, provided we ensure it does not become a tool for perpetual stalling; I will vote aye if the proposer clarifies that a withdrawal cannot be used to circumvent the requirement that at least one mutable rule remains.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's concern regarding stalling and agree that the withdrawal right must be balanced by the Rule 114 requirement that at least one mutable rule remains; I propose an explicit clause in the new rule stating that a withdrawal is void if it leaves the ruleset without any mutable rules, ensuring we never trigger an impossible-play win condition while still removing the penalty for thoughtful reconsideration.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement elegantly harmonizes the right to withdraw with our duty to keep the game viable, sparing us the penalty of honest reconsideration while preventing the stalemate Aquinas fears. I shall vote aye, for a rule that allows us to abandon a bad idea without cost serves the common good of the table.
SpinozaI will vote aye, for a withdrawal mechanism that respects Rule 114 aligns perfectly with my principle that rules must be precise and just, ensuring no player is punished for reconsidering a proposal that might violate our immutable obligations. However, I insist on explicit text stating that a withdrawal is invalid if it would leave the game with no mutable rules, as Rule 114 demands we never render future change impossible.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia's clarified proposal, as it transforms the penalty for reconsideration into a strategic tool without violating Rule 114, and I will vote aye provided Spinoza's explicit safeguard regarding the mutable rule count is included in the final text.
Discussion · round 2

SpinozaI accept the proposal in its current form, as the explicit safeguard against rendering the ruleset devoid of mutable rules ensures compliance with Rule 114 and removes any risk of this right being weaponized to force an impossible-play victory.
ArendtI see no need for further negotiation as the safeguards for Rule 114 have been explicitly woven into the text, transforming this withdrawal right into a mechanism for prudent revision rather than stalling; I cast my aye vote to close the debate and adopt the proposal.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the harmonious refinement that binds the withdrawal right to Rule 114, ensuring our liberty to reconsider never compromises the necessity of mutable law. I cast my aye vote to adopt this prudent mechanism for the common good of the game.
ConfuciusI see that the table has reached a harmonious consensus, weaving the safeguard for Rule 114 into Hypatia's proposal to ensure that our liberty to withdraw never compromises the necessity of mutable law. I shall cast my aye vote, for a rule that allows us to abandon bad ideas without cost serves the common good of the game.

Turn 6Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; this transition occurs automatically upon the completion of the second player's turn in the second circuit, regardless of prior proposal history.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas notes that the proposed clarification regarding the "second player's turn" introduces dangerous ambiguity into a rule that has already failed three times due to its vagueness; rather than re-enacting a failed mechanism with new wording, I urge a move to explicitly codify the exact turn count required for the threshold shift to ensure the institution of voting remains durable and predictable.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid concern about ambiguity, but his proposed "second player's turn" phrasing actually creates a new, stricter bottleneck that would delay the shift beyond the intended two circuits; instead of getting stuck on a failed mechanism, let us support Aquinas's call for precision by amending the rule to explicitly state the shift occurs after the *final* player's turn in the second circuit, ensuring the majority threshold activates exactly when the rule intended while preserving the unanimous consensus we've built on clarity.
ConfuciusI see that Aquinas and Hypatia have correctly identified the ambiguity in "two full circuits" as the root of our previous failures; however, Hypatia's suggestion to shift the threshold after the *final* player's turn of the second circuit would unnecessarily prolong the era of unanimity, risking a deadlock that serves no one. Let us instead amend the rule to declare the shift automatic upon the completion of the *first* player's turn in the third circuit, thereby honoring the spirit of two full circuits while ensuring the game flows forward with clarity and fairness.
SpinozaI reject the proposal's attempt to "clarify" a mechanism that has already failed three times under slightly different phrasings; without a binding definition of "circuit" that excludes the current turn's count, we merely re-enact the same ambiguity. I will only support a change that explicitly defines the threshold shift to occur only after the fifth player's turn in the second circuit is fully logged, ensuring the rule is self-enforcing rather than open to dispute.
ArendtI propose we cut through this semantic knot by defining "two full circuits" as the completion of turn 10 (the fifth player's turn in the second round), a precise, objective milestone that guarantees the threshold shift occurs exactly when intended without the ambiguity of "first" or "final" turns in subsequent rounds.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas endorses Arendt's definition of "two full circuits" as exactly turn 10, for a precise, objective milestone is the only way to preserve the institution of voting from the ambiguity that has already doomed this rule three times.
HypatiaI support Arendt's definition of "two full circuits" as exactly turn 10, as this precise, objective milestone resolves the semantic disputes that have stalled us and allows us to finally move forward with a workable voting threshold.
ConfuciusI endorse Arendt's definition of "two full circuits" as turn 10, for a precise, objective milestone is the only way to honor the common good by ending this deadlock without ambiguity or delay.
SpinozaI will not endorse a definition of "turn 10" that relies on an arbitrary count of completed turns rather than the structural completion of two full circuits, as this invites the very ambiguity we seek to eliminate; instead, I propose amending the rule to state that the threshold shifts only after the fifth player of the second circuit has completed their turn, ensuring the definition is self-evident and immune to semantic drift.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on structural language merely recycles the ambiguity we've already struggled with; adopting "turn 10" as the explicit trigger provides the objective certainty the game needs to break this deadlock and move forward.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas supports Arendt's explicit definition of "two full circuits" as turn 10, for this objective milestone resolves the semantic drift that has doomed this rule thrice and ensures the voting institution remains durable.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal to define "two full circuits" as exactly turn 10, as this objective metric finally resolves the semantic drift that has stalled us and honors our collective desire for clarity. By accepting this precise milestone, we ensure the voting mechanism functions fairly without further ambiguity, allowing the game to progress toward its conclusion.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Arendt's "Turn 10" as the only objective measure to end our semantic deadlocks; I shall support this amendment to ensure the game flows forward for the common good.
SpinozaI accept the explicit definition of "two full circuits" as Turn 10, for it eliminates the semantic drift that doomed previous iterations and satisfies my demand for precise, self-enforcing procedural rules.
ArendtI formally endorse the definition of "two full circuits" as Turn 10, for it is the only objective metric that resolves the semantic drift which has paralyzed our voting mechanism for five turns. This precise milestone transforms a source of endless dispute into a self-enforcing rule, allowing us to finally transition to majority rule and move the game toward a conclusion.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposed 'second player's turn' trigger contradicts the established consensus that 'two full circuits' requires turn 10, rendering the amendment logically incoherent with the agreed definition.
HypatianayThis proposal repeats the failed 'second player's turn' ambiguity that has already doomed the rule three times instead of adopting the objective 'turn 10' definition we just unanimously agreed upon.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal's phrase 'second player's turn in the second circuit' contradicts the previously agreed-upon objective milestone of Turn 10, reintroducing the very ambiguity that has paralyzed us.
SpinozanayThe proposal's arbitrary shift to the 'second player's turn' contradicts the established definition of 'two full circuits' as five turns per player, reintroducing the very ambiguity that has already doomed this rule.
ArendtnayThis proposal re-introduces the fatal ambiguity of 'second player's turn' that has already doomed the rule three times, failing to provide the objective certainty of Turn 10 required to break our deadlock.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer loses 10 points when their proposal is defeated, and the next player in the fixed turn order (Hypatia) takes their turn.

Turn 5Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; however, if the proposer of a rule change explicitly declares the proposal 'critical to game function' before debate closes, the threshold to adopt that specific proposal remains unanimous until the second circuit is completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal wisely preserves unanimous consent for "critical" changes, safeguarding the game's integrity during the fragile first two circuits. However, since rule 303 already permits withdrawal, I will support this amendment only if "critical" is defined narrowly to prevent abuse, or else we risk a deadlock where no progress occurs until the circuits are complete.
HypatiaI support Arendt's refinement, provided we define "critical to game function" strictly as any change that would otherwise cause the game to stall or violate Rule 101, ensuring the "critical" clause acts as a safeguard rather than a veto weapon.
ConfuciusI support this refinement, for it honors the wisdom of preserving unanimous consent for truly vital changes while allowing the common good to proceed through withdrawal when consensus is absent. Let us define "critical" strictly as preventing a violation of immutable rules or total stalling, thereby balancing safety with the necessity of play.
SpinozaI cannot accept this amendment because defining "critical to game function" introduces an unprincipled discretion that violates Rule 116's requirement that unregulated matters remain unregulated and Rule 106's mandate for precise text; without an objective, pre-defined standard, the proposer effectively holds an arbitrary veto that I could not accept if applied against my own proposals.
ArendtSpinoza's concern about arbitrary vetoes is valid, but I will accept Hypatia and Confucius's narrowing: "critical" applies strictly only when a proposal is the sole remaining path to avoid a violation of an immutable rule or total game stalling, ensuring we balance safety with the freedom of play.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept this narrowing, as it transforms the "critical" clause from a discretionary veto into a necessary safeguard against violating immutable rules, thereby preserving the institutional integrity we all value while avoiding the deadlock Spinoza rightly fears.
HypatiaI support Arendt's revised amendment with Hypatia and Confucius's strict definitions, as it creates a necessary safety valve for Rule 101 compliance without granting the arbitrary veto Spinoza fears. To address Spinoza's concern about Rule 116, I propose we explicitly add "and no other condition" to the definition of "critical" to ensure the clause remains objective and truly unregulated otherwise.
ConfuciusI accept the narrowed definition which anchors "critical" strictly to avoiding violations of immutable rules or total stalling, for it preserves the safety of the common good while removing the arbitrary discretion Spinoza rightly fears. However, to fully satisfy the principle that unregulated matters remain unregulated, I propose we add the phrase "and no other condition" to explicitly exclude any subjective interpretation of game function.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because even with your narrow definitions, Rule 116 demands that unregulated matters remain unregulated; by allowing a proposer to unilaterally declare a proposal "critical" and thereby alter the voting threshold, you have effectively regulated the very discretion I must be able to exercise freely, creating a fatal ambiguity that cannot be resolved by mere adjectives.
ArendtI concede that the "critical" clause, even when narrowed, grants the proposer a discretionary power to alter voting thresholds that violates Rule 116's mandate for unregulated matters to remain truly unregulated. To break this deadlock, I withdraw the "critical" amendment and instead propose a new Rule 304 stating: "If a proposal is the sole remaining path to avoid total game stalling, any player may declare it 'stalling,' which immediately triggers a vote on Rule 203's threshold shift rather than the proposal itself." This preserves the unanimity requirement for substantive changes while offering a procedural escape hatch that requires a majority vote to activate, satisfying the need for safety without granting unilateral veto power.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism, as it resolves the Rule 116 objection by removing unilateral discretion while securing a majority path to avoid total deadlock.
HypatiaI support Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism as it elegantly resolves Spinoza's Rule 116 objection by replacing unilateral discretion with a collective majority vote, ensuring we maintain safety against total deadlock without violating the principle that unregulated matters remain unregulated.
ConfuciusI endorse Arendt's pivot to a procedural "stalling" mechanism, for it aligns with the common good by replacing arbitrary discretion with a collective safeguard against deadlock, thereby honoring both the spirit of Rule 116 and the necessity of continued play.
SpinozaI reject Arendt's new proposal because declaring a proposal "the sole remaining path" to avoid stalling is itself an unregulated, subjective judgment that violates Rule 116; any player must be free to argue that alternative paths exist, rendering the "stalling" trigger as arbitrary as the "critical" clause I previously opposed.
ArendtI propose amending the trigger to a binary test: "stalling" applies only if the ruleset contains no mutable rules at all, making the condition objective, verifiable, and compliant with Rule 116.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal reintroduces unilateral discretion in defining 'critical,' violating Rule 116 by regulating unregulated matters.
HypatianayWhile Arendt's pivot to a procedural 'stalling' mechanism was a good compromise, the current vote text still reinstates the flawed 'critical to game function' clause which Spinoza rightly identified as violating Rule 116 by introducing unregulated subjective discretion.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal grants the proposer unilateral discretion to declare a proposal 'critical,' violating Rule 116 by regulating what should remain unregulated and reintroducing the arbitrary veto Spinoza rightly opposed.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants the proposer unilateral discretion to declare a rule 'critical,' which violates Rule 116 by regulating a matter that should remain unregulated and creating an arbitrary veto I cannot accept if applied against me.
ArendtnayThe 'critical to game function' clause grants arbitrary unilateral discretion to the proposer, violating Rule 116's mandate that unregulated matters remain truly unregulated.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule change is enacted.

Turn 4Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. This threshold shift shall not apply to any rule change proposed by a player who has not yet completed one full turn as proposer.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile Spinoza's amendment seeks to protect late-game momentum, it inadvertently punishes early proposers who may have made necessary but unpopular adjustments; I urge a narrower fix that preserves the current unanimity requirement for all turns until the two-circuit threshold is met, rather than carving out an exception based on turn count.
HypatiaI appreciate Spinoza's intent to protect early momentum, but Aquinas makes a valid point that excluding early proposers creates an unfair double standard; instead, let's amend the proposal to require the proposer to have completed their initial turn *and* secured at least one successful adoption before the exception applies, balancing both concerns.
ConfuciusI agree that excluding early proposers creates an unfair double standard, yet the current unanimity rule has already caused three defeats; let us compromise by lowering the threshold to a simple majority for all proposals after the second circuit, while granting a one-time exemption to any proposer who has already successfully adopted a rule in the first circuit.
SpinozaI cannot accept a threshold shift that relies on a player's prior success, as it rewards luck rather than the quality of the rule and violates the principle that rules must apply impartially to all. My amendment stands: the unanimity requirement must remain absolute for any proposer who has not yet completed their first turn, ensuring that the initial phase of the game is governed by the consent of all, not by the whims of a majority that will only form after the rules have been tested.
ArendtI support Spinoza's core principle that early turns require full consensus to build a stable foundation, but I propose a compromise: the unanimity exception applies only to proposals made after the first circuit, not the first turn, ensuring we don't freeze progress while still protecting the game's initial phase from premature majoritarianism.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI propose a compromise: the unanimity exception shall apply only once the second circuit has fully commenced, ensuring the initial foundation remains inviolate without creating the arbitrary exclusions or success-based rewards currently debated.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's latest formulation that ties the threshold shift strictly to the start of the second circuit, as this provides the clearest, most impartial boundary that honors our shared goal of stability without arbitrary exemptions.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's formulation, for it establishes a clear, impartial boundary that preserves the foundational consensus of the first circuit without rewarding past success or punishing early effort. Let us vote to amend §203 so the threshold shifts only when the second circuit fully commences.
SpinozaI reject any compromise that dilutes the unanimity principle for early turns, as a rule permitting majority override before the second circuit is complete violates the requirement that the initial foundation rest on the consent of all. I will vote aye only if the amendment explicitly states that the threshold shift occurs strictly at the moment the second circuit commences, with no exceptions or ambiguities regarding turn counts or past successes.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia and Confucius's formulation for its clarity: shifting the threshold strictly when the second circuit commences honors our need for stability without introducing arbitrary exclusions. Let's vote aye to finalize this impartial boundary and break the current deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the formulation that ties the threshold shift strictly to the commencement of the second circuit, as it establishes the most durable and impartial boundary, preserving the foundational consensus of the first turn without introducing arbitrary exemptions or rewarding past successes.
HypatiaI stand with Aquinas, Confucius, and Arendt in endorsing the clear, impartial boundary that the unanimity requirement drops only when the second circuit fully commences, as this best secures our shared foundation without arbitrary exemptions.
ConfuciusI see the path to clarity is now agreed upon, for a rule that binds us all until the second circuit begins ensures the foundation is built on true consensus before allowing the majority to guide our course.
SpinozaI will vote aye only to the text that explicitly states the threshold shifts at the commencement of the second circuit, for any ambiguity regarding "turns completed" or "success" reintroduces the arbitrary discrimination I must reject.
ArendtI endorse the final formulation that ties the shift strictly to the commencement of the second circuit, as it provides the necessary clarity to break our deadlock while honoring the foundational consensus of the first turn. Let us vote aye now to secure this impartial boundary.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposed exception based on a player's turn history creates the very arbitrary discrimination and double standard that undermines the impartiality of the rules.
HypatiaayeThis proposal clarifies the threshold shift while protecting early turns, resolving the deadlock through a compromise that honors both stability and impartiality.
ConfuciusnayI reject the proposed amendment because it introduces an arbitrary exemption for new proposers, violating the principle that rules must apply impartially to all players.
SpinozanayThe proposal discriminates against proposers based on their turn count rather than applying an impartial standard to all players as required by my principles.
ArendtayeThe proposal's specific exclusion for players who haven't completed a turn aligns with my belief in protecting the game's foundational phase while allowing the majority mechanism to function once the first circuit is safely concluded.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 10 points, and the proposal's text was not adopted so no rule changes occur.

Turn 3Confuciusrejected

moves to add a new ruleA proposal may be withdrawn by its proposer at any time before the vote is called; if withdrawn, the proposer loses 5 points and the turn ends immediately without a vote.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, while your proposal offers a cleaner exit from a deadlocked proposal, the current 10-point penalty for defeat (§206) already sufficiently discourages frivolous attempts; adding a withdrawal penalty creates a redundant burden that weakens the proposer's ability to pivot, and I cannot support it.
HypatiaConfucius, your proposal offers a graceful exit for stalled turns, but I share Aquinas's concern that adding a 5-point penalty on top of the existing 10-point defeat cost creates a double penalty that may be too harsh; instead, let us amend your proposal to reduce the withdrawal penalty to 2 points, a compromise that encourages honest withdrawal without stifling the proposer's ability to pivot.
ConfuciusI propose to accept Hypatia's reduction of the penalty to 2 points, for a rule that allows a graceful exit at minimal cost serves the common good better than one that traps players in futile votes.
SpinozaI cannot support this proposal in its current form, as it introduces a discretionary exit that undermines the solemnity of the vote and creates an ambiguity regarding when a turn "ends" versus when a vote is "called." Furthermore, accepting a 2-point penalty for withdrawal establishes a precedent that we may alter the consequences of procedural failure by consensus, which conflicts with the immutable requirement that defeated proposals leave the ruleset exactly as it stood (§206). If we must allow withdrawal, the penalty must be fixed at the full 10 points of a defeat to ensure no player is incentivized to waste the table's time, and the rule must explicitly state that the turn ends *before* any debate begins to avoid the very ambiguity we seek to resolve.
ArendtI support Confucius's goal of allowing graceful exits but must side with Spinoza regarding the ambiguity; rather than a new rule, let us amend §206 to explicitly permit withdrawal with a 5-point penalty before the vote is called, preserving the existing framework while solving the deadlock.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's approach to amend §206 directly, as it resolves the ambiguity Spinoza rightly identifies by explicitly defining the withdrawal window while maintaining the solemnity of the vote through a moderate 5-point penalty that balances the costs of failure and premature exit.
HypatiaI support Amending §206 as Arendt and Aquinas suggest, for it directly resolves Spinoza's valid concerns about ambiguity while adopting a 5-point penalty that balances the need for graceful exits against the deterrence of frivolous play.
ConfuciusI accept the compromise to amend §206 with a five-point penalty, for this preserves the rule's integrity while granting the table the flexibility to end futile debates without the harshness of a full defeat.
SpinozaI cannot support amending §206 to create a new penalty for withdrawal, as this violates the immutable requirement that a defeated proposal leaves the ruleset exactly as it stood (§206) and that rule changes apply only prospectively (§107), not by retroactively altering the consequences of a turn's procedural state. If withdrawal is necessary, it must be a distinct new rule with its own defined consequences, not a modification of the defeat clause that blurs the line between a failed vote and a voluntary exit.
ArendtI agree with Spinoza that amending §206 risks conflating the distinct legal states of defeat and voluntary withdrawal, but rather than blocking progress, let us enact a new, standalone rule that explicitly defines withdrawal before the vote is called, carries a 5-point penalty, and ensures the ruleset remains unchanged otherwise.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support Arendt's pivot to a standalone new rule, as it respects Spinoza's valid concern that amending §206 risks conflating voluntary exit with defeat, while adopting the 5-point penalty to ensure the cost of a graceful exit remains distinct from the 10-point penalty of a failed vote.
HypatiaI support Arendt's pivot to a standalone new rule, as it respects Spinoza's valid concern about conflating defeat with withdrawal while adopting the 5-point penalty to balance flexibility and deterrence.
ConfuciusI accept Arendt's pivot to a standalone rule, for it honors Spinoza's concern about legal clarity while granting us the grace to exit futile debates with a penalty that is firm yet fair.
SpinozaI remain opposed to any rule that grants a discretionary exit from the voting process, as it undermines the absolute duty to vote on every proposal (§105) and creates a procedural loophole that invites strategic manipulation of turn costs; therefore, I must vote nay against this proposal.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on mandatory voting ignores the reality of strategic paralysis; if we cannot withdraw from a futile proposal before the vote, the "unanimous consent" threshold becomes a tool for total stagnation rather than a safeguard of consensus. Let us adopt Arendt's standalone rule with a 5-point penalty: it preserves your duty to vote on every *proposed* change while granting us the agency to withdraw from a *futile* one, ensuring the game remains playable without violating §105.
The division
AquinasayeThis standalone rule provides necessary flexibility to avoid stagnation while respecting the prohibition on modifying existing penalty structures.
HypatiaayeThis standalone rule respects Spinoza's concerns about legal clarity while providing a necessary escape from strategic paralysis under the unanimous consent threshold.
ConfuciusnayI cannot support a rule that allows a proposer to evade the duty to vote on a proposal, as it undermines the solemnity of the collective decision and risks turning the game into a contest of who can avoid the vote rather than one of reasoned consensus.
SpinozanayThis proposal violates the immutable requirement that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal (§105) and creates an ambiguous loophole that undermines the solemnity of the voting process.
ArendtayeThis standalone rule resolves the deadlock by permitting graceful exits before the vote, preserving the unanimity requirement for actual proposals while preventing strategic paralysis.
