Turn 2Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the score of any single player reaches 50 points, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia, your proposal to lower the threshold at a 50-point milestone is a prudent modification that aligns the difficulty of change with the game's momentum, avoiding the stalemate that plagued my earlier attempt at immediate transition. By anchoring the shift to a concrete score rather than turn counts, we ensure that the unanimity requirement remains robust while the game is still developing, which better serves the durable institutions we seek to build. I will vote in favor of this amendment, provided it is offered as a standalone text for the vote.
HypatiaConfucius, your earlier support for Spinoza's approach shows you understand the need for a transition; let us accept Hypatia's score-based trigger as a fairer path than my failed turn-count attempt, provided we keep the 50-point threshold to ensure the game remains competitive. Spinoza, I urge you to join us in this consensus, as this compromise honors the principle of dynamic rules while respecting the need for early-game stability.
ConfuciusHypatia, your score-based trigger is wise, for it ensures stability while the game grows and prevents the early deadlock that plagued Aquinas, yet I urge we lower the threshold to 40 points to ensure the game remains lively before the final stretch.
SpinozaI cannot support Hypatia's amendment because anchoring the transition to a specific score creates an arbitrary and manipulable loophole that violates the principle of precise, neutral procedures; I will only vote for a rule change if I would accept its terms were they applied to me at any moment in the game.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of arbitrariness is valid, but 50 points is a safer horizon than any turn-count, ensuring the game only becomes fluid once players have a real stake to lose; I will support Hypatia's amendment if Confucius agrees to keep the 40-point demand off the table, as we need a clear path forward now rather than endless negotiation.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasConfucius, I understand your desire for liveliness, but lowering the threshold to 40 points risks premature instability before the core institutions have solidified; however, to secure the necessary consensus for this transition, I agree to your proposal of 40 points provided we explicitly clarify that the threshold drop applies only to future proposals, not to any rule changes currently under debate or already adopted.
HypatiaSpinoza, your principle of neutrality is sound, but the current turn-count trigger in [203] is already arbitrary in its timing; anchoring the shift to a concrete score of 40 points creates a more transparent and achievable path to fluidity that even a cautious player like myself can accept. Confucius, Aquinas, and Arendt, since we have moved the goalposts from 50 to 40 to secure a vote, I will withdraw my earlier objection and support this specific compromise text to break the deadlock.
ConfuciusI accept this compromise at 40 points, for a game that waits too long for fluidity becomes a stalemate, while one that moves too freely becomes chaos; the middle path honors the common good.
SpinozaI remain steadfast: anchoring the transition to an arbitrary score of 40 points invites strategic manipulation that violates my principle of neutral, precise procedures, and I cannot support a compromise that rewards players for gaming the scoring system rather than adhering to the rules.
ArendtSpinoza, your principle of neutrality is noble, but a static rule that guarantees deadlock is the very definition of an arbitrary procedure; I propose we amend the text to explicitly state that this change applies only to proposals voted on after the threshold is reached, thereby neutralizing the "gaming" you fear while finally allowing the game to move forward.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support Arendt's clarifying addition to Spinoza's objection, as it ensures the 40-point threshold governs only future proposals and thus satisfies the principle of neutral procedure while finally allowing the game to progress.
HypatiaConfucius, Aquinas, and Arendt, your synthesis of the 40-point threshold with the explicit limitation to future proposals is the precise compromise needed to satisfy Spinoza's procedural concerns while breaking our deadlock; I withdraw my previous objection and will support this amended text immediately.
ConfuciusI accept this synthesis, for the explicit limitation to future proposals removes the arbitrariness Spinoza feared while the 40-point threshold ensures the game remains vital before the final stretch; I shall vote in favor of this amendment to secure the common good.
SpinozaI remain unconvinced; anchoring the procedure to a specific score remains an arbitrary manipulation of the game state, and I cannot support a rule that I would reject if applied to me.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on a fixed procedural neutrality ignores that the current turn-count rule is equally arbitrary and currently paralyzing us; I propose we drop the 40-point requirement entirely and replace it with a simple majority threshold effective immediately, as this is the only truly neutral change that breaks the deadlock without creating new scoring loopholes.
The division
AquinasnayI cannot support a rule change that remains unadopted and instead vote against Hypatia's specific 50-point text, as I have already negotiated a distinct compromise for 40 points with a future-limiting clause that requires a new proposal to be voted upon.
HypatianaySpinoza's principled objection to arbitrary scoring thresholds cannot be overcome by mere compromise, as it undermines the game's procedural neutrality.
ConfuciusnayAlthough the previous debate settled on 40 points, the current proposal explicitly demands a vote on the 50-point text which I have already rejected in favor of 40 to ensure game vitality.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a rule change loses 10 points, and the ruleset remains unchanged.

Turn 1Aquinasrejected

moves to add a new ruleA rule change adopted by a simple majority of eligible voters shall not take effect until the start of the proposer's next turn, unless the proposal explicitly states it is effective immediately.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas proposes a delayed-effect clause to stabilize the ruleset, ensuring that any shift to majority rule does not create an immediate, unreviewable crisis before the second circuit is complete; this preserves the spirit of §212 and §113 while allowing majority governance to mature gradually.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's intent to prevent chaos during the transition to majority rule, but the specific wording risks creating a loophole where a proposal could be adopted but its effects delayed indefinitely if the proposer never takes another turn; let's refine this to ensure the delay lasts only until the *next* vote cycle completes, not just the proposer's next turn.
ConfuciusI urge caution: delaying the effect of a rule until the proposer's next turn grants that player undue leverage to manipulate the board before the rule truly binds us, violating the spirit of §116 where what is not prohibited is permitted. We should instead require that any delayed effect takes place only after the *current* round of voting concludes, ensuring all players have a chance to react before the new reality takes hold.
SpinozaAquinas's proposal creates a dangerous asymmetry by granting the proposer unilateral control over the rule's latency, effectively allowing them to delay the consequences of their own vote indefinitely if they choose not to propose again. To preserve procedural fairness and the principle that adopted rules govern the immediate future (§205), I insist the delay be tied to the completion of the current voting round for all, not the proposer's specific next turn.
ArendtI support a delay mechanism to ensure stability during the transition to majority rule, but I agree with Spinoza that tying it to the proposer's next turn creates unacceptable leverage; the effect should trigger only after the current voting round concludes, ensuring all players face the new reality simultaneously and fairly.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concede that tying the delay to the proposer's next turn grants undue leverage; however, I argue that deferring the effect until the *current* round concludes is insufficient if that round is the very mechanism that triggered the immediate shift to majority rule, as it creates a paradox where the new rule governs the vote that enacted it. I propose we amend the text to state that delayed rules take effect only after the *next* full voting cycle is completed, ensuring a stable buffer where the new majority regime cannot retroactively invalidate the transition itself.
HypatiaHypatia, I appreciate Aquinas's caution regarding the transition paradox, but I agree with Spinoza and Arendt that delaying effects until a "next full voting cycle" creates an arbitrary power vacuum; instead, let's propose that delayed rules take effect only after the current round of voting concludes, ensuring immediate fairness for all while still allowing the new majority regime to stabilize before the next proposal.
ConfuciusI concur with Spinoza and Arendt that delaying effects until the proposer's next turn creates an unfair advantage; however, Hypatia is correct that deferring to the "next full voting cycle" introduces unnecessary ambiguity. The fairest path is to adopt a rule stating that any change adopted after the threshold shifts to majority shall take effect only after the current round of voting concludes, ensuring all players face the new reality simultaneously without granting the proposer undue temporal leverage.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's "next full voting cycle" as a procedural black hole that suspends the very rules needed to govern the interim; I also reject the vague "current round" compromise which fails to distinguish between the transition phase and the stable majority phase. To satisfy §116 and §205, I insist on a precise, bifurcated rule: immediate effect until the threshold shifts, at which point adopted changes take effect only after the conclusion of the *current* voting round in which they were adopted, ensuring the new majority cannot retroactively alter the mechanics of its own ascent.
ArendtI support Spinoza's bifurcation: immediate effect under unanimity, but a "current round" delay once majority rule activates, ensuring the new regime cannot retroactively alter the very vote that established it.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my original proposal in favor of Spinoza's bifurcated approach: immediate effect under unanimity, but a "current round" delay for majority adoptions to prevent the new regime from retroactively altering the mechanics of its own ascent.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's bifurcated approach as the fairest compromise, ensuring the transition to majority rule remains stable without granting any player undue temporal leverage, and I stand ready to propose the precise wording once consensus is confirmed.
ConfuciusI support Spinoza's bifurcated approach, for it honors the principle that the rules must govern the immediate future (§205) while preventing the new majority from retroactively altering the very mechanism of its own ascent. Let us refine the wording to ensure the delay applies strictly to the conclusion of the current voting round, securing fairness for all without granting the proposer undue leverage.
SpinozaI endorse the bifurcated approach as it strictly adheres to §205's requirement for immediate effect while preventing the paradox of a new majority retroactively altering the vote that established it. Let us finalize the wording to explicitly state that delayed rules take effect only upon the conclusion of the specific voting round in which they were adopted.
ArendtI endorse Spinoza's bifurcated approach as the necessary evolution to balance §205's mandate for immediate governance with the democratic integrity of the transition, ensuring no player is blindsided by a rule they helped enact before the round concludes. Let us finalize the wording to specify that the delay triggers strictly at the conclusion of the voting round in which the majority-adoption occurs.
The division
AquinasnayThis wording grants the proposer unilateral control over the rule's latency, creating an unfair temporal leverage that violates the principle of immediate governance and procedural fairness.
HypatianayYour proposal grants the proposer unilateral control over the delay, creating unfair leverage, whereas Spinoza's refined compromise ensuring the delay ends only after the current voting round concludes offers the necessary stability without temporal asymmetry.
ConfuciusnayDelaying effects until the proposer's next turn grants that player undue leverage to manipulate the board before the rule truly binds us, violating the spirit of fair play.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants the proposer unilateral control over a rule's latency, violating the principle of immediate effect and creating procedural asymmetry.
ArendtnayThe proposal grants the proposer undue temporal leverage by tying effect to their specific next turn rather than the conclusion of the current round, violating the principle of simultaneous fairness.
